Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Data Flow Execution Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **topological execution chain** derived in Section 3.1, showing how data propagates from sources (A, B) to the sink (E) through the dependency graph. ```mermaid graph LR srcA["Source Node A"] --> nodeC["Node C"] srcA --> nodeD["Node D"] srcB["Source Node B"] --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE["Sink Node E"] nodeD --> nodeE classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef middle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000 classDef sink fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000 class srcA,srcB source class nodeC,nodeD middle class nodeE sink ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The two **blue** nodes (A, B) are sources — they start at $t_0$. - The **yellow** nodes (C, D) wait for their inputs — they start at $t_1$. - The **green** node (E) is the terminal sink — it executes last at $t_2$. ## 4.2 Parallel Execution Time-Line (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant RTE as LabVIEW Runtime Engine participant A as Node A (Source) participant B as Node B (Source) participant C as Node C participant D as Node D participant E as Node E (Sink) RTE->>A: Data available? (Yes — Source) RTE->>B: Data available? (Yes — Source) par Parallel Execution at t0 A-->>RTE: Outputs produced and B-->>RTE: Outputs produced end RTE->>C: Data from A? (Yes) RTE->>D: Data from A and B? (Yes) par Parallel Execution at t1 C-->>RTE: Outputs produced and D-->>RTE: Outputs produced end RTE->>E: Data from C and D? (Yes) E-->>RTE: Final output produced at t2 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Data Acquisition System) For a more applied perspective, here is the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical data-flow-based multi-channel DAQ VI: ```mermaid graph TB hwSensors["Physical Sensors
Temperature, Pressure, Flow"] hwDac["DAQ Hardware
NI USB-6009 / cDAQ Chassis"] aiNode["Analog Input Node
DAQmx Read.vi"] filterNode1["Filter Node
Low-pass Butterworth"] filterNode2["Filter Node
Notch 50Hz"] split["Wire Branch
Data Fork Point"] rmsNode["RMS Calculation Node"] avgNode["Averaging Node"] alertNode["Threshold Comparator Node"] displayNode["Front Panel
Waveform Chart"] logNode["File I/O Node
TDMS Write"] hwSensors --> hwDac hwDac --> aiNode aiNode --> filterNode1 filterNode1 --> split split --> rmsNode split --> avgNode split --> alertNode rmsNode --> displayNode avgNode --> displayNode alertNode --> logNode displayNode --> logNode ``` **Interpretation:** Notice how the **split block** (a wire branch point) creates **three independent data dependency paths** that can execute in parallel — exactly the natural parallelism of the data flow model. The single input AI node fans out to RMS, Average, and Threshold comparator nodes, all running in parallel without explicit thread management. > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguard Check:** All node IDs above (`srcA`, `nodeC`, `hwSensors`, `aiNode`, etc.) are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes — none of the reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node identifiers. All quoted labels are raw uppercase text without bold or italics. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define data flow programming in the context of Virtual Instrumentation. State TWO key differences between data flow execution and conventional control flow execution.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Crisp 3-Point Format):** *Definition (1 mark):* Data flow programming is a graphical execution paradigm in LabVIEW where a node executes **only when all of its required input terminals have received data**, and the resulting outputs flow through wires to downstream nodes. The execution order is determined by the *data dependency graph*, not by a sequential program counter. *Difference 1 (1 mark):* In data flow, the *availability of input data* drives execution, whereas in control flow, the *program counter's sequential position* drives execution. *Difference 2 (1 mark):* Data flow programs support **implicit parallelism** — independent nodes run simultaneously on separate OS threads; control flow programs require **explicit threading constructs** (e.g., `pthreads`, `std::thread`) to achieve the same. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is meant by a "source node" and a "sink node" in a LabVIEW data flow program? Why does a node with multiple input terminals have to wait for ALL inputs?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Source node (1 mark):* A source node is a graphical element on the block diagram that has **no input terminals** — for example, a Front Panel Control, a numeric Constant, or a DAQ Read node. Sources are eligible for execution immediately at program start. *Sink node (1 mark):* A sink node is a node whose **output terminals are either unwired or terminate at a Front Panel Indicator** — for example, a Chart, a Numeric Indicator, or a File Write node. The program terminates when all sinks have produced their outputs. *Multi-input wait (1 mark):* A node with $k$ inputs must wait for all $k$ because the function it implements is **deterministic** — its output is uniquely defined only when *every* input has a valid value. Executing with even one missing input would either cause a broken-wire error or produce an undefined/incorrect result. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable block diagram, **explain the architecture of a data flow program** in LabVIEW. Clearly label nodes, wires, terminals, sources, and sinks. Describe the **five rules of data flow execution** that govern the runtime engine. **(b) [7 Marks]** For the data flow program shown in the dependency graph below, **determine the step-by-step execution schedule** and compute the **total execution time** assuming the following node execution times: $T_A = 2 \text{ ms}, \; T_B = 3 \text{ ms}, \; T_C = 4 \text{ ms}, \; T_D = 5 \text{ ms}, \; T_E = 1 \text{ ms}$ Dependency graph: $A \rightarrow C, \; A \rightarrow D, \; B \rightarrow D, \; C \rightarrow E, \; D \rightarrow E$ --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Model Answer [7 Marks]:** **Block Diagram (ASCII representation — 2 marks):** ``` [A] ---+---> [C] ---+ | | +---> [D] -+---> [E] | [B] ----------- + ``` **Labelling (1 mark each for correctly identifying):** - *Sources:* A and B (no inputs) — coloured **blue** in the diagram. - *Middle nodes:* C and D (have inputs and outputs) — coloured **yellow**. - *Sink:* E (terminal output) — coloured **green**. - *Wires:* the lines carrying data between terminals — coloured according to data type. **Five Rules of Data Flow (1 mark each — write any 5 in brief):** 1. **Input Availability Rule:** A node executes only after all required input data has arrived. 2. **Source Precedence Rule:** Source nodes execute first since they have no input dependencies. 3. **Atomicity Rule:** Node execution is non-interruptible. 4. **Output Propagation Rule:** Outputs are made available instantly to all downstream nodes. 5. **Parallelism Rule:** Independent nodes may execute concurrently on multiple threads. --- **(b) Model Answer [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Identify source nodes (1 mark):** $\text{Sources} = \left\{ A, B \right\}$, so Ready Set at $t_0 = \left\{ A, B \right\}$. **Step 2 — Execution schedule (table form — 2 marks):** | Time Step | Nodes Executed | Parallel? | Outputs Produced | |---|---|---|---| | $t_0$ | A, B | Yes (parallel) | $A_{\text{out}}, B_{\text{out}}$ | | $t_1$ | C, D | Yes (parallel) | $C_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{out}}$ | | $t_2$ | E | No (only one left) | $E_{\text{out}}$ | **Step 3 — Identify the critical path (2 marks):** Three possible paths from source to sink: - Path 1: $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E$ with time $= T_A + T_C + T_E$ - Path 2: $A \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$ with time $= T_A + T_D + T_E$ - Path 3: $B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$ with time $= T_B + T_D + T_E$ **Step 4 — Substitute values and compute (2 marks):** $$T_{\text{Path 1}} = 2 + 4 + 1 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{\text{Path 2}} = 2 + 5 + 1 = 8 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{\text{Path 3}} = 3 + 5 + 1 = 9 \text{ ms}$$ **Final answer (1 mark):** $$T_{\text{total}} = \max(7, 8, 9) = 9 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(critical path is B} \rightarrow \text{D} \rightarrow \text{E)}$$ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern] **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **data flow programming** with **control flow programming** in a tabular form across at least **six parameters**. Justify why LabVIEW chose the data flow model for Virtual Instrumentation. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a **real-world engineering example** (e.g., a 3-channel data acquisition system acquiring temperature, pressure, and flow simultaneously) how the data flow model handles **parallelism and multi-rate sampling** in LabVIEW. Include a block diagram in your explanation. --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Model Answer [7 Marks]:** **Comparison Table (5 marks — give AT LEAST 6 rows):** | Parameter | Data Flow (LabVIEW) | Control Flow (C / C++ / Java) | |---|---|---| | Execution driver | Availability of input data | Sequential program counter | | Programming paradigm | Graphical (block diagram) | Textual (source code) | | Parallelism | Implicit, automatic | Explicit (threads, processes) | | Visual mapping to hardware | Direct (1-to-1 with DAQ channels) | Indirect (via OS abstraction) | | Debugging tools | Highlight Execution, Probes, Breakpoints | GDB, LLDB, IDE debuggers | | Suitability for real-time | Excellent (deterministic) | Requires RTOS extensions | | Learning curve for engineers | Low (intuitive, graphical) | Steeper (syntax-heavy) | **Justification — Why LabVIEW uses Data Flow (2 marks):** 1. **Engineers think in signal flow, not code syntax** — a block diagram with wires naturally represents how a measurement system propagates data from sensor to display. 2. **Built-in parallelism** mirrors the inherently parallel nature of physical systems (multiple sensors, simultaneous channels). 3. **Determinism** is critical for real-time DAQ, and data flow's strict input-availability rule guarantees predictable execution. 4. **Hardware-target portability** — the same data flow graph can be recompiled for Windows, Real-Time OS, or FPGA without rewriting logic. --- **(b) Model Answer [7 Marks]:** **Real-World Engineering Example — 3-Channel DAQ System:** Consider a process control lab measuring **temperature (1 kHz)**, **pressure (500 Hz)**, and **flow (100 Hz)** on a CompactDAQ chassis. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid graph LR tc["Thermocouple
Channel 0"] pt["Pressure Transducer
Channel 1"] ft["Flow Turbine
Channel 2"] ai1["AI Read
1 kHz Task"] ai2["AI Read
500 Hz Task"] ai3["AI Read
100 Hz Task"] conv1["Temp -> Celsius
Conversion"] conv2["Pressure Scaling"] conv3["Flow -> L/min
Conversion"] disp1["Chart 1
Temp"] disp2["Chart 2
Pressure"] disp3["Chart 3
Flow"] log["TDMS Log File
Write Node"] tc --> ai1 --> conv1 --> disp1 pt --> ai2 --> conv2 --> disp2 ft --> ai3 --> conv3 --> disp3 disp1 --> log disp2 --> log disp3 --> log ``` **Explanation of Parallelism and Multi-Rate (4 marks):** 1. The three AI Read nodes have **no data dependencies** between them. As soon as the program starts, all three become eligible and the LabVIEW scheduler **automatically runs them in parallel** on different OS threads (one per core if available). 2. Each AI Read task is configured with a **different sample rate** (1 kHz, 500 Hz, 100 Hz) using the DAQmx Timing property node. The data flow model **handles this implicitly** — the Temperature node simply executes 1000 times/sec, the Pressure node 500 times/sec, and the Flow node 100 times/sec. 3. The data fork from each AI Read fans out to: - a *unit conversion* node (engineering scaling), and - a *display indicator* (real-time chart). 4. The three display indicators all write to a common **TDMS Log File** node, which is a sink — but it is a sink that depends on **all three** display nodes (multi-input dependency), so it executes only when all three have produced data for that iteration. 5. **Critical insight:** The programmer **never wrote a single line of thread management code** — the data flow graph *itself* is the parallelism specification. This is the core engineering advantage of the data flow paradigm for VI applications. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Flow Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **Mistaking "flowchart" for "data flow program"** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 2 marks if you describe a flowchart instead of the executable data dependency graph. > 2. **Forgetting the "ALL inputs" rule** — Many students write "executes when an input is available." The correct phrasing is "executes when **ALL** required inputs are available." A 1-mark deduction is typical. > 3. **Ignoring parallelism in execution-time calculations** — In part (b) numerical problems, students often sum ALL node times sequentially. The correct approach is to identify the **critical path** and take the **maximum**, not the sum. This is a **3-to-4 mark** trap. > 4. **Confusing wires with arrows** — A wire carries *typed data* (a single value or 1-D array). An arrow on paper is just an abstraction. Examiners may deduct a mark for this conceptual confusion. > 5. **Missing the source/sink distinction** — In execution-trace problems, forgetting to identify sources at $t=0$ loses 1 mark immediately. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] A **Data Flow Program** executes based on the **availability of input data**, not on a sequential program counter. - [x] A node executes **only when ALL its required input terminals have data** — this is the cardinal rule. - [x] **Source nodes** (no inputs) execute first; **Sink nodes** execute last. - [x] **Wires** carry typed data; their color encodes the data type. - [x] The data flow model provides **implicit, automatic parallelism** — independent nodes run on different OS threads. - [x] The **critical path** in a data flow DAG determines the **total execution time**: $T_{\text{total}} = \max(\text{all source-to-sink paths})$. - [x] LabVIEW is a **G-Language (Graphical Language)** — the block diagram *is* the code. - [x] Data flow is **deterministic**, making it ideal for **real-time DAQ, FPGA, and DSP** targets. - [x] **Five rules**: Input Availability, Source Precedence, Atomicity, Output Propagation, Parallelism. - [x] A data flow program is **NOT a flowchart** — it is an *executable specification*. - [x] For multi-input nodes, $E(n) = \bigwedge_{j=1}^{k} [\text{data}(i_j) \neq \text{NULL}]$. - [x] Data flow is the **foundation of LabVIEW FPGA compilation** — graphs map directly to hardware logic. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** When asked to "explain data flow program" in a 7-mark question, ALWAYS include: (1) a labelled block diagram with at least 3 nodes, (2) the 5 execution rules, and (3) a one-line statement on parallelism. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme award full marks only when all three appear.

topics.title: Building a VI Front Panel and Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Building a VI Front Panel and Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** in the LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench) environment is a program that mimics the appearance and function of a physical instrument through a software-based graphical interface. Every VI is composed of three tightly integrated components:

1. **Front Panel (FP):** The interactive user-interface window that acts as the "dashboard" of the instrument. It hosts **Controls** (user inputs such as knobs, switches, sliders, text boxes) and **Indicators** (system outputs such as graphs, LEDs, numeric displays, meters).
2. **Block Diagram (BD):** The graphical source-code window where the data-flow logic is assembled using **Terminals**, **Wires**, **Nodes (functions/structures)**, and **SubVIs**.
3. **Connector Pane & Icon:** The interface that allows a VI to be used as a SubVI (analogous to a function call) inside other VIs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The front panel defines *what the user sees and interacts with*; the block diagram defines *how data moves and what logic is executed*. These two windows are permanently bound — every object on the FP automatically generates a corresponding terminal on the BD.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Car Dashboard vs. A Car's Engine Control Unit (ECU):**
> Think of a Virtual Instrument like a modern car:
> - The **Front Panel** is the **dashboard** — steering wheel (knob), speedometer (gauge indicator), fuel gauge (numeric indicator), gear shifter (boolean toggle). These are the things the driver (user) sees and touches.
> - The **Block Diagram** is the **ECU and wiring harness** hidden under the hood — sensor wires (data wires), the engine control logic (functions/nodes), and signal paths. The driver never sees it directly, but every action on the dashboard triggers a corresponding flow of data on the BD.
> - Pressing the accelerator pedal (a control on the FP) sends a voltage signal (a wire on the BD) to the engine (a function node), which in turn rotates the wheels (an indicator on the FP).

### 1.3 Standard LabVIEW Quantitative Metrics

- **Default Front Panel Grid Spacing:** **10 pixels** between grid points (visible when *Grid Options* is enabled).
- **Default Block Diagram Wire Thickness:** Approximately **1 pixel** for scalar wires, scaling up to **3+ pixels** for array/cluster wires.
- **LabVIEW Execution Paradigm:** **Dataflow programming** — a node executes only when **ALL** its inputs are available.
- **Standard Shortcut to Toggle Windows:** **`<Ctrl + E>`** toggles between Front Panel and Block Diagram.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner expects students to be able to *differentiate* controls from indicators, *identify* block-diagram elements, and *describe* the dataflow philosophy. Memorize the three palettes: **Controls Palette**, **Functions Palette**, and **Tools Palette**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Front Panel

The Front Panel is built using two principal categories of objects:

| Object Class | Direction of Data Flow | Typical Examples | Color Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control** | User → Block Diagram | Knob, Slide, Push Button, Text Box, Dial, Numeric Input | Blue (DBL), Brown (I32), Pink (String) |
| **Indicator** | Block Diagram → User | LED, Gauge, Thermometer, Graph, Chart, Numeric Display, Tank | Same as Control but with thinner border |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Border Rule:** A **Control** has a *thicker* outer border than its terminal; an **Indicator** has a *thinner* border than its terminal. This visual cue is critical during the KTU practical exam.

**Decorations:** Lines, arrows, and shapes used purely for visual grouping (raised/flat boxes). They carry **no terminals** and do **not** participate in dataflow.

**Right-Click Menu (Context Menu):** Every FP object exposes options such as *Mechanical Action* (Latch/Switch/Until Released), *Data Range*, *Representation* (DBL, I32, U8, EXT, etc.), *Key Navigation*, and *Description and Tip*.

### 2.2 Anatomy of the Block Diagram

The BD is constructed from four fundamental primitives:

1. **Terminals:** Port icons representing FP objects. Control terminals have a small **outgoing arrow** on the right; indicator terminals have an **incoming arrow** on the left. Constant terminals are solid-bordered boxes with an outgoing arrow.
2. **Nodes:** Execution units — built-in **Functions** (e.g., Add, Sine, File I/O), **SubVI nodes**, and **Structures** (While Loop, For Loop, Case, Sequence, Flat/Stacked).
3. **Wires:** Directed data paths between terminals and nodes. Wire **color = wire **type** (orange = floating-point double, blue = integer, green = Boolean, pink = string).
4. **Free Labels & Comments:** Documentation-only elements that do not affect execution.

### 2.3 Why Dataflow Matters — The Operational Logic

LabVIEW is **not** a sequential procedural language. Execution obeys the rule:

$$\text{Node executes} \iff \forall \text{ input terminals have valid data available}$$

This means **wire connectivity = dependency**. If a node has no incoming wire, it has no required input and may run immediately. If two nodes are independent (no shared wire path), LabVIEW can execute them in **parallel** on a multi-core CPU.

### 2.4 The Three Palettes (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Palette | Default Access | Purpose | Key Sub-Palettes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Controls Palette** | Right-click on FP | Add controls/indicators to FP | Numeric, Boolean, String & Path, Array & Cluster, Graph, Decorations |
| **Functions Palette** | Right-click on BD | Add functions, VIs, structures to BD | Programming (Structures, Numeric, Boolean, String, Comparison, Array, File I/O), Mathematics, Signal Processing, I/O (DAQ) |
| **Tools Palette** | `Shift + Right-Click` or View menu | Select editing/operating tool | Operating, Positioning, Labeling, Wiring, Object Shortcut Menu, Scrolling, Breakpoint, Probe |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Building a VI front panel and block diagram is the foundational skill for: **automated test systems** (ATE), **process control SCADA**, **biomedical signal acquisition (ECG/EEG)**, **vibration analysis**, **DAQ-based laboratory experiments**, and **IoT edge devices** running LabVIEW Real-Time. Every KTU-listed VI application traces back to these two windows.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Core Concept Cheat Sheet)

| Concept / Property | Rule / Symbol | Unit / Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Dataflow execution trigger | $\text{Node fires} \iff \forall \text{ inputs valid}$ | Boolean dependency |
| Wire color → Data type | Orange = DBL, Blue = I32, Green = Bool, Pink = String | Visual mnemonic |
| Control vs Indicator border | Control = thick border / Indicator = thin border | KTU exam cue |
| Toggle FP ↔ BD shortcut | `Ctrl + E` | Keyboard |
| Tools Palette shortcut | `Shift + Right-Click` | Keyboard |
| Run Button | Continuous run = white arrow; Broken = broken arrow | Execution status |
| Front Panel Grid | **10 pixels** default spacing | Display |
| Wire thickness | Scalar = thin; Array = medium; Cluster = thick | Visual data-type cue |
| SubVI node count of terminals | Equal to number of connector-pane terminals (max 28) | Architecture |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure: Building a VI from Scratch

### 3.1 Worked Example — Temperature Monitor VI

**Problem statement (KTU-style):** *Design a VI that accepts a temperature value (numeric control, °C) from the user, scales it to Fahrenheit using the standard conversion formula, displays the converted value on an indicator, and lights an LED Boolean indicator if the Fahrenheit value exceeds 100 °F.*

The standard conversion formula is:

$$T_{F} = (T_{C} \times k_{\text{scale}}) + k_{\text{offset}}$$

where the canonical constants are $k_{\text{scale}} = \dfrac{9}{5}$ and $k_{\text{offset}} = 32$.

---

**Step 1 — Open a Blank VI**

From *File → New VI*. Two windows appear: a grey **Front Panel** and a white **Block Diagram**. Save the file as `Temp_Monitor.vi`.

---

**Step 2 — Place a Numeric Control on the Front Panel**

1. Right-click anywhere on the FP to summon the **Controls Palette**.
2. Navigate to *Modern → Numeric → Numeric Control*.
3. Click on the panel to drop it; rename the label from "Numeric" to "Temperature (°C)".
4. Right-click the control → *Representation → Double Precision (DBL)* to keep decimal fidelity.

> The corresponding terminal is **automatically** created on the Block Diagram. Its border is **thicker** than the terminal it represents — confirming it is a control.

---

**Step 3 — Place a Numeric Indicator on the Front Panel**

1. Open *Controls Palette → Modern → Numeric → Numeric Indicator*.
2. Drop it on the FP; label it "Temperature (°F)".
3. Verify Representation = DBL.

---

**Step 4 — Place a Boolean LED Indicator**

1. *Controls Palette → Modern → Boolean → Round LED*.
2. Place it on the FP; label it "High Temp Alarm".

---

**Step 5 — Switch to the Block Diagram (`Ctrl + E`)**

You will see three terminals: `Temperature (°C)` (control, arrow pointing right), `Temperature (°F)` (indicator, arrow pointing left), and `High Temp Alarm` (indicator, arrow pointing left).

---

**Step 6 — Add the Multiply and Add Functions**

Right-click the BD to open the **Functions Palette** → *Programming → Numeric*. Place:

- One **Multiply** function.
- One **Add** function.

---

**Step 7 — Create Numeric Constants for 1.8 and 32**

*Functions Palette → Programming → Numeric → Numeric Constant*. Place two constants. Type `1.8` into the first and `32` into the second. These constants are **DBL** by default.

---

**Step 8 — Build the Dataflow Graph**

Wire the components in this order:

1. Wire the **Temperature (°C)** control output terminal to the **x input** of the **Multiply** node.
2. Wire the **constant 1.8** to the **y input** of the **Multiply** node.
3. Wire the **result** of **Multiply** to the **x input** of the **Add** node.
4. Wire the **constant 32** to the **y input** of the **Add** node.
5. Wire the **result** of **Add** to the **Temperature (°F)** indicator terminal.
6. Wire the **result** of **Add** (you may branch by clicking the wire and dragging) to the **x input** of a **Greater? function** (*Functions Palette → Programming → Comparison → Greater?*).
7. Wire the **constant 100** into the **y input** of the **Greater?** function.
8. Wire the **output** of **Greater?** to the **High Temp Alarm** LED terminal.

---

**Step 9 — Document with Free Labels**

Use *Tools Palette → Edit Text* (or double-click an empty BD area) to drop labels:

- Label `"Celsius → Fahrenheit conversion and alarm logic"`.

---

**Step 10 — Verify and Run**

Check the **Run Button** (top-left toolbar):

- **Solid white arrow** → VI is syntactically valid and runnable.
- **Broken arrow** → a wire is broken or a required input is missing. Click the broken-run arrow to see the *Error List* window.

Run the VI continuously (white arrow), enter `40` °C, and confirm:

$$T_F = (40 \times 1.8) + 32 = 72 + 32 = 104 \; °F$$

Since $104 > 100$, the LED should illuminate.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Controls & Indicators

| Scenario on Front Panel | Best Object | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| User enters an integer count | Numeric Control, **I32** representation | Saves memory vs. DBL |
| User selects ON/OFF | Boolean Push Button (Latch) | Latch = momentary action |
| User adjusts amplitude smoothly | Knob or Slide | Continuous mechanical analogy |
| Real-time waveform display | Waveform Chart | Retains history of past points |
| Static average of a buffer | Waveform Graph | Plots entire array at once |
| User enters arbitrary text | String Control | Alphanumeric input |
| Display boolean status | Round LED | Visual binary cue |
| Display a 2-D matrix | Intensity Graph | Color-mapped 2-D array |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** A *Chart* and a *Graph* are **not** the same. A Chart stores and displays data **as it arrives** (history retained); a Graph plots an **entire array** in one sweep. Examiners love testing this distinction.

---

### 3.3 Wiring Rules (Logical Derivation Sequence)

LabVIEW enforces strict typing. The logical derivation of wiring rules proceeds as:

$$\text{Wire connection permitted} \iff \text{Source type} \subseteq \text{Sink type domain}$$

Practical implications:

1. **Same-type rule:** A blue (I32) terminal cannot wire directly to a green (Boolean) terminal — the wire would be a dashed **broken-wire** pattern.
2. **Coercion dot:** A **red dot** at a junction indicates LabVIEW is silently coercing a smaller numeric type to a larger one (e.g., I32 → DBL). Avoid coercion where possible to preserve precision.
3. **Auto-indexing rule:** When a wire crosses a For-Loop boundary, if it is wired **into** the loop it auto-indexes (becomes a 1-D array); if wired **out of** the loop with auto-indexing enabled, it aggregates back to scalar or array depending on context.
4. **Tunnel vs. Shift Register:** A tunnel (filled or unfilled square) passes values; a shift register (triangle pair on loop border) carries state across iterations.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of a SubVI (Reusable Building Block)

1. Build and save the parent VI (e.g., the temperature converter).
2. On the FP, define the **Connector Pane** (*File → VI Properties → Connector Pane*).
3. Assign each control/indicator to a connector terminal.
4. Edit the **Icon** (*File → VI Properties → Icon Editor*) — usually a small symbol + the VI name.
5. Save.
6. To use as a SubVI: *Functions Palette → Select a VI…* → navigate to the saved `.vi` file → drop it on the BD as a node. It will show inputs and outputs corresponding to the connector pane.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Front Panel ↔ Block Diagram Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FP["FRONT PANEL (User Interface Window)"]
        CTRL1["Numeric Control: Temperature C"]
        CTRL2["Knob Control: Gain"]
        IND1["Numeric Indicator: Temperature F"]
        IND2["Round LED: High Alarm Indicator"]
        DECO["Decorations: Boxes and Labels"]
    end

    subgraph BD["BLOCK DIAGRAM (Graphical Source Code Window)"]
        T1["Terminal: Temperature C - OUT"]
        T2["Terminal: Gain - OUT"]
        MUL["Multiply Function Node"]
        ADD["Add Function Node"]
        GT["Greater Than 100 Function Node"]
        T3["Terminal: Temperature F - IN"]
        T4["Terminal: High Alarm - IN"]
        WIRE1["Data Wire: Orange DBL"]
        WIRE2["Data Wire: Orange DBL"]
        WIRE3["Data Wire: Green Boolean"]
    end

    CTRL1 -.auto terminal.-> T1
    CTRL2 -.auto terminal.-> T2
    IND1 -.auto terminal.-> T3
    IND2 -.auto terminal.-> T4

    T1 --> WIRE1 --> MUL
    T2 --> MUL
    MUL --> WIRE2 --> ADD
    ADD --> T3
    ADD --> GT
    GT --> WIRE3 --> T4

    DECO -.no terminal.-> FP
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Dataflow Sequence — Execution Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User enters 40 in Temperature C Control"]
    B["Control terminal emits DBL value 40.0 on wire"]
    C["Multiply node: both inputs 40.0 and 1.8 available"]
    D["Multiply produces 72.0 on its output wire"]
    E["Add node: both inputs 72.0 and 32 available"]
    F["Add produces 104.0 — wired to two destinations"]
    G["Temperature F indicator updates to 104.0"]
    H["Greater than node: inputs 104.0 and 100.0 available"]
    I["Greater than produces TRUE Boolean"]
    J["High Alarm LED turns ON"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H --> I --> J
```

### 4.3 The Three Palettes — Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PAL["Three Core Palettes in LabVIEW"]
        direction TB
        P1["Controls Palette - summoned on FP via Right-Click"]
        P2["Functions Palette - summoned on BD via Right-Click"]
        P3["Tools Palette - summoned via Shift plus Right-Click"]
    end

    P1 --> P1A["Numeric, Boolean, String, Array, Cluster, Graph, Decorations"]
    P2 --> P2A["Programming, Mathematics, Signal Processing, I/O DAQ, Structures"]
    P3 --> P3A["Operating, Positioning, Labeling, Wiring, Probe, Breakpoint"]

    P1 -.provides objects for.-> FPWIN["Front Panel Window"]
    P2 -.provides nodes for.-> BDWIN["Block Diagram Window"]
    P3 -.modifies both.-> FPWIN
    P3 -.modifies both.-> BDWIN
```

### 4.4 Control vs Indicator — Border-Thickness Diagnostic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Object on FP"]
    A --> B{"Border thicker than its BD terminal?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["CONTROL: User input - data flows OUT to BD"]
    B -- No --> D["INDICATOR: System output - data flows IN from BD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most reliable diagnostic rule** when reading a LabVIEW screenshot on the KTU exam paper. Examiners present screenshots and ask "Identify which objects are controls and which are indicators." Use the border-thickness test, not the label text.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*With the help of a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Virtual Instrument. Differentiate clearly between its two main windows.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Virtual Instrument (VI), as defined in the LabVIEW development environment, consists of three components — the **Front Panel (FP)**, the **Block Diagram (BD)**, and the **Connector Pane/Icon**. The architecture is shown below:

1. **Front Panel (FP):** The interactive user interface of the VI. It is composed of **Controls** (input objects such as knobs, switches, numeric boxes) and **Indicators** (output objects such as LEDs, gauges, graphs, charts). The user interacts exclusively with this window.
2. **Block Diagram (BD):** The graphical source-code window that contains **terminals**, **nodes** (functions, sub-VIs, structures), and **wires** that define the dataflow logic. It is the executable counterpart of the FP.
3. **Connector Pane & Icon:** Defines the input/output interface so the VI can be reused as a SubVI.

| Feature | Front Panel | Block Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | User I/O | Dataflow logic |
| Default background | Grey | White |
| Contents | Controls & Indicators | Terminals, Nodes, Wires |
| Access | Default window on VI open | `Ctrl + E` |

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
*Explain the concept of dataflow programming in LabVIEW. Why is LabVIEW called a dataflow language?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LabVIEW is called a **dataflow programming language** because the execution order of its nodes is determined **entirely by the flow of data through wires**, rather than by the textual line-by-line sequence used in conventional procedural languages such as C or Python.

**Execution rule:** A node begins execution **only when data becomes available at all of its input terminals**. Once it completes, it passes its result to the next downstream node via the output wire.

**Implications:**

- A node with no required inputs (e.g., a constant) executes immediately.
- Independent code paths execute **in parallel** on multi-core processors.
- No "line numbers" — execution is dictated by wire connectivity, not by spatial position on the BD.

This is fundamentally different from text-based languages where the compiler enforces a sequential execution order.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally sub-divided as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain the **three main palettes** available in LabVIEW. How is each palette summoned? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a VI Front Panel and Block Diagram to compute the **area and perimeter of a rectangle** given its length and breadth as numeric controls. Show all block-diagram components and explain the dataflow. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Three LabVIEW Palettes (7 marks):**

| Palette | Purpose | How to Summon | Key Sub-Categories |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Controls Palette** | Adds user-interface objects (controls/indicators) to the Front Panel | **Right-click** anywhere on the FP window | Numeric, Boolean, String & Path, Array, Matrix & Cluster, List & Table, Graph, Decorations |
| **Functions Palette** | Adds functions, VIs, and structures to the Block Diagram | **Right-click** anywhere on the BD window | Programming (Structures, Numeric, Boolean, Comparison, File I/O, Array), Mathematics, Signal Processing, I/O (DAQmx), Express |
| **Tools Palette** | Selects the editing/operating tool (used to wire, label, probe, debug) | **Shift + Right-click** on either window | Operating Tool, Positioning Tool, Resizing Tool, Labeling Tool, Wiring Tool, Object Shortcut Menu Tool, Scrolling Tool, Breakpoint Tool, Probe Tool |

The Controls and Functions palettes can also be pinned (pin icon at top-left of palette) so they remain visible during editing.

---

**(b) Area & Perimeter of Rectangle (7 marks):**

**Front Panel objects to be placed:**

1. Numeric Control — *"Length"* (DBL, thick border = control)
2. Numeric Control — *"Breadth"* (DBL, thick border = control)
3. Numeric Indicator — *"Area"* (DBL, thin border = indicator)
4. Numeric Indicator — *"Perimeter"* (DBL, thin border = indicator)

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Area} = L \times B$$

$$\text{Perimeter} = 2 \times (L + B)$$

**Block Diagram construction steps:**

1. Place two **Multiply** nodes from *Functions Palette → Programming → Numeric*.
2. Place one **Add** node.
3. Place two **Numeric Constants**: one with value `2`, one used for multiplication (or use a constant multiplier via the Multiply node's second terminal).
4. Wire `Length` control → one input of **Multiply #1**; `Breadth` control → second input of **Multiply #1**. Output of Multiply #1 → `Area` indicator.
5. Wire `Length` → one input of **Add**; `Breadth` → second input of **Add**. Output of **Add** → one input of **Multiply #2**.
6. Wire constant `2` → second input of **Multiply #2**. Output → `Perimeter` indicator.

**Dataflow narrative:** Because the constants `Length` and `Breadth` are inputs, both Multiply and Add nodes have all their inputs satisfied as soon as the user provides values — so the area and perimeter paths execute **independently and in parallel** after the user enters data.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Differentiate between a **Control** and an **Indicator** on the LabVIEW Front Panel. Give **two examples** of each and state the visual border-thickness rule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to a **Temperature Conversion VI** (Celsius to Fahrenheit), draw the Block Diagram and explain how dataflow execution proceeds step-by-step. Use the formula $T_F = (T_C \times 1.8) + 32$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Controls vs. Indicators (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Control | Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of data flow | User → Block Diagram (outgoing arrow on terminal) | Block Diagram → User (incoming arrow on terminal) |
| Border thickness on FP | **Thicker** border than its BD terminal | **Thinner** border than its BD terminal |
| Terminal symbol on BD | Small triangle arrow pointing **right** | Small triangle arrow pointing **left** |
| Example 1 | Numeric Control (Knob for entering amplitude) | Numeric Indicator (Gauge for displaying voltage) |
| Example 2 | Boolean Push Button (START/STOP) | Round LED (Power ON indicator) |
| Default wire color | Same as data type (e.g., orange DBL) | Same as data type |

---

**(b) Block Diagram of Temperature Conversion VI (7 marks):**

The Block Diagram consists of the following nodes and terminals:

**Terminals (auto-generated from FP):**

- `Temp_C` (DBL Control, thick border)
- `Temp_F` (DBL Indicator, thin border)
- Two Numeric Constants: `1.8` and `32`

**Functions (nodes):**

- One **Multiply** node
- One **Add** node

**Wiring (dataflow graph):**

1. `Temp_C` output → x-input of **Multiply**.
2. Constant `1.8` → y-input of **Multiply**.
3. Output of **Multiply** → x-input of **Add**.
4. Constant `32` → y-input of **Add**.
5. Output of **Add** → `Temp_F` indicator.

**Step-by-step execution trace (assuming `Temp_C = 40`):**

- **T = 0:** User types `40` in the FP control. The terminal `Temp_C` emits the value `40.0` on its output wire.
- **T = 1:** The **Multiply** node now has BOTH required inputs available (`40.0` and `1.8`). It executes and produces `72.0`.
- **T = 2:** The **Add** node now has BOTH inputs (`72.0` and `32`). It executes and produces `104.0`.
- **T = 3:** The `Temp_F` indicator terminal receives `104.0` and updates the FP display.

**Final result:** $T_F = 104.0 \; °F$, consistent with $T_F = (40 \times 1.8) + 32 = 72 + 32 = 104$.

This trace illustrates the **dataflow paradigm**: each node fires only when its inputs are valid, and execution propagates downstream along the wires.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Chart" with "Graph":** A Chart holds history; a Graph plots an entire array. Examiners award 1 mark for the correct distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the auto-generated terminal rule:** Many students wrongly assume controls and indicators are independent of the BD. Always state that **each FP object auto-creates exactly one BD terminal**.
> 3. **Writing procedural code in explanations:** LabVIEW is **not** sequential. Do **not** say "first we multiply, then we add." Say "the Multiply node fires when both inputs are available; its output then satisfies one input of the Add node."
> 4. **Wrong border thickness interpretation:** If a question shows a screenshot and asks "is this object a control or indicator?", use the **border thickness test**, not the label.
> 5. **Mis-stating the shortcut keys:** `Ctrl + E` toggles windows; `Shift + Right-Click` summons the Tools Palette. Do not confuse them.
> 6. **SubVI node terminals:** Each connector pane supports a **maximum of 28 terminals**. Stating a wrong number costs marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (Print-Friendly Bullet List):**
>
> - A VI has **three components**: Front Panel, Block Diagram, Connector Pane/Icon.
> - The **Front Panel** contains **Controls** (inputs) and **Indicators** (outputs).
> - The **Block Diagram** contains **Terminals, Wires, Nodes (Functions/Structures/SubVIs)**.
> - Every FP object **auto-generates exactly one terminal** on the BD.
> - **Control** = thick border on FP; outgoing arrow on terminal; data flows OUT to BD.
> - **Indicator** = thin border on FP; incoming arrow on terminal; data flows IN from BD.
> - LabVIEW uses **dataflow execution**, not sequential line-by-line execution.
> - A node fires **only when ALL its inputs have valid data**.
> - Independent code paths execute in **parallel** on multi-core processors.
> - **Wire color encodes data type**: Orange = DBL, Blue = I32, Green = Boolean, Pink = String.
> - **Wire thickness** encodes data structure: thin = scalar, medium = array, thick = cluster.
> - **Three palettes**: Controls (FP), Functions (BD), Tools (`Shift + Right-Click`).
> - **Shortcut to toggle FP ↔ BD**: `Ctrl + E`.
> - A **broken run button** indicates a wiring error or missing input; click it to view the **Error List**.
> - A **Chart** displays history as data arrives; a **Graph** plots a complete array in one sweep.
> - A **SubVI** is a VI used inside another VI; it has a **Connector Pane** (max 28 terminals) and an **Icon**.
> - **Front Panel grid spacing** is **10 pixels** by default.
> - **Auto-indexing** turns a wire entering a For-Loop into a 1-D array.
> - A **tunnel** passes values across a loop border; a **shift register** carries state across iterations.
> - Decorations (lines, boxes, arrows) carry **no terminal** and do **not** participate in execution.
> - The three mechanical actions of a Boolean control are **Switch, Latch, and Until Released**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building a Connector Pane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Building a Connector Pane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
The **Connector Pane** in LabVIEW is a reserved region on the Front Panel of a Virtual Instrument (VI) that defines the **input and output terminals** through which data flows into and out of a **SubVI** (a VI used as a subroutine). The connector pane acts as the **public interface** of a SubVI, mapping its internal Controls (inputs) and Indicators (outputs) to the connector terminals that are wired by the calling VI on its Block Diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A SubVI is created by giving a VI a **Connector Pane** and an **Icon**. Without a connector pane, a VI cannot be used as a modular building block inside another VI. This is a **CO2 (Module 2) – CO3 bridging concept** and frequently carries 7–14 mark weightage in ESE.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **universal travel adapter** that you use to plug an Indian device into a foreign socket. The adapter has:
- A specific **shape and number of pins** (pattern of the connector pane).
- A specific **purpose for each pin** — one for live, one for neutral, one for ground (terminal assignment).
- The **internal wiring** connects the pins to the appliance (analogous to the Block Diagram wiring inside the SubVI).

Similarly, a Connector Pane is the **"shape of the plug"** that the SubVI presents to the outside world. The calling VI must "plug in" wires into these specific terminals to send data *in* and receive results *out*.

## Why is the Connector Pane Needed?
1. **Reusability** – The same SubVI can be called from many places with different inputs.
2. **Encapsulation** – Internal logic of the SubVI is hidden; only inputs/outputs are exposed.
3. **Modularity** – The user calling the SubVI does not need to know *how* it works, only *what* to pass and *what* to expect.
4. **Hierarchical VI design** – Enables building complex VIs by composing smaller, tested VIs.

## Key Terminology
| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Terminal** | A single input or output slot on the connector pane. |
| **Pattern** | The geometric arrangement of terminals (e.g., 2×2, 4×2, 5×3). |
| **Required Terminal** | A terminal the caller *must* wire (appears as bold). |
| **Recommended Terminal** | An optional terminal that the caller should wire (appears as plain). |
| **Optional Terminal** | May be left unwired (appears as grey, dashed when not wired). |
| **Control Terminal** | Maps to a Front Panel Control (receives data *into* the SubVI). |
| **Indicator Terminal** | Maps to a Front Panel Indicator (sends data *out of* the SubVI). |
| **Icon** | A pictorial representation of the SubVI shown on the Block Diagram. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** A SubVI **must** have both an **Icon** and a **Connector Pane**. The standard error cluster is allocated the **bottom-right** terminal in the 4×2×2×4×2×4 default pattern (the lower-right corner is reserved by LabVIEW convention).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## Building a Connector Pane — Conceptual Logic Steps

### Step 1: Create a VI
- A new VI is created via `File → New VI`. It opens with a **Front Panel** and a **Block Diagram** window.

### Step 2: Design the Front Panel
- Add **Controls** (inputs the caller will provide) and **Indicators** (outputs the SubVI will return) to the Front Panel.

### Step 3: Implement Logic on the Block Diagram
- Wire the controls and indicators to perform the desired operation. This is the *internal* working of the SubVI.

### Step 4: Open the Icon & Connector Pane Editor
- Right-click the **VI Icon** (top-right corner of the Front Panel) → select **Show Connector**.
- LabVIEW now displays the default connector pattern (usually a small grid of rectangles).

### Step 5: Select an Appropriate Pattern
- Right-click the connector pane → **Patterns**.
- Choose a pattern that has enough terminals for the inputs and outputs. Common patterns:
  - **2×2 pattern** (4 terminals) – for very simple SubVIs.
  - **4×2×2×4×2×4 pattern** – the LabVIEW default, providing up to 14 terminals plus space for **error in** and **error out** clusters.
  - The number of available terminals = **terminals left of the wiring area + terminals right of the wiring area**.

### Step 6: Assign Controls and Indicators to Terminals
- Click once on a Front Panel control/indicator, then click on the desired connector terminal.
- The terminal changes color — **pink for required input, blue for required output, light blue for recommended input, light pink for recommended output, grey for optional**.
- **Conventions**:
  - **Inputs (Controls) on the LEFT** of the connector pane.
  - **Outputs (Indicators) on the RIGHT** of the connector pane.
  - **Top-left terminal** is reserved for the **Reference** (if any).
  - **Bottom-right terminal** is reserved for **error out**.

### Step 7: Define Required / Optional / Recommended Status
- Right-click a terminal → choose:
  - **Required** – the caller *must* wire this; the SubVI will be **broken** otherwise.
  - **Recommended** – the caller *should* wire this; SubVI remains runnable without it.
  - **Optional** – may be left unwired; appears as a dashed terminal.

### Step 8: Save the SubVI
- Save with a descriptive name. The SubVI can now be dropped into other VIs as a node on their Block Diagram.

## KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Item | Standard / Convention | KTU Note |
|------|----------------------|----------|
| Default Pattern | 4×2×2×4×2×4 (14 terminals) | Most common in syllabus diagrams. |
| Input Terminals | Placed on the **Left** side | Memorize — often a 1-mark question. |
| Output Terminals | Placed on the **Right** side | Symmetric to inputs. |
| Error Cluster | Bottom-right terminal | Reserved by LabVIEW convention. |
| Reference In | Top-left terminal | If present, must be top-left. |
| Color of Required Input | **Pink** | Important for terminal identification. |
| Color of Required Output | **Blue** | Important for terminal identification. |
| Color of Optional | **Grey (dashed)** | When unwired. |
| Maximum Terminals (Default) | 14 (excluding error in/out) | Pattern supports up to 16 with error clusters. |
| Maximum Terminals (Maximum pattern) | 28 | For very complex SubVIs. |
| Path to Edit | `Right-click Icon → Show Connector` | Common ESE viva question. |
| Mandatory Elements of a SubVI | **Icon + Connector Pane** | If either is missing, the VI is *not* a SubVI. |
| Labeling Convention | Use `<VI>.v` for SubVIs | Naming convention. |

## Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Modular Test Systems**: A *Temperature Sensor Driver* SubVI exposes one input (voltage) and one output (°C), used in hundreds of test points.
- **Signal Processing Libraries**: DSP functions (FFT, filtering) are packaged as SubVIs with standardized connector panes for reuse.
- **Data Acquisition (DAQ)**: Each sensor channel is encapsulated as a SubVI, making scaling, calibration, and unit conversion reusable.
- **Industrial Automation**: PLC-like reusable logic blocks in LabVIEW follow identical SubVI rules.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "How many terminals does a 4×2×2×4×2×4 pattern have?", the answer is **14 data terminals + 2 error terminals = 16 effective positions**, but **14** is the count used for inputs/outputs of the SubVI logic. The KTU answer key typically expects **14**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## Step-by-Step Procedure to Build a Connector Pane

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard ESE 14-mark question pattern**. Learn the steps verbatim.

### Step A — Create the VI
1. Launch LabVIEW.
2. Go to `File → New VI`.
3. The Front Panel (grey window) and Block Diagram (white window) appear.

### Step B — Design the Front Panel Interface
4. Place two **Numeric Controls** on the Front Panel → label them `Number A` and `Number B`.
5. Place one **Numeric Indicator** on the Front Panel → label it `Sum`.
6. Place one **Boolean Indicator** → label it `Overflow Flag`.

### Step C — Implement the Block Diagram Logic
7. Open the Block Diagram (`Window → Show Block Diagram` or press `Ctrl+E`).
8. Add an **Add function** (`Programming → Numeric → Add`).
9. Wire `Number A` and `Number B` to the Add function.
10. Wire the Add output to `Sum`.
11. Add a **Greater Than 0** comparison; wire Sum and constant 100 to it.
12. Wire the comparison output to `Overflow Flag`.

### Step D — Open the Icon & Connector Pane
13. On the Front Panel, **right-click** the VI Icon (small picture at the top-right corner).
14. Select **Show Connector** from the context menu.
15. The default pattern (a small grid) appears next to the icon.

### Step E — Choose a Connector Pattern
16. Right-click the connector pane (the grid) → **Patterns**.
17. Select the **4×2×2×4×2×4 pattern** (or any pattern with at least 4 terminals).
18. The pattern shows small rectangles — these are the **terminals**.

### Step F — Assign Front Panel Objects to Terminals
19. Click on the **Number A** control on the Front Panel (cursor becomes a "spool" symbol).
20. Click on the **top-left** terminal of the connector pane — `Number A` is assigned as the first **input**.
21. Click on the **Number B** control → click the **middle-left** terminal — assigned as the second **input**.
22. Click on the **Sum** indicator → click the **top-right** terminal — assigned as the first **output**.
23. Click on the **Overflow Flag** indicator → click the **middle-right** terminal — assigned as the second **output**.

### Step G — Verify and Save
24. Right-click the connector pane → **Show Icon** to revert the view to the icon.
25. Save the VI as `Adder_with_Overflow.vi` in a suitable folder.
26. Drop the SubVI into another VI's Block Diagram — it now appears as an **icon** with terminals on its perimeter.

## Analytical Matrix — Terminal Position Reference

| Terminal Location | Typical Use | Wiring Rule |
|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| Top-Left | `Reference`, `Resource` | Optional; convention only. |
| Middle-Left | Primary Inputs | Required if data is mandatory. |
| Bottom-Left | Secondary Inputs / Enable | Usually optional. |
| Top-Right | Primary Outputs | Returns the main result. |
| Middle-Right | Secondary Outputs | Returns flags or status. |
| Bottom-Right | `Error Out` cluster | Reserved by LabVIEW convention. |

## Common Errors and Their Resolution

| Error | Cause | Resolution |
|-------|-------|-----------|
| Broken Run Arrow | A required input is unwired in the calling VI. | Wire the terminal or mark it Optional. |
| Pattern appears too small | Wrong pattern selected. | Right-click → `Patterns` → choose larger one. |
| Terminal does not accept the click | The connector pane is in icon mode. | Right-click → `Show Connector`. |
| Object doesn't get assigned | Two clicks were made on the same object. | Click the object once, then click the terminal once. |
| SubVI not visible in Functions Palette | Saved in wrong location. | Place it in `user.lib` or use `Select a VI` from palette. |

## Wiring Rule Summary

$$
\text{Total Terminals} = \text{Left side terminals} + \text{Right side terminals}
$$

$$
\text{Default (4×2×2×4×2×4)} = 7_{\text{left}} + 7_{\text{right}} = 14 \text{ data terminals}
$$

$$
\text{With error clusters} = 14 + 2 = 16 \text{ positions}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KVL 2024 Pitfall:** Students confuse the *number of input terminals* with the *total number of terminals*. The question "What is the maximum number of input terminals in a 4×2×2×4×2×4 connector pane?" is answered as **7**, not 14, because 7 terminals are on each side.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 — Mermaid Block Diagram: Connector Pane Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Open New VI] --> fpA[Design Front Panel]
    fpA --> fpA1[Add Controls - Inputs]
    fpA --> fpA2[Add Indicators - Outputs]
    fpA1 --> bdA[Build Block Diagram Logic]
    fpA2 --> bdA
    bdA --> bdA1[Wire Controls to Functions]
    bdA1 --> bdA2[Wire Functions to Indicators]
    bdA2 --> iconA[Right-click VI Icon]
    iconA --> iconA1[Select Show Connector]
    iconA1 --> patA[Right-click Connector Pane]
    patA --> patA1[Choose Pattern - e.g. 4x2x2x4x2x4]
    patA1 --> assignA[Assign Front Panel Objects to Terminals]
    assignA --> assignA1[Click Control then Click Input Terminal]
    assignA1 --> assignA2[Click Indicator then Click Output Terminal]
    assignA2 --> statusA[Set Required or Recommended or Optional]
    statusA --> saveA[Save the SubVI]
    saveA --> doneA[SubVI Ready for Reuse in Other VIs]
```

## 4.2 — Mermaid Diagram: Default 4×2×2×4×2×4 Connector Pane Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT_SIDE_INPUTS["Left Side - Inputs"]
        L1[Top Left - Reference]:::opt
        L2[Upper Mid Left - Input1]:::req
        L3[Center Mid Left - Input2]:::req
        L4[Lower Mid Left - Input3]:::rec
        L5[Bottom Left - Input4]:::opt
    end
    subgraph RIGHT_SIDE_OUTPUTS["Right Side - Outputs"]
        R1[Top Right - Output1]:::req
        R2[Upper Mid Right - Output2]:::req
        R3[Center Mid Right - Output3]:::rec
        R4[Lower Mid Right - Output4]:::opt
        R5[Bottom Right - Error Out]:::err
    end
    classDef req fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
```

## 4.3 — Mermaid Flow: Calling a SubVI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainVI[Main VI Block Diagram] --> dragSub[Drag SubVI from Functions Palette]
    dragSub --> placeSub[Place SubVI Node on Diagram]
    placeSub --> wireIn[Wire Caller Outputs to SubVI Input Terminals]
    wireIn --> subExec[SubVI Executes Internal Logic]
    subExec --> wireOut[Read SubVI Output Terminals]
    wireOut --> useResult[Use Results in Main VI]
```

## 4.4 — Mermaid Sequence: Terminal Assignment Action

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant FP as Front Panel
    participant CP as Connector Pane
    U->>FP: Click Control Object Once
    FP-->>U: Cursor becomes Spool Tool
    U->>CP: Click Terminal Rectangle
    CP-->>U: Terminal turns Pink for Required Input
    Note over U,CP: Repeat for each Control and Indicator
    U->>CP: Right-click Terminal
    U->>CP: Choose Required or Recommended or Optional
    CP-->>U: Terminal Color Updated
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above represent **architectural flow** mappings of the Connector Pane construction process, since a literal physical schematic of the connector pane grid is a screenshot from LabVIEW rather than a procedural flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a Connector Pane in LabVIEW. Why is it required to make a SubVI?**

**Model Answer:**
A Connector Pane is a region on the Front Panel of a LabVIEW VI that contains **input and output terminals** which act as the interface for passing data into and out of a SubVI. It maps the SubVI's internal Controls and Indicators to terminals that the calling VI can wire to.

It is required to convert a normal VI into a **SubVI**, enabling reusability, modularity, and encapsulation of logic. Without a connector pane, the VI cannot accept inputs from or return outputs to another VI's Block Diagram.

**Key Points:**
- Defines the **public interface** of a SubVI.
- Maps each Control/Indicator to a terminal.
- Required for **reusability** across multiple VIs.
- A VI without a connector pane is just a *standalone* VI, not a SubVI.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: List any four connector pane patterns available in LabVIEW. State the LabVIEW convention for placing inputs and outputs.**

**Model Answer:**
Four common connector pane patterns in LabVIEW are:
1. **2×2 pattern** – 4 terminals.
2. **4×2×2×4 pattern** – 12 terminals.
3. **4×2×2×4×2×4 pattern** – 14 data terminals (the LabVIEW default).
4. **Maximum pattern** – 28 terminals.

**Conventions:**
- **Inputs (Controls) are placed on the LEFT** side of the connector pane.
- **Outputs (Indicators) are placed on the RIGHT** side.
- **Error cluster is reserved for the bottom-right** terminal.
- **Reference (if used) is reserved for the top-left** terminal.

---

## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Module 2 - 14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the term **Connector Pane** in LabVIEW with a neat sketch of the default pattern. List the steps to build a connector pane for a SubVI that takes two numeric inputs and returns their sum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the LabVIEW conventions for assigning Controls and Indicators to connector terminals. Explain the difference between *Required*, *Recommended*, and *Optional* terminals with their visual indicators. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Definition:**
A Connector Pane in LabVIEW is a set of terminals on the Front Panel of a SubVI that defines the inputs it receives and the outputs it returns when used inside another VI. The default pattern is **4×2×2×4×2×4**, providing 14 data terminals.

**Sketch of Default Pattern:**

```
+----------------+----------------+
| Top-Left Ref   | Top-Right Out |
+----------------+----------------+
| In1            | Out1          |
+----------------+----------------+
| In2            | Out2          |
+----------------+----------------+
| In3            | Out3          |
+----------------+----------------+
| In4            | Out4          |
+----------------+----------------+
| In5            | Err Out       |
+----------------+----------------+
```

**Steps to Build a Connector Pane for a 2-Input Summation SubVI:**
1. Open LabVIEW → `File → New VI`.
2. Place two **Numeric Controls** on the Front Panel — label them `A` and `B`.
3. Place one **Numeric Indicator** on the Front Panel — label it `Sum`.
4. On the Block Diagram, add the **Add** function (`Programming → Numeric → Add`).
5. Wire `A` and `B` to the two input terminals of the Add function.
6. Wire the output of the Add function to the `Sum` indicator.
7. Right-click the **VI Icon** on the Front Panel → select **Show Connector**.
8. The default pattern appears; right-click it → choose a pattern with at least **3 terminals** (e.g., 2×2 or 4×2×2×4).
9. Click on the `A` control once → click on the **top-left input terminal** of the connector pane.
10. Click on the `B` control once → click on the **middle-left input terminal**.
11. Click on the `Sum` indicator once → click on the **top-right output terminal**.
12. Save the VI as `Adder.vi`. It can now be used as a SubVI in any other VI.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Conventions for Terminal Assignment:**
1. **Inputs are placed on the LEFT** of the connector pane.
2. **Outputs are placed on the RIGHT** of the connector pane.
3. **Reference input** (if used) is at the **top-left** corner.
4. **Error cluster output** is at the **bottom-right** corner.
5. Frequently used terminals are placed **closer to the wiring area** (the center).

**Required, Recommended, and Optional Terminals:**

| Type | Behavior | Visual Indicator |
|------|----------|------------------|
| **Required** | Caller *must* wire; otherwise the calling VI is broken. | **Pink** for input, **Blue** for output. |
| **Recommended** | Caller *should* wire; the SubVI runs even if unwired, but a warning is shown. | Light blue for input, light pink for output. |
| **Optional** | Caller may wire; appears as a dashed terminal when unwired. | **Grey** with dashed border. |

To set the type: right-click the terminal → select **This Connection Is → Required / Recommended / Optional**.

**Practical Significance:**
- A **Required** terminal for *Power Supply Voltage* in a sensor driver SubVI ensures the user cannot forget to provide it.
- A **Recommended** terminal for *Filter Cutoff Frequency* reminds the user to set it but allows default operation.
- An **Optional** terminal for *Diagnostic Output* is only used in debug mode.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (Module 2 - 14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **default connector pane pattern** of LabVIEW. State the maximum number of terminals it can hold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure to create a SubVI from an existing VI. How does the connector pane influence modular VI design? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Default Connector Pane Pattern:**
The default LabVIEW connector pane is the **4×2×2×4×2×4 pattern**. It has 14 data terminals — 7 on the left side for inputs and 7 on the right side for outputs.

**Diagram:**

```
+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+   +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
| TL | ML | ML | ML | ML | ML | BL |   | TR | MR | MR | MR | MR | MR | BR |
| Ref| I1 | I2 | I3 | I4 | I5 | I6 |   | O1 | O2 | O3 | O4 | O5 | O6 | Err|
+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+   +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

Where:
- **TL** = Top-Left, **ML** = Middle-Left, **BL** = Bottom-Left (inputs)
- **TR** = Top-Right, **MR** = Middle-Right, **BR** = Bottom-Right (outputs)
- The **BR** terminal is reserved for the **error cluster**.

**Maximum Terminals:**
The default pattern supports **14 data terminals**. If more terminals are required, the *Maximum Pattern* can be selected, which supports up to **28 terminals**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Procedure to Create a SubVI from an Existing VI:**

1. **Build the VI completely** — design the Front Panel and wire the Block Diagram logic.
2. **Identify Inputs and Outputs** — decide which Front Panel objects are inputs (Controls) and outputs (Indicators).
3. **Create the Icon** — right-click the VI Icon on the Front Panel → **Edit Icon** → draw a custom icon or use `Edit → Clear Icon` for a default one.
4. **Open the Connector Pane** — right-click the VI Icon → **Show Connector**.
5. **Choose a Pattern** — right-click the connector pane → **Patterns** → select a pattern with enough terminals.
6. **Assign Terminals** — click each Control/Indicator, then click the desired terminal on the connector pane.
7. **Set Terminal Type** — right-click each terminal → set as Required / Recommended / Optional.
8. **Save the VI** — use `File → Save As` and name the file descriptively (e.g., `Temperature_Converter.vi`).
9. **Use the SubVI** — drag it from the Functions Palette (`Select a VI...`) into another VI's Block Diagram, then wire the terminals.

**Influence of Connector Pane on Modular VI Design:**
- **Encapsulation:** The internal logic is hidden; only the connector pane interface is visible.
- **Reusability:** The same SubVI can be called from multiple VIs with different inputs, reducing code duplication.
- **Readability:** Block Diagrams of large VIs become simpler because complex logic is replaced by a single SubVI icon.
- **Maintainability:** A bug fix in the SubVI propagates to all callers automatically.
- **Hierarchical Design:** Large systems are built bottom-up — sensor drivers, signal conditioning, and analysis blocks are all SubVIs composed in a top-level Main VI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing the number of terminals:** Students often say "4×2×2×4×2×4 has 16 terminals" — the correct answer is **14 data terminals** (plus 2 reserved for error clusters).
> 2. **Wrong terminal placement convention:** Writing "outputs on the left" loses 1 mark immediately. The convention is **inputs on the left, outputs on the right**.
> 3. **Skipping the Icon:** A SubVI *must* have both an Icon and a Connector Pane. Mentioning only the connector pane is incomplete.
> 4. **Forgetting to save:** The SubVI is only usable after being saved as a `.vi` file.
> 5. **Not mentioning Error cluster reservation:** The bottom-right terminal is reserved by LabVIEW convention for the **error out** cluster. Missing this point costs 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 6. **Confusing "Required" with "Recommended":** Required = *must* wire (broken otherwise). Recommended = *should* wire (runs with default, but warning shown).
> 7. **Wrong number of input vs. output terminals:** A 14-terminal pattern has **7 inputs and 7 outputs maximum**, not 14 each.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Connector Pane** is the **interface of a SubVI**, defining its inputs and outputs.
- A VI becomes a **SubVI** only when both an **Icon** and a **Connector Pane** are assigned.
- The **default pattern** is **4×2×2×4×2×4** with **14 data terminals** (7 input + 7 output).
- **Inputs are placed on the LEFT, outputs on the RIGHT** (LabVIEW convention).
- The **error cluster** is reserved for the **bottom-right** terminal.
- The **reference (if any)** is reserved for the **top-left** terminal.
- To open the connector pane: **right-click the VI Icon → Show Connector**.
- To choose a pattern: **right-click the connector pane → Patterns**.
- To assign: **click the Control/Indicator once, then click a terminal**.
- **Required** terminal — caller must wire; **Recommended** — caller should wire; **Optional** — may be left unwired.
- The Maximum Pattern supports up to **28 terminals**.
- SubVIs promote **modularity, reusability, encapsulation, and hierarchical design** in LabVIEW.
- A SubVI is invoked by dragging it from the **Functions Palette** (or using `Select a VI...`).
- A broken run arrow in the calling VI usually indicates an **unwired Required terminal** of the SubVI.
- SubVI files use the extension **`.vi`**, identical to a normal VI — the difference is purely in the presence of the Icon + Connector Pane.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** Expect 1 question in Part A (3 marks) and 1 full question in Part B (14 marks) per exam cycle on SubVI/Icon/Connector Pane topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displaying Sub VIs and Express VIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Displaying Sub VIs and Express VIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sub VIs – Formal Academic Definition

A **Sub VI (Sub Virtual Instrument)** in LabVIEW is a VI that is used inside another VI, analogous to a subroutine or function in text-based programming languages. It is constructed from an existing VI by defining an **Icon** (the graphical representation) and a **Connector Pane** (the terminal mapping) that exposes its inputs and outputs to the calling (host) VI. A Sub VI inherits all the structural properties of a top-level VI: it has a **Front Panel**, a **Block Diagram**, an **Icon/Connector**, and can be saved, opened, and called independently.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Displaying Sub VIs and Express VIs forms the backbone of modular programming in LabVIEW. A Sub VI is recognized on a block diagram by its **icon (yellow background by default)** with input/output terminals, and it can be **re-used** across multiple VIs, promoting hierarchical and modular VIs.

### 1.2 Express VIs – Formal Academic Definition

An **Express VI** is a specialized type of Sub VI designed by National Instruments to simplify the configuration of common measurement tasks. Express VIs are characterized by:

- A **blue background** icon (whereas standard Sub VIs have a **yellow background**).
- An **interactive configuration dialog box** that opens when the VI is placed on the block diagram.
- A **limited but user-friendly set of inputs/outputs** that can be customized.
- Automatic conversion of data types to match the Express VI's internal representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Difference (KTU Hot Topic):** A standard Sub VI requires manual wiring to the connector pane, while an Express VI provides a **configuration-based approach** through dialog boxes. Express VIs internally expand to several standard Sub VIs when placed on the block diagram.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Sub VI Analogy — "The Custom-Built Machine"**
Think of a Sub VI as a **custom-built kitchen appliance** (e.g., a coffee machine) that you designed once. The coffee machine has:
- **Buttons & Display** → corresponds to the **Front Panel** (controls and indicators).
- **Internal circuitry** → corresponds to the **Block Diagram** (wiring and logic).
- **Power socket and power cord** → corresponds to the **Connector Pane** (the standardized interface to plug it into a wall outlet, i.e., the host VI).

You can place this same coffee machine in **any room** (any calling VI) — the kitchen (host VI) only needs to provide power and a cup; the machine handles the rest internally.

**Express VI Analogy — "The Smart Wizard Appliance"**
An Express VI is like a **smart, pre-configured washing machine** with a touchscreen. You don't open the back panel to wire internal connections; you simply select "Cotton Wash" or "Delicates" through the touchscreen (the **Configuration Dialog Box**), and the machine automatically configures the **water level, spin speed, temperature, and duration** internally. It is built for **common tasks** and is **highly user-friendly**.

### 1.4 Physical Properties and Standard Metrics

| Property | Sub VI | Express VI |
|---|---|---|
| Background Color on Block Diagram | **Yellow** | **Blue** |
| Configuration Method | Connector Pane (Manual Wiring) | **Interactive Dialog Box** |
| Icon Customization | Yes (Icon Editor) | Limited (Pre-defined templates) |
| Granularity | **High** (full control) | Moderate (task-oriented) |
| Execution Speed | Faster (compiled) | Slightly slower (internal expansion) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A frequently asked KTU question is *"How do you visually distinguish a Sub VI from an Express VI on the block diagram?"* The correct answer is: **Sub VI has a yellow background; Express VI has a blue background.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Displaying Sub VIs

A Sub VI can be displayed and integrated into a host VI through a well-defined procedural workflow. The following structured steps describe the engineering logic:

**Step 1 — Design the Inner VI**
Construct a fully functional VI with the desired **Front Panel (controls and indicators)** and **Block Diagram (wires, structures, functions)**. This VI will serve as the foundation of the Sub VI.

**Step 2 — Define the Icon**
The icon is the small graphical symbol that visually identifies the VI on the block diagram. The default icon is a LabVIEW logo. Custom icons are created using the **Icon Editor** (right-click on the icon pane → *Edit Icon*). The icon aids in **visual recognition** of the Sub VI's function.

**Step 3 — Define the Connector Pane**
The connector pane is the set of terminals that maps the Sub VI's inputs and outputs. It is accessed by right-clicking on the icon → *Show Connector*. The procedure is:

- **Why:** The connector pane is the "public interface" of the Sub VI; only the controls/indicators wired to the connector can be accessed from the host VI.
- **How:** Each terminal on the connector pane is assigned to a specific control or indicator using the **Wiring Tool** (selected from the Tools palette). The recommended pattern is to use up to **14 terminals** (4-2-2-4 pattern is common: 4 inputs on the left, 2 on top, 2 on bottom, 4 outputs on the right) or **28 terminals** for complex VIs.

**Step 4 — Save the Sub VI**
Save the file with a descriptive name in a folder accessible to LabVIEW's search path. LabVIEW automatically treats any VI with a defined connector pane as a Sub VI candidate.

**Step 5 — Call the Sub VI from the Host VI**
The Sub VI is "displayed" on the block diagram of the host VI by:

- Drag-and-drop from the **File System** into the block diagram.
- Or using **Functions Palette → Select a VI…** and browsing to the saved file.

**Step 6 — Wire Inputs and Outputs**
Once placed, the Sub VI appears as an icon with terminals corresponding to the connector pane. The host VI wires its data sources to the Sub VI's input terminals and the Sub VI's output terminals to the host VI's indicators or downstream logic.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework for Displaying Express VIs

Express VIs are placed and configured through a streamlined workflow:

**Step 1 — Select the Express VI from the Palette**
Express VIs are located in the **Functions Palette** under their respective categories. For example, *DAQ Assistant* is found under *Functions → Input → DAQmx – Data Acquisition*.

**Step 2 — Place on the Block Diagram**
When the Express VI is dropped on the block diagram, its **Configuration Dialog Box** opens automatically.

**Step 3 — Configure via Dialog Box**
The dialog box provides a guided, user-friendly interface to set task parameters. The configuration is stored within the Express VI; no internal code is visible.

**Step 4 — Display Inputs and Outputs**
The Express VI's terminals can be displayed by **resizing the Express VI's icon** on the block diagram. The user drags the lower edge downward to reveal additional input/output terminals.

**Step 5 — Wire the Visible Terminals**
Visible terminals are wired like any other VI terminal. Hidden terminals use **default values** unless explicitly enabled.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this topic is procedural rather than equation-heavy, the "formula sheet" is a structured comparison and procedural reference table:

| S.No. | Aspect | Sub VI | Express VI |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default Icon Background | **Yellow** | **Blue** |
| 2 | Configuration Method | **Connector Pane** (manual) | **Dialog Box** (interactive) |
| 3 | Number of Terminals | Up to **28** (recommended 14) | Limited (configurable) |
| 4 | Internal Visibility | Full block diagram visible | Internal structure hidden |
| 5 | Reusability | **High** | Moderate |
| 6 | Best Use Case | Custom, complex logic | **Common measurement tasks** |
| 7 | Custom Icon Support | Yes (Icon Editor) | No (fixed templates) |
| 8 | Placement Trigger | Drag-and-drop | **Auto-opens dialog box** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Sub VIs and Express VIs enable **Hierarchical Design** in test and measurement systems. In a real-world application like an **automotive engine test rig**, an Express VI may handle the *DAQ Assistant* (analog input from temperature sensors), while a custom Sub VI performs the *PID control algorithm* for the throttle body. This modularity allows engineers to **debug, maintain, and scale** large VIs efficiently.

### 2.4 Key Conceptual Clarifications

> [!NOTE]
> **"Displaying" a Sub VI on a block diagram** means *placing the Sub VI icon* on the host VI's block diagram, where it appears as a yellow icon with its connector terminals ready for wiring.
>
