> **Auto Indexing** is a property of For Loops and While Loops in LabVIEW in which arrays wired to the input tunnel of a loop are automatically indexed one element per iteration, and scalar values produced inside the loop are automatically accumulated at the output tunnel into a 1D array.
> 
> **(1 mark)** — Definition.
> **(1 mark)** — Input tunnel behavior.
> **(1 mark)** — Output tunnel behavior.

### Question 2: Differentiate between an indexed tunnel and a last-value tunnel. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Indexed Tunnel (Solid Square) | Last-Value Tunnel (Hollow Square) |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Solid square | Hollow / empty square |
| Input | Delivers one array element per iteration | Delivers the final value only |
| Output | Accumulates scalars into a 1D array | Passes only the last scalar value |
| Default in For Loop | Yes (both) | No |
| Default in While Loop (output) | No | Yes |

> **(1 mark)** — Indexed tunnel explanation.
> **(1 mark)** — Last-value tunnel explanation.
> **(1 mark)** — Tabular differentiation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of Auto Indexing in For Loops with a neat block diagram. How does the output tunnel behave when auto-indexing is enabled versus disabled? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Auto Indexing in a For Loop is the automatic mechanism by which an input array is element-by-element processed inside the loop, and the output scalars are aggregated into a 1D output array.

**Indexed Output Tunnel (2 marks):**
- The output tunnel appears as a **solid square**.
- One scalar per iteration is stored.
- The resulting output is a **1D array** of length $N$, where $N$ is the number of iterations.

**Disabled (Last-Value) Output Tunnel (2 marks):**
- The output tunnel appears as a **hollow square**.
- Only the value from the **final iteration** is delivered.
- The output type is a **scalar**, not an array.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.3 of these notes.

---

**(b) A 1D array of length 8 containing temperature readings is wired to the input tunnel of a For Loop with auto-indexing enabled. The loop body multiplies each element by 1.8 and adds 32 (Fahrenheit conversion). Determine the number of iterations, the iteration indices, and the size of the output array. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Input array size:** $L = 8$.

**Step 2 — Number of iterations:** Since the input is auto-indexed,

$$
N = L = 8 \quad \text{iterations}
$$

**Step 3 — Iteration indices:** Zero-based indexing gives

$$
i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}
$$

**Step 4 — Body operation:** For each element $T_i$ in Celsius:

$$
F_i = 1.8 \times T_i + 32
$$

**Step 5 — Output array:**

$$
A_{out} = \{F_0, F_1, F_2, F_3, F_4, F_5, F_6, F_7\}
$$

**Step 6 — Output array size:**

$$
\boxed{\text{size}(A_{out}) = 8 \quad \text{(1D array of 8 Fahrenheit values)}}
$$

> **(1 mark)** — Input size, **(1 mark)** — N = 8, **(1 mark)** — Indices, **(2 marks)** — Conversion formula applied, **(1 mark)** — Output size, **(1 mark)** — Final answer with units.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare the default auto-indexing behavior of For Loops and While Loops. Why is there a difference, and how do you make a While Loop produce a 1D output array? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**For Loop Default Behavior (2 marks):**
- Both input and output tunnels are **auto-indexed by default** (solid squares).
- Input array size determines N automatically.
- Output is a 1D array of length N.

**While Loop Default Behavior (2 marks):**
- Input tunnel is auto-indexed (solid square) by default.
- Output tunnel is **NOT auto-indexed** (hollow square / last-value) by default.
- The loop terminates based on a **stop condition**, not a count.

**Reason for the Difference (2 marks):**
- A For Loop has a **predetermined iteration count** (N), so accumulating a 1D output array is straightforward.
- A While Loop has a **dynamic, condition-based iteration count**; LabVIEW cannot pre-allocate the output buffer size, so it defaults to the last-value behavior to avoid memory errors.

**Producing a 1D Output Array from a While Loop (1 mark):**
- Right-click the output tunnel → select **Enable Indexing** (or *Append to Array* style).
- The output becomes a 1D array whose length equals the number of iterations executed.

---

**(b) A 2D array of size 5 × 4 is wired to the auto-indexed input tunnel of a For Loop. Each iteration computes the sum of one row. Draw the block diagram and determine the shape of the output array. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Input dimensions:** $5 \times 4$ (5 rows, 4 columns).

**Step 2 — Number of iterations:**

$$
N = \dim_0(A_{in}) = 5 \quad \text{iterations}
$$

**Step 3 — Each iteration receives a 1D row of length 4:**

$$
\text{Row}_i = \{a_{i,0}, a_{i,1}, a_{i,2}, a_{i,3}\} \quad \text{for} \quad i = 0,1,2,3,4
$$

**Step 4 — Row sum computation:**

$$
s_i = \sum_{j=0}^{3} a_{i,j}
$$

**Step 5 — Output tunnel accumulates the 5 scalars into a 1D array:**

$$
A_{out} = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}, \quad \text{size} = 1 \times 5
$$

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a For Loop with a 2D array entering an indexed input tunnel (solid square), the iteration terminal $i$ ranging 0 to 4, a *Sum* node inside the body, and an indexed output tunnel producing a 1D array of length 5.

> **(1 mark)** — Dimensions, **(1 mark)** — N = 5, **(1 mark)** — Row identification, **(1 mark)** — Sum formula, **(1 mark)** — Output shape, **(2 marks)** — Block diagram.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Auto Indexing Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the default tunnel shape between For and While Loops** — students often write "While Loop output is also auto-indexed by default." This loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention that the input array's dimension-0 size auto-sets N in a For Loop** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this explicit statement.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong tunnel symbol** — use a **solid square** for indexed, **hollow square** for last-value. Wrong symbol = **1 mark lost**.
> 4. **Treating shift registers as auto-indexable** — they are not. Mentioning this distinction can earn a **bonus 1 mark** in part (a) answers.
> 5. **Not specifying zero-based indexing** — always state that $i$ starts from **0**, not 1. This is checked strictly in KTU valuation key.
> 6. **Forgetting the "Append to Array" alternative** — when a While Loop's output is dynamically sized, examiners expect students to know that *Enable Indexing* AND *Resize / Append* both apply.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Auto Indexing**

- **Definition:** Auto Indexing = automatic element-by-element processing at loop tunnels.
- **For Loop:** Both input and output tunnels are **auto-indexed by default**.
- **While Loop:** Input is auto-indexed by default; **output is NOT** (last-value default).
- **Tunnel Symbols:** Solid square = indexed; Hollow square = last-value / disabled.
- **N auto-set rule:** For Loop count terminal $N$ = dimension-0 size of auto-indexed input array.
- **Zero-based indexing:** Iteration index $i$ starts at **0**, ends at $N-1$.
- **Multi-dimensional input:** 2D array → N = rows; each iteration yields a 1D row.
- **Output always 1D:** Auto-indexed output of a For Loop is always **1D**, regardless of body operation.
- **Disable indexing** by right-clicking the tunnel → *Disable Indexing* (used for last-value extraction).
- **Shift registers ≠ auto-indexing:** Shift registers carry **state**, not array slices.
- **Memory:** Auto-indexed output tunnel holds a **buffer of size $N \times s_{element}$** during loop execution.
- **Common KTU question pattern:** "Differentiate For Loop and While Loop auto-indexing default behavior" (asked in nearly every cycle).
- **Worked numerics to memorize:**
  - 1D input length $L$ → 1D output length $L$.
  - 2D input $M \times N$ → $N$ iterations, 1D output length $M$.
- **Always mention** in derivations that **zero-based indexing** applies and the count terminal is **auto-wired** by the indexed input tunnel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Structures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Case Structure** in Virtual Instrumentation (VI) — specifically within the **LabVIEW Graphical Programming Environment (GPE)** developed by National Instruments (NI) — is a fundamental *program execution control structure* that enables **conditional, mutually-exclusive execution of graphical code blocks** based on the value of a single input known as the **Selector Terminal**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Case Structure is a polymorphic conditional branching construct in G-language that contains one or more sub-diagrams (cases). At any given instant, **only ONE case executes** while all others remain dormant, governed strictly by the Boolean/Integer/String/Enum value wired to the selector terminal. The structure operates on a principle called **"Single Case Selection per Execution Cycle"**, making it the direct graphical equivalent of the `switch-case` or `if-else if-else` ladder in textual programming languages such as C, C++, or Java. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Order Counter" Imagine walking into a restaurant with a complex menu. You speak into a microphone and say your order: - **"Burger"** → The Burger station chef prepares your meal. - **"Pizza"** → The Pizza station chef prepares your meal. - **"Salad"** → The Salad station chef prepares your meal. **Crucially:** Only **ONE chef works** on your order. The other chefs wait idle. The "Order" you speak is the **Selector Input**, and each chef's kitchen is a **Case Sub-diagram**. This is exactly how a Case Structure works inside a LabVIEW Block Diagram. ## 1.3 Classification of Case Structures (by Selector Type) | Selector Data Type | Example Selector Values | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Boolean** | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | Binary decisions (ON/OFF, Valid/Invalid) | | **Integer (I8, I16, I32, U8, etc.)** | `0, 1, 2, 3, ...` | Multi-way discrete selection | | **String** | `"Start"`, `"Stop"`, `"Reset"` | State machine command dispatch | | **Enum (Enumerated)** | `State_Idle`, `State_Run` | Typed state machine inputs | | **Error Cluster** | `No Error` / `Error` | Error-handling in modular VIs | > [!NOTE] > **Default Case Mandate:** In KTU lab exams, examiners specifically look for whether students have configured a **Default Case**. A case structure that does not list every possible selector value must have a "Default" case to handle unexpected inputs gracefully — this is a mandatory best practice in robust VI design. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards - **Boolean Logic Threshold (TTL):** $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\text{ V}$ (TRUE), $V_{IL} \leq 0.8\text{ V}$ (FALSE) - **Default Execution Time per Case (on modern CPUs):** **< 1 µs** for simple arithmetic cases - **KTU Standard:** Case Structures are evaluated within a **dataflow execution model** — they execute only when **all required inputs** at the selector terminal and input tunnels are available. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Case Structure — The Operational Breakdown A LabVIEW Case Structure consists of four primary components that work in concert: ### Step 1: The Selector Terminal (Case Selector) - Located on the **left border** of the structure, indicated by a small question mark `?` symbol at the top-left corner. - Accepts **exactly one input wire** of a supported data type. - The wired value determines which sub-diagram is loaded into memory and executed. ### Step 2: Sub-Diagrams (Cases) - Each case is a **distinct, separately scrollable** code window inside the structure. - The structure resembles a "filmstrip" or "stack of transparencies" — you flip between them like cards. - Cases are **labeled** at the top (e.g., `True`, `False`, `0`, `1`, `"Error"`, `"Default"`). ### Step 3: Input Tunnels (Source Data Channels) - Data flowing **into** the structure appears as **input tunnels** on the left edge. - These tunnels supply the **same data** to *every* case sub-diagram simultaneously. - A tunnel is *always* available regardless of which case is executing. ### Step 4: Output Tunnels (Sink Data Channels) - Data flowing **out** of the structure appears as **output tunnels** on the right edge. - Each case sub-diagram **must supply a value** to every output tunnel — this is the **"All Cases Must Provide Data" rule** enforced by LabVIEW's compiler. - A **solid (filled) tunnel** indicates every case has a wired source; a **hollow (white) tunnel** indicates an unwired case (compiler warning). ## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" Behind Execution > [!IMPORTANT] > **WHY** does only one case execute? — Because the Case Structure is a **polymorphic, mutually-exclusive** construct. The compiler guarantees that branching paths do not interfere, preserving the G-language's **deterministic dataflow semantics**. **HOW** it works internally (execution lifecycle): 1. **Tunnel Data Latching:** All input wires are read and held in a temporary buffer. 2. **Selector Evaluation:** The selector terminal's value is read and matched against case labels. 3. **Case Selection:** The matching sub-diagram is identified; if no match, the **Default** case is selected. 4. **Sub-Diagram Execution:** Only the selected sub-diagram's nodes execute; others are skipped. 5. **Output Tunnel Aggregation:** The data from the selected case is passed out through the output tunnels. ## 2.3 The "All Cases Must Provide Data" Rule (A Critical KTU Concept) > [!WARNING] > **Compiler Behavior:** If Case A provides a value to Output Tunnel X, but Case B does NOT, LabVIEW will display a **broken Run Arrow** (a hollow arrow indicates compilation error). The unwired tunnel reads a *default* value (0 for numerics, FALSE for Boolean, empty string for strings), which leads to **silent logic bugs** if the student is unaware. **The Three Tunnel States a KTU Student Must Memorize:** | Tunnel State | Visual Appearance | Meaning | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **Solid/Filled Square** | Filled dark square | All cases wired correctly | ✅ Full marks | | **Hollow/White Square** | Empty white square | At least one case unwired | ⚠️ Partial deduction | | **No Square** | Just a line | Output is unused | ❌ No marks on output | ## 2.4 Default Case — The Safety Net When a Case Structure is configured for **Integer, String, or Enum** selectors, there are *infinitely many* (Integer) or *many* (String) possible selector values. The developer must explicitly handle all expected values. For the rest, a **Default case** acts as the fallback. **Textual Pseudocode Equivalent:** ``` SELECTOR_VALUE = read_input() IF SELECTOR_VALUE == 0: execute_case_0() ELSE IF SELECTOR_VALUE == 1: execute_case_1() ELSE IF SELECTOR_VALUE == 2: execute_case_2() ELSE: execute_default_case() // The "Default" handler ``` ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of Boolean Cases | $N = 2^1 = 2$ | Two cases: TRUE, FALSE | | 2 | Number of Integer-n Cases | $N = n$ (or $n+1$ with Default) | e.g., $n=3$ → cases 0,1,2 | | 3 | Selector Match Condition | $V_{selector} \equiv V_{case\_label}$ | Exact type-equivalent match | | 4 | Tunnel Data Propagation | $D_{out} = f_{selected}(D_{in})$ | Output = selected case's computation | | 5 | Execution Time Bound | $T_{total} = T_{selector} + T_{case} \approx O(1)$ | Constant time dispatch | | 6 | Default Case Necessity | $C_{default} = \text{TRUE}$ if $\vert \text{cases listed} \vert < \vert \text{domain} \vert$ | Required for unbounded domains | | 7 | Error Cluster Selector | Selector = Boolean (first element of cluster) | TRUE = Error path, FALSE = No Error | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Case Structures are the backbone of: - **DAQ (Data Acquisition) State Machines:** Determining whether to Acquire, Log, Display, or Stop based on user input. - **Instrument Communication Protocols:** Decoding SCPI command responses (e.g., `"MEAS:VOLT?"` vs `"MEAS:CURR?"`). - **Industrial PLC Replacement:** Software-defined ladder logic replacement for discrete control tasks. - **Error-Handling Pipelines:** Routing normal flow vs. error flow — a critical KTU 2024 module topic. - **Adaptive Signal Processing:** Choosing filter coefficients based on input frequency band. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Boolean Case Structure (Thermostat Control) **Problem Statement:** A virtual instrument reads a room temperature $T$ (in °C). If $T > 25$, the VI must turn ON a cooling fan; otherwise, turn it OFF. Model the Case Structure logic. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Define the Boolean Selector:** The selector must be a Boolean. We compute: $$S = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } T > 25 \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{if } T \leq 25 \end{cases}$$ This is achieved by wiring the output of a `Greater?` comparator into the selector terminal. **Step 2 — Construct the Case Sub-diagrams:** **Case "TRUE" (Cooling Required):** - Place a `Boolean Constant (TRUE)` on the FP terminal connector. - Wire it to the `Fan Control` output indicator. **Case "FALSE" (Cooling Not Required):** - Place a `Boolean Constant (FALSE)` on the FP terminal connector. - Wire it to the `Fan Control` output indicator. **Step 3 — Verify the "All Cases Must Provide Data" Rule:** The output tunnel `Fan Control` must be wired in BOTH the TRUE case and the FALSE case. The tunnel appears as a **solid filled square**, confirming compliance. **Step 4 — Execution Trace:** Let $T = 30$: $$S = (30 > 25) = \text{TRUE}$$ The TRUE case executes: $$Fan_{out} = \text{TRUE (ON)}$$ Let $T = 20$: $$S = (20 > 25) = \text{FALSE}$$ The FALSE case executes: $$Fan_{out} = \text{FALSE (OFF)}$$ The Case Structure correctly routes control signals based on the threshold. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Integer Case Structure (Menu Dispatcher) **Problem Statement:** Design a Case Structure that takes an integer selector (0, 1, 2, 3) representing a menu choice and produces a corresponding String output. ### Analytical Truth Table: | Selector Value | Case Label | Output String $S_{out}$ | Default Case Used? | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | "Add" | `"Operation Selected: Addition"` | No | | 1 | "Subtract" | `"Operation Selected: Subtraction"` | No | | 2 | "Multiply" | `"Operation Selected: Multiplication"` | No | | 3 | "Divide" | `"Operation Selected: Division"` | No | | 4 or higher | "Default" | `"Invalid Selection"` | **Yes** | ### Step-by-Step Configuration: **Step 1:** Right-click the Case Structure → **"Add Case for Every Value"** (or manually add cases). **Step 2:** Label each case with the integer value: `0`, `1`, `2`, `3`. **Step 3:** Add a **Default** case: right-click the case label area → **"Make This The Default Case"**. **Step 4:** For each case, place a String Constant and wire it to the output tunnel: ``` Case "0" Sub-diagram: [ "Operation Selected: Addition" ] ─┐ Case "1" Sub-diagram: [ "Operation Selected: Subtraction" ] ─┼─→ Output Tunnel Case "2" Sub-diagram: [ "Operation Selected: Multiplication" ] ─┤ Case "3" Sub-diagram: [ "Operation Selected: Division" ] ─┤ Case "Default" Sub-diagram: [ "Invalid Selection" ] ─┘ ``` **Step 5 — Verification via Selector Value 5:** $$S_{selector} = 5 \notin \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$$ $$\therefore S_{out} = \text{"Invalid Selection" (Default case)}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: Error Cluster Case Structure The **Error Cluster** is a special predefined LabVIEW cluster containing three fields: $$E_{cluster} = \{ \text{status (Boolean)}, \ \text{code (I32)}, \ \text{source (String)} \}$$ When wired to a Case Structure's selector terminal, only the **Boolean status** field is used: $$S = E_{cluster}.\text{status} = \begin{cases} \text{FALSE} & \text{(No Error — execute normal path)} \\ \text{TRUE} & \text{(Error — execute error-handling path)} \end{cases}$$ ### Analytical Trace: Suppose a VI reads a file and encounters a missing file: $$E_{cluster} = \{ \text{status} = \text{TRUE}, \ \text{code} = 7, \ \text{source} = \text{"ReadFile.vi"} \}$$ The **TRUE** case executes, where the developer typically places: - A dialog box (`"Error: File Not Found. Abort."`). - Logging the error code to a file. - Setting the `Error Out` cluster to pass downstream. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selector Type vs. Behavior | Property | Boolean | Integer | String | Enum | Error Cluster | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Number of Cases** | 2 | $n$ (user-defined) | $n$ (user-defined) | $n$ (typed) | 2 | | **Default Case Mandatory?** | No | Recommended | Recommended | No (if exhaustive) | No | | **Compiler Strictness** | High | High | High | Highest | High | | **Common Use** | ON/OFF logic | Multi-state | Command dispatch | State machine | Error pipeline | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | Medium | High | Very High | ## 3.5 Performance & Complexity Analysis Let $N$ be the number of cases in a structure. The **time complexity** of selector matching in LabVIEW's internal implementation is approximately: $$T_{match} = O(\log N) \quad \text{(for ordered Integer/Enum)}$$ $$T_{match} = O(1) \quad \text{(for Boolean)}$$ $$T_{match} = O(N) \quad \text{(worst case, String comparison)}$$ **Memory footprint** for an unselected case is **negligible** (code is loaded only when selected), making Case Structures highly memory-efficient compared to always-executing sequences. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Case Structure Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INPUT TUNNELS
Data In 1, Data In 2, Data In 3"] --> B["CASE STRUCTURE
SELECTOR BASED DISPATCH"] B --> C{"Selector
Value Match?"} C -->|Match Case 0| D["CASE 0
Sub-diagram Executes"] C -->|Match Case 1| E["CASE 1
Sub-diagram Executes"] C -->|Match Case N| F["CASE N
Sub-diagram Executes"] C -->|No Match Found| G["DEFAULT CASE
Fallback Sub-diagram"] D --> H["OUTPUT TUNNELS
Aggregated Result"] E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I["DOWNSTREAM VI
Continues Dataflow"] ``` ## 4.2 Dataflow Through a Case Structure (Selector Lifecycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["START: Selector Terminal
Receives Input Value"] --> S2{"Data Type
Validation"} S2 -->|Boolean| S3a["Compare to TRUE/FALSE Labels"] S2 -->|Integer| S3b["Compare to Integer Labels"] S2 -->|String| S3c["Compare to String Labels"] S2 -->|Enum| S3d["Compare to Enum Type Tags"] S3a --> S4{"Match Found?"} S3b --> S4 S3c --> S4 S3d --> S4 S4 -->|YES| S5["Load Matching Sub-diagram
into Execution Context"] S4 -->|NO| S6["Load DEFAULT Sub-diagram
if configured"] S5 --> S7["Execute Internal Nodes
in Dataflow Order"] S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8["Output Tunnel Writes
to Downstream Wires"] S8 --> S9["END: Continue Dataflow"] ``` ## 4.3 Tunnel Configuration — Input vs Output ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT_SIDE["LEFT EDGE - INPUT TUNNELS"] IT1["In1
Source Data"]:::inputStyle IT2["In2
Source Data"]:::inputStyle IT3["In3
Source Data"]:::inputStyle end subgraph CASE_BODY["CASE STRUCTURE BODY"] CB["Active Sub-diagram
(only one executes)"]:::caseStyle end subgraph OUTPUT_SIDE["RIGHT EDGE - OUTPUT TUNNELS"] OT1["Out1
Must be wired in EVERY case"]:::outputStyle OT2["Out2
Must be wired in EVERY case"]:::outputStyle end IT1 --> CB IT2 --> CB IT3 --> CB CB --> OT1 CB --> OT2 classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef caseStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Chart — Case vs Sequence Structures ```mermaid flowchart TB Q{"Programming Need?"} -->|Conditional
Execution| QS["CASE STRUCTURE
Mutually Exclusive
One case runs at a time"] Q -->|Sequential
Execution| QSEQ["SEQUENCE STRUCTURE
Ordered Execution
All frames run in order"] QS --> QS1["IF ELSE equivalent
SWITCH CASE equivalent"] QSEQ --> QSEQ1["Forces execution order
Eliminates dataflow ambiguity"] style QS fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style QSEQ fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.5 State Machine Pattern (Case Structure as a Core Component) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> State_Init : Start State_Init --> State_Acquire : User Input Acquire State_Acquire --> State_Process : Data Ready State_Process --> State_Display : Processing Done State_Display --> State_Acquire : Continuous Mode State_Display --> State_Stop : User Input Stop State_Stop --> [*] : VI Terminates State_Init : INIT CASE State_Acquire : ACQUIRE CASE State_Process : PROCESS CASE State_Display : DISPLAY CASE State_Stop : STOP CASE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each state label (e.g., `State_Acquire`) corresponds to a **Case sub-diagram** in the LabVIEW implementation. A **While Loop** wraps around the Case Structure, and a **Shift Register** holds the next state value, creating the classic **LabVIEW State Machine Architecture** — a frequently tested KTU 2024 concept. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Case Structure in LabVIEW. List any TWO data types that can be wired to its selector terminal. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A Case Structure is a conditional execution control structure in LabVIEW that contains one or more sub-diagrams (cases). At any given moment, **only one case executes** based on the value wired to its selector terminal. **Two data types accepted by the selector terminal:** 1. **Boolean Data Type:** Selects between two cases (TRUE and FALSE). Used for binary logic. 2. **Integer Data Type (I8, I16, I32, U8, etc.):** Selects among multiple numbered cases (0, 1, 2, ..., n). Used for multi-way branching. *(Acceptable alternatives: String, Enum, Error Cluster — award 1.5 marks each.)* ### Question 2: What is the significance of the "Default Case" in a Case Structure? Why is it recommended for Integer/String selectors? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Default Case** is a special fallback sub-diagram that executes when the selector value does not match any of the explicitly defined case labels. **Significance and Recommendation:** 1. **Robustness:** Integer selectors have an infinite domain (..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...). Without a Default case, an unexpected input value would leave the structure with no valid sub-diagram to execute, causing undefined behavior. 2. **Error Prevention:** The Default case acts as an "else" branch, allowing the developer to handle unforeseen conditions gracefully (e.g., log a warning, set a default output). 3. **Compiler Compliance:** While not strictly mandatory for Integer/String selectors, the Default case is a **best practice** in industrial VI design and is specifically checked for in KTU lab evaluations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Case Structure Mechanics **(a)** Explain the internal architecture of a LabVIEW Case Structure with a labeled block diagram. List the FOUR main components and describe the role of the **Selector Terminal**. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** A Case Structure in LabVIEW is a graphical control flow construct that allows conditional, mutually-exclusive execution of sub-diagrams. Its internal architecture consists of **four primary components**: **Component 1 — Selector Terminal:** - Located on the **top-left border** of the structure, marked with a small `?` icon. - Accepts a **single input wire** of a supported data type (Boolean, Integer, String, Enum, or Error Cluster). - The value on this terminal determines which case sub-diagram is loaded and executed. - It does NOT pass data through the structure — it is purely a **routing mechanism**. **Component 2 — Case Sub-Diagrams (Cases):** - Each case is an independent, scrollable code window within the structure. - Cases are **labeled** with their matching selector value (e.g., `True`, `0`, `"Start"`). - The active case displays its label at the top of the structure; non-active cases are hidden. **Component 3 — Input Tunnels:** - Located on the **left edge** of the structure. - Bring data INTO the structure from upstream code. - The same input data is available to **every** sub-diagram (latched at structure entry). **Component 4 — Output Tunnels:** - Located on the **right edge** of the structure. - Send data OUT of the structure to downstream code. - The **"All Cases Must Provide Data" rule** mandates that every sub-diagram must wire a value to each output tunnel. Otherwise, the LabVIEW compiler raises an error (broken Run Arrow). **Block Diagram (Reference):** ``` [Selector Input Wire] │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ CASE STRUCTURE │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Sub-diagram for case │ │ │ │ matching selector │ │ │ │ value │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ [Output Wires] ``` **(b)** Design a Case Structure for a temperature monitoring VI that classifies the input temperature $T$ (in °C) into three categories: **"COLD"** if $T < 15$, **"NORMAL"** if $15 \leq T \leq 30$, and **"HOT"** if $T > 30$. Show the configuration, the case labels, and the output logic. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Selector Construction:** Use an **Integer selector** derived from a comparison cascade: $$S = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } T < 15 \\ 1 & \text{if } 15 \leq T \leq 30 \\ 2 & \text{if } T > 30 \end{cases}$$ This can be implemented using nested `Less Than?`, `Greater Than?`, and `Select` functions, OR by using a **Boolean selector chained with sub-structures** (nested Case Structures). **Step 2 — Case Structure Configuration:** | Case Label | Input Condition | Output String | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | `0` | $T < 15$ | `"COLD"` | | `1` | $15 \leq T \leq 30$ | `"NORMAL"` | | `2` | $T > 30$ | `"HOT"` | | `Default` | Out-of-range (e.g., invalid sensor) | `"INVALID READING"` | **Step 3 — Sub-Diagram Contents (for Integer selector method):** - **Case "0" sub-diagram:** Place a String Constant `"COLD"` → wire to output tunnel. - **Case "1" sub-diagram:** Place a String Constant `"NORMAL"` → wire to output tunnel. - **Case "2" sub-diagram:** Place a String Constant `"HOT"` → wire to output tunnel. - **Default sub-diagram:** Place a String Constant `"INVALID READING"` → wire to output tunnel. **Step 4 — Trace Verification:** For $T = 10$ °C: $$S = 0 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Output} = \text{"COLD"} \quad \checkmark$$ For $T = 22$ °C: $$S = 1 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Output} = \text{"NORMAL"} \quad \checkmark$$ For $T = 35$ °C: $$S = 2 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Output} = \text{"HOT"} \quad \checkmark$$ For $T = -100$ °C (sensor fault): $$S \notin \{0, 1, 2\} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Output} = \text{"INVALID READING"} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Advanced Case Structure Applications **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how a **Case Structure is used in an Error-Handling Pipeline** in LabVIEW. What is the role of the **Error Cluster** in this context? **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Error Cluster Definition:** An Error Cluster in LabVIEW is a predefined cluster containing three data fields: $$E_{cluster} = \{ \text{status (Boolean)}, \ \text{code (I32)}, \ \text{source (String)} \}$$ **Role in Case Structure Selector:** When an Error Cluster is wired to the Selector Terminal of a Case Structure, LabVIEW automatically extracts the **Boolean `status`** field and uses it as the case discriminator: $$S = E_{cluster}.\text{status}$$ **Diagram of the Error-Handling Pipeline:** ``` [Previous VI Subroutine] → [Error Out Wire] │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ CASE STRUCTURE │ │ ┌──────────┐ ┌────────┐ │ │ │ FALSE │ │ TRUE │ │ │ │ No Error │ │ Error │ │ │ │ Path │ │ Path │ │ │ └──────────┘ └────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ │ ▼ ▼ [Continue Normal] [Display Dialog: Processing] "Error Detected"] ``` **Working Mechanism:** - **FALSE Case (No Error):** The "No Error" sub-diagram executes the normal processing logic. For example, it continues reading data, performing calculations, or invoking the next VI in the chain. - **TRUE Case (Error):** The "Error" sub-diagram executes the error-handling logic. This typically includes: 1. A **dialog box** informing the user of the error. 2. **Logging** the error code and source to a file. 3. **Bypassing** normal processing to prevent cascading failures. 4. **Passing the Error Cluster** downstream unchanged. **Key Advantage:** This pattern is highly modular and prevents "error blindness" — a common failure mode in unhandled VI chains. **(b)** Compare **Case Structure**, **Sequence Structure**, and **Flat Sequence Structure** in LabVIEW. State TWO advantages of using a Case Structure over a `For Loop` for menu-selection logic. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Case Structure | Stacked Sequence | Flat Sequence | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Execution Order** | Conditional (one case only) | Sequential (top to bottom) | Sequential (left to right) | | **Dataflow Independence** | Yes (one case at a time) | Forced order | Forced order | | **Use Case** | Branching, state machines | Forced sub-VI order | Forced sub-VI order | | **Default Available?** | Yes | No | No | | **Code Reusability** | High (per case) | Medium | Medium | | **Memory Efficiency** | High (only active case loaded) | Lower (all frames loaded) | Lower (all frames loaded) | **Two Advantages of Case Structure over For Loop for Menu Selection:** 1. **Intent Clarity and Readability:** A Case Structure explicitly maps each selector value to a specific sub-diagram, making the VI self-documenting. A For Loop with a `Select` function or array indexing obscures the menu-to-action mapping and requires additional comments for clarity. 2. **Memory and Computational Efficiency:** A Case Structure loads and executes **only the active sub-diagram**, whereas a For Loop iterates over its body a fixed number of times. For large, complex menu logic, a Case Structure is more memory-efficient because non-selected cases are not evaluated. 3. **Default Case Support:** A Case Structure natively supports a Default case for unhandled inputs, while a For Loop requires an `Index Array` with bounds checking — adding complexity. 4. **Scalability:** Adding a new menu option in a Case Structure is a one-step operation (add a new case), while a For Loop requires modifying the array constant, the loop count, and the indexing logic. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Case Structure Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Default Case (–1 to –2 marks):** When asked to design an Integer or String Case Structure, students often omit the Default case. Examiners expect a complete, robust design. Always include a Default case unless explicitly told otherwise. > > 2. **Unwired Output Tunnel (–2 marks):** If a sub-diagram does not wire a value to an output tunnel, the LabVIEW block diagram is broken. Examiners verify this on paper; show explicit wiring in your answer. > > 3. **Confusing Case Structure with Sequence Structure (–3 marks):** A Case Structure is for *conditional* execution; a Sequence Structure is for *forced-order* execution. Mixing these up in a question costs heavily. > > 4. **Not Mentioning "Mutually Exclusive" Execution (–1 mark):** The defining property of a Case Structure is that only ONE case runs. Always state this explicitly. > > 5. **Selector Data Type Mismatch (–2 marks):** Wiring a Boolean selector to a case labeled `"Start"` will cause a runtime error. Always ensure the data type of the selector matches the case label type. > > 6. **Missing the "All Cases Must Provide Data" Rule (–1 mark):** If a 7-mark question asks for design, explicitly mention this rule in your explanation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Case Structures" (Module 3):** > > ✅ A **Case Structure** executes **only ONE sub-diagram** at a time, based on the value at the **Selector Terminal**. > > ✅ Supported selector data types: **Boolean, Integer, String, Enum, Error Cluster**. > > ✅ The **Default Case** is the fallback that executes when no label matches — **mandatory for Integer/String** selectors. > > ✅ **Input Tunnels** (left edge) provide the same data to every sub-diagram. > > ✅ **Output Tunnels** (right edge) require **ALL cases** to wire a value — "All Cases Must Provide Data" rule. > > ✅ **Boolean selectors** produce exactly 2 cases (TRUE, FALSE). > > ✅ **Error Cluster selectors** use the Boolean `status` field internally. > > ✅ Case Structures are the building block of **State Machines** in LabVIEW. > > ✅ Case Structure ≠ Sequence Structure. Case = conditional branching; Sequence = forced order. > > ✅ **Time complexity** of case selection: $O(1)$ for Boolean, $O(\log N)$ for ordered Integer, $O(N)$ worst case for String. > > ✅ In KTU exams, always draw **clear labeled block diagrams** — this carries 2-3 marks by itself. > > ✅ Mention **"mutually exclusive execution"** and **"dataflow model"** in your definitions for full marks. > > ✅ Use **nested Case Structures** for complex multi-variable conditions (Boolean + Integer combinations). > > ✅ **Tunnel State Memory:** Output tunnels can be configured to pass data through unselected cases if needed (advanced topic — not always required for KTU). ---

topics.title: Sequence Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sequence Structures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Sequence Structure** in Virtual Instrumentation (specifically in the **LabVIEW** graphical programming environment, the de-facto standard tool for KTU's OERUT833 syllabus) is a **structural programming node** used on the Block Diagram to enforce a strict, deterministic order of execution among code segments (frames) that would otherwise be governed only by the natural data-flow paradigm of LabVIEW. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** *A Sequence Structure is a graphical structure that contains one or more sub-diagrams (called "frames") which execute sequentially in a guaranteed order, irrespective of data availability, thereby overriding the inherent parallel data-flow execution model of Virtual Instruments.* There are two official variants defined by National Instruments (NI) / the LabVIEW reference architecture: | Type | Visual Appearance on Block Diagram | Data Transfer Mechanism | |------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------| | **Flat Sequence Structure** | A single horizontal film-strip with frames side-by-side | Sequence Local terminals (right edge of each frame) | | **Stacked Sequence Structure** | A stack of frames (like a deck of cards) | Sequence Local terminals connecting between frames | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chef preparing a recipe** in a kitchen. The recipe has four steps: 1. Boil water 2. Add pasta 3. Wait 8 minutes 4. Drain and serve In a **real LabVIEW data-flow model** (no sequence structure), the chef would only start step 2 once the *output* of step 1 (boiled water) was ready — true to the natural dependencies. However, sometimes the chef needs an *artificial* step that does **not depend on data**, e.g., *"turn on the kitchen light"* before step 1. The light has no physical data dependency, but logically must come first. A **Sequence Structure is exactly that "ordering knob"** — it tells LabVIEW, *"Hey, run this frame FIRST, that frame SECOND, and so on, even if no data wire connects them."* ## 1.3 Why Sequence Structures Are Needed in Virtual Instrumentation In data-flow programming, nodes execute as soon as **all their inputs are available**. This is a feature, but it can become a problem in hardware- or time-sensitive VI tasks: - Initializing a **DAQ device** must occur *before* acquiring a sample. - Closing a **file reference** must occur *after* the write is complete. - Resetting a **counter** must occur *before* starting a new count. Without a sequence structure, you cannot **guarantee** which operation will run first when there is no data dependency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** LabVIEW is fundamentally a **data-flow language**, not a procedural (C/Java) language. Sequence Structures are the *only* sanctioned way to force execution order without resorting to artificial data dependencies. This is a favorite two-mark question in KTU exams. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Timing Considerations - **Frame Number Indexing:** Begins at **0** (not 1) in both Flat and Stacked sequence structures. The first frame on the strip / on top of the stack is **Frame 0**. - **Maximum Frames (Practical):** Up to **~4 billion** frames technically supported, but KTU exam scenarios use **2 to 5 frames**. - **Execution Time per Frame:** Governed by the longest-running node inside that frame. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OERUT833 syllabus explicitly lists *"For Loop, While Loop, Case Structure, Flat Sequence Structure, Stacked Sequence Structure, and Formula Node"* under Module 3 (Programming Structures). Treat Sequence Structures as a **core, exam-mandatory** sub-topic. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The LabVIEW Data-Flow Execution Model (Background) LabVIEW uses a **directed data-flow execution model**, which can be summarized in three axioms: 1. A node executes **only when data is available at all of its input terminals**. 2. A node supplies data to **all of its output terminals** when it finishes executing. 3. Nodes that have no data dependency between them **can execute in parallel** (LabVIEW is inherently multithreaded). **Problem:** When two operations have *no data dependency* but *must* occur in a specific order, Axiom 3 violates the developer's intent. Sequence Structures are the official solution. ## 2.2 Flat Sequence Structure — Operational Logic The **Flat Sequence Structure** displays all frames in a single row, side-by-side, on the block diagram. Execution proceeds strictly from **left to right (Frame 0 → Frame 1 → … → Frame N-1)**. ### Step-by-step Execution Logic - **Step 1:** Place a Flat Sequence Structure on the block diagram (Functions Palette → Programming → Structures → Flat Sequence). - **Step 2:** Right-click the structure border → **"Add Frame After"** or **"Add Frame Before"** to expand the number of frames. - **Step 3:** Place nodes **inside** each frame as you would on the bare block diagram. - **Step 4 (Optional — Sequence Local):** Right-click the *right edge* of a frame → **"Add Sequence Local"** to pass data from that frame to *all subsequent* frames to its right. - **Step 5:** Run the VI. Frames execute in the *visible left-to-right order*, regardless of any internal data wiring between frames. ### Key Properties of the Flat Sequence Structure - **Visual Transparency:** All frames are visible simultaneously → easier to debug. - **No Memory Overhead:** Frames are simply reordered; the compiler generates equivalent sequential code. - **Sequence Local Scope:** A Sequence Local terminal created on Frame *k* is **readable** by all frames *k+1, k+2, …* (one-way data flow: earlier → later). - **No Backward Data Transfer:** You cannot pass data *from* a later frame *to* an earlier frame using Sequence Locals (this is a **common KTU pitfall** — see Section 5 warning). ## 2.3 Stacked Sequence Structure — Operational Logic The **Stacked Sequence Structure** displays only **one frame at a time** on the block diagram (the "top" frame), with a frame selector at the top border. It mimics a **film strip or deck of cards**. ### Step-by-step Execution Logic - **Step 1:** Place a Stacked Sequence Structure (Functions Palette → Programming → Structures → Stacked Sequence). - **Step 2:** Right-click the frame border → **"Add Frame Before"** or **"Add Frame After"**. - **Step 3:** Use the **frame selector** (the small drop-down at the top of the structure) to navigate between frames in the diagram view. - **Step 4:** Use **Sequence Local terminals** (right-click the structure border) to pass data between frames. - **Step 5:** When the VI runs, LabVIEW executes **Frame 0 first**, then **Frame 1**, and so on. ### Key Properties of the Stacked Sequence Structure - **Space Efficiency:** Only one frame is visible at a time, saving block-diagram real estate. - **Sequence Local Terminals:** Placed on the *left border* of any frame. They act as **output terminals from earlier frames** and **input terminals to current and later frames**. - **Disadvantage:** Hidden frames can lead to *invisible bugs* during debugging — a known KTU examiner complaint. ## 2.4 Comparative Analysis: Flat vs. Stacked | Feature | Flat Sequence Structure | Stacked Sequence Structure | |---------|-------------------------|----------------------------| | **Visible frames** | All visible at once | Only one (selected) visible | | **Diagram space usage** | High (wide) | Low (compact stack) | | **Debugging ease** | Easier | Harder (must scroll frames) | | **Data transfer direction** | Left-to-right only | Top-to-bottom (Frame 0 → N-1) | | **Sequence Local terminal** | Right edge of source frame | Left edge of destination frame | | **Recommended for** | Simple 2–3 frame tasks, tutorials | Many frames (>3), compact VIs | | **Best Practice (NI recommendation)** | ✅ Preferred in modern LabVIEW | ⚠️ Discouraged for new code | ## 2.5 Sequence Local Terminals — Deep Rules A **Sequence Local** is a special terminal on the border of a sequence frame used to pass data from that frame to **all later frames**. Critical rules: - The terminal that **sources** the data appears on the **right edge** (Flat) or **left edge** (Stacked) of the originating frame. - The terminal that **receives** the data appears on the **left edge** (Flat) or **left edge** (Stacked) of every subsequent frame. - The source terminal is shown as a **solid filled triangle**; the receiving terminals are shown as **hollow (unfilled) triangles**. - The data type is **strictly typed** at creation (you wire a wire of a specific type, and it propagates to all later frames). - The **value is read** at the moment the receiving frame *starts* executing (not when the source frame finished — a subtle but important timing detail). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** A common exam trick asks *"Can data flow from Frame 2 to Frame 1 using Sequence Locals?"* The correct answer is **NO**. Sequence Local data flow is **strictly unidirectional** from lower-indexed to higher-indexed frames. ## 2.6 When NOT to Use Sequence Structures (Best Practices) The NI LabVIEW development guidelines (which KTU's official LabVIEW CORE 1 / CORE 2 references follow) recommend: - **Use data flow** whenever a *natural* data dependency exists — do not artificially add sequence structures. - **Use Stacked → Flat migration** if you have many frames; flat is preferred for readability. - **Replace sequence structures with state machines** for complex control flow (this is Module 4/5 territory in KTU's syllabus). - **Avoid sequence structures in loops** unless absolutely necessary — they can introduce latency. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Item | Description | Typical Value / Range | |---------------|-------------|----------------------| | $N_{frames}$ | Number of frames in a sequence structure | 2 – 5 (typical) | | $T_{total}$ | Total execution time of the structure | $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} T_k$ | | $T_k$ | Execution time of the *k*-th frame | Determined by slowest node in frame | | $f_{idx}$ | Frame index | $0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ | | $\text{SL}_{src}$ | Sequence Local source terminal | Right edge (Flat) / Left edge (Stacked) | | $\text{SL}_{dst}$ | Sequence Local destination terminal | Available on all frames $k_{dst} > k_{src}$ | | Data type | Sequence Local data type | Strictly typed at wire connection | **Total execution time of a Sequence Structure:** $$ T_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{N_{frames}-1} T_k $$ where $T_k$ is the wall-clock execution time of the slowest node in frame $k$. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Equation Matters:** Unlike parallel data-flow code, sequence structures do **not** benefit from parallel execution. The total VI execution time for a sequence is the **sum** of per-frame times, not the **maximum**. This is a frequently tested KTU conceptual question. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial DAQ:** Sequence a device *init* → *config* → *acquire* → *stop* operation deterministically. - **Automated Test Equipment (ATE):** Ensure a calibration step occurs **before** a measurement step. - **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Simulation:** Set initial conditions in Frame 0, run simulation in Frame 1, log results in Frame 2. - **File I/O Operations:** Open file (Frame 0) → Write (Frame 1) → Close (Frame 2) — a textbook KTU example. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive worked examples** that match the analytical depth expected in KTU 14-mark problems. Every algebraic or logical step is made explicit. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Timing Analysis of a Flat Sequence Structure ### Problem Statement A Flat Sequence Structure contains **3 frames** with the following per-frame node execution times: - **Frame 0:** A SubVI takes **120 ms**; a numeric constant operation takes **5 ms** (in parallel inside the frame). - **Frame 1:** A single waveform generation node takes **85 ms**. - **Frame 2:** A file write SubVI takes **60 ms**; a DAQ stop node takes **40 ms** (in parallel). Compute the **total execution time** of the sequence structure. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify per-frame durations.** Inside a single frame, nodes with no data dependency *can* run in parallel. Therefore, the per-frame duration is governed by the **longest single node** in that frame (not the sum). **Step 2 — Compute $T_0$ (Frame 0).** $$ T_0 = \max(120 \text{ ms}, 5 \text{ ms}) = 120 \text{ ms} $$ > *Textual reasoning:* The numeric constant is essentially instantaneous; the SubVI dominates. **Step 3 — Compute $T_1$ (Frame 1).** $$ T_1 = 85 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $T_2$ (Frame 2).** $$ T_2 = \max(60 \text{ ms}, 40 \text{ ms}) = 60 \text{ ms} $$ > *Textual reasoning:* The DAQ stop and the file write can run in parallel; the file write takes longer. **Step 5 — Apply the total-execution-time formula.** $$ T_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{2} T_k $$ $$ T_{total} = T_0 + T_1 + T_2 $$ $$ T_{total} = 120 \text{ ms} + 85 \text{ ms} + 60 \text{ ms} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{total} = 265 \text{ ms}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to **sum every node's time within every frame** (i.e., $T_0 = 120 + 5 = 125$ ms). This is wrong because data-flow parallelism inside a frame still applies. Always use the **maximum** within a frame, the **sum across frames**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Sequence Local Data-Flow Logic ### Problem Statement A Stacked Sequence Structure has **4 frames** (Frame 0, 1, 2, 3). A Sequence Local is created on **Frame 1** with the source value `x = 10`. Determine which frames can **read** this value, and provide a justification. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Recall the unidirectionality rule.** Sequence Local data flows from **lower-indexed frames to higher-indexed frames** only. There is **no** backward flow. **Step 2 — Identify the source frame.** The source is on **Frame 1** ($k_{src} = 1$). **Step 3 — Identify eligible destination frames.** A destination terminal is accessible on any frame $k_{dst}$ such that $k_{dst} > k_{src}$. $$ k_{dst} \in \{ k \in \mathbb{Z} \mid k > 1 \text{ and } 0 \le k \le 3 \} $$ $$ k_{dst} \in \{ 2, 3 \} $$ **Step 4 — Frame-by-frame accessibility table.** | Frame | Index $k$ | Can read Sequence Local? | Reasoning | |-------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------| | Frame 0 | 0 | ❌ No | $k < k_{src}$ (no backward flow) | | Frame 1 | 1 | ❌ No (source) | Source terminal only; not a destination | | Frame 2 | 2 | ✅ Yes | $2 > 1$ | | Frame 3 | 3 | ✅ Yes | $3 > 1$ | **Step 5 — State the final answer.** $$ \boxed{\text{Frames 2 and 3 can read the value } x = 10.} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Identifying the Correct Frame Order ### Problem Statement The following tasks must occur in a specific order for a hardware test VI: - **(A)** Initialize the DAQ device. - **(B)** Configure the analog input channel. - **(C)** Acquire 1000 samples. - **(D)** Write the acquired data to a TDMS file. - **(E)** Reset and close the DAQ device. Map these tasks to a **3-frame** Flat Sequence Structure (you may combine related tasks into a single frame) and justify the ordering. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Determine logical dependencies.** - (A) Initialize → must precede (B) Config. - (B) Config → must precede (C) Acquire. - (C) Acquire → must precede (D) Write. - (D) Write → must precede (E) Reset/Close. **Step 2 — Group into 3 frames.** | Frame | Tasks Included | Justification | |-------|----------------|---------------| | **Frame 0** | A (Initialize) + B (Configure) | Both are *setup* operations; no acquired data yet, can run as parallel nodes inside the same frame. | | **Frame 1** | C (Acquire) + D (Write) | Once data is acquired, it can be written in the same frame using a wire dependency; saves a frame. | | **Frame 2** | E (Reset/Close) | Final cleanup; must run **last** so all other operations are complete. | **Step 3 — Verify the global order is preserved.** $$ \text{Frame 0: } A \to B \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Frame 1: } C \to D \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Frame 2: } E $$ **Step 4 — Confirm correctness.** The required logical order $A \to B \to C \to D \to E$ is preserved: ✅ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Quick-Reference) | Criterion | No Sequence Structure (Pure Data-Flow) | Flat Sequence Structure | Stacked Sequence Structure | |-----------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------| | Execution order | Unpredictable without data deps | Guaranteed left→right | Guaranteed top→bottom | | Parallelism | Maximum (multi-core friendly) | None (sequential) | None (sequential) | | Memory | Lowest | Slightly higher (intermediate state) | Same as Flat | | Debugging | Easy (visible) | Easy (all visible) | Hard (one frame visible) | | Best use case | Independent parallel operations | 2–3 step deterministic tasks | 4+ step space-constrained tasks | | KTU exam frequency | Medium | **Very High** | High | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section provides **architectural block-level diagrams** rendered in Mermaid syntax to visualize the operational flow of both Flat and Stacked Sequence Structures, and their inter-frame data transfer mechanisms. ## 4.1 Flat Sequence Structure — Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef frame fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef slocal fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 F0["FRAME 0
Initialize DAQ"]:::frame F1["FRAME 1
Configure Channel"]:::frame F2["FRAME 2
Acquire 1000 Samples"]:::frame F3["FRAME 3
Write TDMS File"]:::frame F4["FRAME 4
Reset and Close DAQ"]:::frame SL1["Sequence Local
DAQ Ref"]:::slocal SL2["Sequence Local
Channel Config"]:::slocal SL3["Sequence Local
Acquired Data"]:::slocal OUT["Final Output
Status Code"]:::output F0 -- "DAQ handle" --> SL1 SL1 -. "read by F1-F4" .-> F1 F1 -- "Channel cfg" --> SL2 SL2 -. "read by F2-F4" .-> F2 F2 -- "Waveform data" --> SL3 SL3 -. "read by F3-F4" .-> F3 F3 --> F4 F4 --> OUT ``` **Reading the diagram:** Frames execute **strictly left-to-right**. Sequence Locals (orange diamonds) pass data **forward only** to all subsequent frames. ## 4.2 Stacked Sequence Structure — Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef frame fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sel fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef slocal fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 SEL["Frame Selector
Index 0 to N-1"]:::sel F0["FRAME 0
Open File Reference"]:::frame F1["FRAME 1
Generate Waveform"]:::frame F2["FRAME 2
Write to File"]:::frame F3["FRAME 3
Close and Error Check"]:::frame SL1["Sequence Local
File Ref"]:::slocal SL2["Sequence Local
Waveform Data"]:::slocal SL3["Sequence Local
Error Cluster"]:::slocal SEL -- "selects frame to view" --> F0 SEL -- "selects frame to view" --> F1 SEL -- "selects frame to view" --> F2 SEL -- "selects frame to view" --> F3 F0 -- "out" --> SL1 SL1 -. "to F1, F2, F3" .-> F1 F1 -- "out" --> SL2 SL2 -. "to F2, F3" .-> F2 F2 -- "out" --> SL3 SL3 -. "to F3" .-> F3 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Only **one frame is visible** at a time (controlled by the Frame Selector). The Stacked Sequence Structure mimics a "deck of cards" — execution is top-to-bottom (Frame 0 → Frame 3). ## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef start fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef endnode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef warn fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["Need deterministic
execution order?"]:::start B{"Natural data
dependency exists?"}:::decision C["Use pure
data-flow
(no sequence)"]:::endnode D{"Number of
frames needed?"}:::decision E["Flat Sequence
Structure
(2-3 frames)"]:::endnode F["Stacked Sequence
Structure
(>3 frames)"]:::endnode G["Consider
State Machine
(Module 4-5)"]:::warn A --> B B -- "Yes" --> C B -- "No" --> D D -- "1-3" --> E D -- "4+" --> F D -- "Variable /
user-driven" --> G ``` ## 4.4 Sequence Local Terminal — Symbol Legend ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef source fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dest fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef frame fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 FR["FRAME k
(source frame)"]:::frame SRC["Filled Triangle
SOURCE TERMINAL"]:::source DST1["Hollow Triangle
DESTINATION in Frame k+1"]:::dest DST2["Hollow Triangle
DESTINATION in Frame k+2"]:::dest FR -- "data wire" --> SRC SRC -. "data propagates" .-> DST1 SRC -. "data propagates" .-> DST2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap This section contains **board-exam-pattern** questions with complete model solutions, structured exactly per the KTU 2024 scheme (Part A: 3 marks, Part B: 14 marks with internal choice). --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Part A — Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **Q: Define a Sequence Structure in LabVIEW. Mention its two types.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Sequence Structure** is a programming structure in LabVIEW used to enforce a specific, deterministic order of execution among code segments (called *frames*) on the block diagram, thereby overriding the default data-flow execution order. The **two types** are: 1. **Flat Sequence Structure** — displays all frames side-by-side in a single row. 2. **Stacked Sequence Structure** — displays frames in a stacked, deck-of-cards arrangement, with only one frame visible at a time. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining both types.)* --- ### Part A — Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **Q: What is a Sequence Local terminal? Can data flow backward using it?** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Sequence Local terminal** is a special terminal placed on the border of a sequence frame that allows data to be passed from that frame to **all subsequent** frames in the structure. **No, data cannot flow backward** using a Sequence Local. The data flow is **strictly unidirectional** — from a lower-indexed frame to all higher-indexed frames. For example, in a Stacked Sequence Structure, Frame 1 cannot read a Sequence Local created on Frame 2. *(Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for the unidirectional rule.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] > **Q: (a) Explain the operational difference between a Flat Sequence Structure and a Stacked Sequence Structure in LabVIEW. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with suitable diagrams.** *(7 marks)* > > **Q: (b) A VI uses a Flat Sequence Structure with 4 frames. The internal node timings are:** > **Frame 0:** Node A (50 ms) and Node B (30 ms) in parallel. > **Frame 1:** Node C (75 ms). > **Frame 2:** Node D (40 ms) and Node E (60 ms) in parallel. > **Frame 3:** Node F (90 ms) and Node G (20 ms) in parallel. > **Calculate the total execution time of the sequence structure.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution — (a):** **Operational Difference:** - **Flat Sequence Structure:** All frames are visible side-by-side in a single row on the block diagram. Execution proceeds from **left to right (Frame 0 → Frame 1 → …)**. The developer can see and edit every frame simultaneously, which improves debugging but consumes significant horizontal space on the block diagram. - **Stacked Sequence Structure:** Frames are stacked vertically (like a deck of cards). Only **one frame is visible at a time**, controlled by the frame selector (a small dropdown at the top border). Execution proceeds from **top to bottom**. This saves diagram space but makes debugging more difficult as the developer must scroll between frames. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Flat Sequence Structure | Stacked Sequence Structure | |--------|--------------------------|------------------------------| | Visual layout | All frames in a row | Frames stacked; one visible at a time | | Space usage | High (wide) | Low (compact) | | Debugging | Easy | Hard | | Data transfer | Sequence Local on right edge | Sequence Local on left edge | | Best suited for | Few frames (2–3) | Many frames (>3) | | NI recommendation | Preferred | Discouraged for new development | **Diagram (textual representation):** **Flat:** `[ Frame 0 ] → [ Frame 1 ] → [ Frame 2 ] → [ Frame 3 ]` **Stacked:** ``` ┌──────────────┐ │ Frame 0 │ ← visible ├──────────────┤ │ Frame 1 │ ├──────────────┤ │ Frame 2 │ ├──────────────┤ │ Frame 3 │ └──────────────┘ ▲ Frame Selector ``` **Model Solution — (b):** **Step 1 — Compute per-frame duration using the max-rule inside a frame.** **Frame 0:** $$ T_0 = \max(50 \text{ ms}, 30 \text{ ms}) = 50 \text{ ms} $$ **Frame 1:** $$ T_1 = 75 \text{ ms} $$ **Frame 2:** $$ T_2 = \max(40 \text{ ms}, 60 \text{ ms}) = 60 \text{ ms} $$ **Frame 3:** $$ T_3 = \max(90 \text{ ms}, 20 \text{ ms}) = 90 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the total execution time formula.** $$ T_{total} = T_0 + T_1 + T_2 + T_3 $$ $$ T_{total} = 50 + 75 + 60 + 90 $$ $$ \boxed{T_{total} = 275 \text{ ms}} $$ --- ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] > **Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain how a Stacked Sequence Structure is used to coordinate a hardware initialization and shutdown procedure in a Virtual Instrument. Use Sequence Local terminals to pass the DAQ device reference between frames.** *(7 marks)* > > **Q: (b) Discuss the best practices and pitfalls of using Sequence Structures in LabVIEW. When should a developer avoid using a sequence structure?** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution — (a):** **Stacked Sequence Structure for DAQ Init/Shutdown:** A **Stacked Sequence Structure** with **3 frames** is ideal for a hardware-controlled VI because it keeps the block diagram compact while enforcing strict execution order. **Frame 0 — Hardware Initialization:** - Open a DAQ device reference (using `DAQmx Create Virtual Channel.vi`). - Set the sample rate and input range. - **Source Sequence Local** `DAQ_Ref` is placed on the **left edge** of Frame 0 to export the reference. **Frame 1 — Data Acquisition:** - Start the acquisition task. - Read 1000 samples into a waveform. - **Source Sequence Local** `Waveform_Data` is placed on the **left edge** of Frame 1. **Frame 2 — Shutdown:** - Stop the acquisition task. - Clear the DAQ task. - **Read** the `DAQ_Ref` Sequence Local to ensure the correct reference is being closed. - Display any errors. **Frame 3 — Logging (optional):** - Write the `Waveform_Data` to a TDMS file. - Pass the file path via a Sequence Local created in Frame 0. **Architectural Diagram (textual):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ FRAME 0: Initialize │ │ • DAQmx Open Task │ │ • Configure Channel │ │ • → [SL: DAQ_Ref] │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ FRAME 1: Acquire │ │ • DAQmx Read │ │ • → [SL: Waveform_Data] │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ FRAME 2: Shutdown │ │ • [SL: DAQ_Ref] → Stop Task │ │ • [SL: Waveform_Data] → Save │ │ • [SL: Error] → Display │ └─────────────────────────────────┘ ▲ Frame Selector ``` **Execution Order:** Frame 0 → Frame 1 → Frame 2 (deterministic, guaranteed). **Model Solution — (b):** **Best Practices:** 1. **Use sequence structures only when natural data flow is insufficient.** If a clear data wire already enforces order, do not add a sequence structure. 2. **Prefer Flat over Stacked** for readability and ease of debugging. 3. **Keep frames small and focused** on a single logical operation. 4. **Use descriptive labels** on the frame border (right-click → *"Visible Items → Label"*) to document the purpose of each frame. 5. **Limit the total number of frames** — if you need more than 4–5, consider a **State Machine** architecture (covered in later modules). 6. **Pass data via Sequence Locals** instead of using global variables, to maintain scope discipline. **Pitfalls:** 1. **Assuming parallel execution inside a structure** — frames are strictly sequential, even if marked as "parallel" (this is a feature, not a bug, of the structure). 2. **Trying to pass data backward** — Sequence Local data flow is unidirectional. 3. **Using Stacked structures with hidden bugs** — errors in a non-visible frame can be hard to detect. 4. **Overusing sequence structures** can hide the natural data-flow architecture of the VI, making the code harder to refactor. **When to Avoid Sequence Structures:** - When the operations are **truly independent** and can benefit from parallel execution on multi-core CPUs. - When the VI has a **large number of states or transitions** — use a State Machine instead. - When the ordering can be **expressed naturally via data-flow wiring** (e.g., wire the output of a SubVI to the input of the next). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **"Sum ALL node times within a frame" mistake:** Many students incorrectly add *all* node durations inside a frame. **Correct rule:** use the *maximum* within a frame (because data-flow parallelism still applies inside a frame); use the *sum* across frames. > 2. **"Sequence Locals can flow backward" mistake:** Sequence Local data flow is **strictly forward** (lower-index → higher-index). The reverse is **impossible** without using global variables. > 3. **"Sequence structure makes code parallel" mistake:** The structure *removes* parallelism between frames. Do not write in the exam that sequence structures "improve" multi-threading. > 4. **"Frame indexing starts at 1" mistake:** Frames are indexed from **0**, not 1. This off-by-one error is a common 1-mark deduction. > 5. **"Stacked is just a 'compact' Flat" mistake:** Although functionally similar, Stacked has a frame-selector mechanism and the Sequence Local terminal placement differs. Examiners may deduct marks for conflating them. > 6. **"Sequence structure replaces the While Loop" mistake:** Sequence structures do **not** loop. They execute exactly once per call, in linear order. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - ✅ A **Sequence Structure** forces deterministic execution order on the LabVIEW block diagram, overriding the data-flow model. - ✅ Two types: **Flat Sequence Structure** (side-by-side) and **Stacked Sequence Structure** (deck-of-cards, one frame visible at a time). - ✅ Frames are indexed starting from **0**. - ✅ Frames execute **strictly sequentially**; there is no parallelism between frames. - ✅ Inside a single frame, nodes with no data dependency **can still run in parallel** — use the **maximum** node duration for the frame's time. - ✅ Total execution time = **sum** of per-frame durations. - ✅ **Sequence Local terminals** pass data from one frame to **all subsequent** frames. - ✅ Sequence Local data flow is **strictly unidirectional** (lower-indexed → higher-indexed frames). No backward flow. - ✅ Prefer **Flat** over **Stacked** for new development (NI recommendation). - ✅ Avoid using sequence structures when **natural data-flow dependencies** already exist. - ✅ For complex state-driven logic, use a **State Machine** (Module 4/5), not a long sequence structure. - ✅ Sequence structures do **not loop** — they execute once per call. - ✅ Typical KTU 3-mark question topics: definition, two types, Sequence Local behavior. - ✅ Typical KTU 14-mark question topics: timing analysis (sum of max-per-frame), frame ordering, comparison Flat vs. Stacked, debugging pitfalls.

topics.title: Customizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Customizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: CUSTOMIZING VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Official Terminology):**
> In Virtual Instrumentation (specifically LabVIEW — the de-facto standard platform for VI taught under KTU), **"Customizing"** refers to the systematic modification of a Virtual Instrument's (VI) **User Interface (Front Panel)**, **Source Code (Block Diagram)**, and **Execution Environment (VI Properties)** to match specific application requirements, user preferences, and deployment constraints. It encompasses visual styling, runtime behavior configuration, property-based runtime control, and user-defined menu/toolbar development.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Apartment" Metaphor

Imagine you just bought a **brand-new apartment** (the basic VI created from a template). The walls are white, doors swing only one way, and the kitchen has a fixed menu. Now, you decide to:
- **Paint the walls** → *Customizing colors of controls/indicators*
- **Replace the swing door with a sliding door** → *Customizing Boolean mechanical action*
- **Add a smart lock that you can open from your phone** → *Property Nodes (runtime customization)*
- **Set the apartment to "Airplane Mode" (no maintenance noise) when guests are over** → *VI Properties → Window Appearance*
- **Create a custom menu in the kitchen** → *Run-Time Menu Editor*

**Customizing = the act of transforming a generic VI into a polished, user-specific, deployment-ready instrument.**

### 🧩 Three Pillars of Customization

| Pillar | What You Customize | KTU 2024 Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Front Panel Customization** | Colors, fonts, sizes, alignment, scale formatting, Boolean actions | High (40%) |
| **2. VI & Environment Customization** | VI Properties, Window Appearance, Run-Time options, Menu Editor | High (45%) |
| **3. Programmatic Customization** | Property Nodes, Invoke Nodes, Control References | Medium (15%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Emphasizes Customization (2024 Scheme Outcome Mapping):**
> - Maps to **CO3**: "Develop customized VIs for specific measurement and control tasks."
> - Aligns with **Bloom's Level: Apply / Create** — students must *apply* properties to *create* deployable VIs.
> - Tested frequently in **Part A (3 marks)** as short definitions, and in **Part B (14 marks)** as "Customize a given VI to..." style problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2️⃣ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Front Panel Object Customization

### 🎨 A. Visual Customization
When you right-click any Front Panel object → **Properties**, you get a dialog with multiple tabs:

1. **Appearance Tab** — Color, size, font, visibility, enable/disable state, label position.
2. **Scale Tab** — Min, max, increment, logarithmic mapping (for charts/graphs).
3. **Display Format Tab** — Floating point precision, time format, scientific notation.
4. **Documentation Tab** — Description, tip text (visible at runtime).
5. **Data Binding Tab** — Source/Network connection (Advanced).

### ⚙️ B. Boolean Control Mechanical Actions (HIGH-YIELD for KTU!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **mechanical action** defines **how a Boolean control behaves** when clicked. KTU frequently asks to "draw the timing diagram" for these actions.

The **6 standard mechanical actions** are:

| # | Action Name | Click Behavior | Release Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Switch When Pressed** | Value toggles immediately | — | Light switches |
| 2 | **Switch When Released** | — | Value toggles on release | Door bells |
| 3 | **Switch Until Released** | Value = TRUE while held | Value reverts to FALSE on release | Push-to-talk |
| 4 | **Latch When Pressed** | Value = TRUE, latched | Stays TRUE until read once by code | Stop buttons |
| 5 | **Latch When Released** | — | Value = TRUE on release, latched until read | Emergency stops |
| 6 | **Latch Until Released** | Value = TRUE while held | Stays TRUE until read once | One-shot triggers |

> [!NOTE]
> The **three "Latch"** actions automatically reset to FALSE **after the Block Diagram reads them once** (this is critical for KTU problems).

### 📐 C. Numeric Indicator Scale Customization
For numeric controls/indicators, the **Scale tab** allows:
- **Mapping mode**: Linear, Logarithmic (base 10 or e).
- **Scale range**: Minimum, Maximum, Increment.
- **Scale style**: Show as slider, knob, or simple numeric box.
- **Mapping function** (log scale): $y = a \cdot \log_{10}(x) + b$

### 🖋️ D. String & Path Controls
- **Display Modes**: Normal, '\' Codes, Password, Hex.
- **Scrollbar** visibility and orientation.
- **Justification**: Left, Center, Right.

---

## 2.2 VI Properties — The Customization Powerhouse

Press **`File → VI Properties`** (or `Ctrl+I`) to access **10+ categories** of VI-level customization. The most KTU-relevant ones are:

| Category | What It Customizes | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **General** | VI name, icon, description, revision number | Documenting a measurement system |
| **Window Appearance** | Title bar, menu bar, scrollbar, toolbar, resizing | **Kiosk-style deployed VIs** (frequently asked!) |
| **Window Size** | Default size, run-time sizing rules | Forcing fixed display on a panel PC |
| **Execution** | Reentrancy, priority, allow debugging, auto-error handling | Determinism for control systems |
| **Print Options** | Headers, footers, scaling, margins | Lab report generation |
| **Documentation** | VI description and help context | Knowledge transfer in teams |

### 🎯 Window Appearance (Most KTU-Relevant Sub-Page)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Window Appearance** page offers **3 preset dialog styles** for the Front Panel:

| Preset | Title Bar | Menu Bar | Scrollbar | Toolbar | Resizable |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Top-Level Application Window** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Dialog** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Default** (Standard) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Custom** | User-defined | User-defined | User-defined | User-defined | User-defined |

➡️ The **"Custom"** option unlocks all customization — this is the KTU board's favourite!

### ⏱️ Execution Properties (Critical for Real-Time VI)

| Property | Function | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Reentrancy** | Non-reentrant (default) / Shared clone / Preallocated clone | Allows multiple simultaneous calls of a subVI |
| **Priority** | 0 (lowest) to 9 (highest) — Time-critical to Background | Affects OS scheduling |
| **Auto Error Handling** | Pause on error / Suspend / None | Required for unattended VIs |
| **Allow Debugging** | Yes/No | Required for probe/breakpoint usage |

---

## 2.3 Runtime Customization via Property Nodes & Invoke Nodes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property Nodes** = Read/Write **properties** of a control/indicator **at runtime** (e.g., change color when value exceeds a threshold).
> **Invoke Nodes** = **Execute a method** (action) on a control/indicator at runtime (e.g., Reinitialize to Default).

### How Property Nodes Work

1. Right-click a terminal on the Block Diagram → **Create → Property Node**.
2. The node has **terminals** (top = write, bottom = read) for individual properties.
3. Examples: `Visible`, `Disabled`, `Blinking`, `Value`, `Color`, `Position`, `Bounds**.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Property Node Math

The most important mathematical relationship in customized VIs is the **logarithmic scale mapping** used for sensor calibration:

$$
x_{\text{displayed}} = x_{\text{min}} + \left( x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}} \right) \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(R) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})}{\log_{10}(R_{\text{max}}) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = raw input value
- $R_{\text{min}}, R_{\text{max}}$ = raw scale endpoints
- $x_{\text{min}}, x_{\text{max}}$ = display scale endpoints

For the **Boolean latching logic** (Latch When Pressed), the implicit equation is:

$$
\text{Output}(t) = \text{TRUE} \quad \text{while} \quad \text{Button Pressed} \cup (\text{Output} = \text{TRUE} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Block Diagram not yet read it})
$$

After **one read cycle**, $\text{Output} \leftarrow \text{FALSE}$ automatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Logarithmic customization is used in **sound-level meters (dB scale)**, **RF power measurements (dBm)**, and **vibration sensors** — all standard KTU lab applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3️⃣ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Linear Scale to Logarithmic for a Numeric Indicator

This is a common **14-mark KTU problem**: "A pressure transducer outputs 100 Pa to 10,000 Pa. Display the value on a 0 to 100 dB scale with logarithmic mapping."

### Step 1 — Identify the raw and display ranges

Given:
- $R_{\text{min}} = 100$ Pa
- $R_{\text{max}} = 10000$ Pa
- $x_{\text{min}} = 0$ dB
- $x_{\text{max}} = 100$ dB

### Step 2 — Apply the normalized logarithmic formula

$$
x_{\text{displayed}} = x_{\text{min}} + (x_{\text{max}} - x_{\text{min}}) \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(R) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})}{\log_{10}(R_{\text{max}}) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(R_{\text{min}}) &= \log_{10}(100) = 2 \\
\log_{10}(R_{\text{max}}) &= \log_{10}(10000) = 4 \\
\Rightarrow \; \Delta_{\log} &= 4 - 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{displayed}} &= 0 + (100 - 0) \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(R) - 2}{2} \\
