&= 50 \cdot \left( \log_{10}(R) - 2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with a test point

Take $R = 1000$ Pa (geometric mean, should map to 50 dB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 50 \cdot (\log_{10}(1000) - 2) \\
  &= 50 \cdot (3 - 2) \\
  &= 50 \cdot 1 = 50 \;\text{dB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — LabVIEW Implementation Note

In LabVIEW, the **Logarithmic Mapping checkbox** in `Properties → Scale` automatically applies this formula when the user sets the **Scale Range (Mapped)** endpoints. The VI internally uses the formula derived above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This same 5-step derivation is used in **NI LabVIEW's "Logarithmic Scale.vi"** which is an open-source reference for custom scale design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Boolean Latch Behavior — Timing Analysis

This derivation is the **most-tested Part A 3-marker** in KTU 2024 ESE for this module.

### Problem Setup
A "Stop" button uses **Latch When Pressed** mechanical action. The VI contains a `While Loop` with the button wired to its **conditional terminal**.

### Step-by-Step Boolean Trace

Let $P(t)$ = Button Pressed state, $R(t)$ = Button Read state by the loop, $B(t)$ = Boolean Value stored.

$$
B(t) = \begin{cases}
\text{TRUE} & \text{if } P(t) = \text{TRUE} \\
\text{TRUE} & \text{if } B(t^-) = \text{TRUE} \;\text{AND}\; R(t^-) = 0 \quad \text{(latched, not yet read)} \\
\text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise (default and after one read)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Truth Table (Time Step | Pressed | Read | Stored Value | Loop Action)

| t (s) | P(t) | R(t) | B(t) | Loop Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0 | 0 | FALSE | Running |
| 0.5 | 1 | 0 | TRUE | Still running (TRUE condition) |
| 1.0 | 0 | 0 | TRUE | Latched — still TRUE |
| 1.2 | 0 | 1 | **FALSE** | Loop reads & exits ✅ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse "Latch When Pressed" with "Switch When Pressed". The latching is what allows the **VI to detect a quick button press even if the loop is sleeping** — the value stays TRUE until read.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Customization

| If Your Goal Is... | Use This Customization | KTU Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| Change button color when value crosses limit | **Property Node** (Color property) | 7 |
| Disable a control during a critical operation | **Property Node** (Disabled property) | 7 |
| Re-run the VI with default values | **Invoke Node** (Reinit to Default) | 7 |
| Hide the menu bar in a deployed kiosk VI | **VI Properties → Window Appearance → Custom** | 14 |
| Convert a number to dB display | **Numeric Properties → Scale → Log Mapping** | 14 |
| Build a custom right-click menu | **Menu Editor (Runtime Shortcut Menus)** | 14 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4️⃣ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Layers of VI Customization — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["LAYER 1: Front Panel Object Customization"]
    nodeB["Color, Font, Size, Alignment"]
    nodeC["Boolean Mechanical Actions"]
    nodeD["Numeric Scale Log Mapping"]
    nodeE["String Display Modes"]

    nodeF["LAYER 2: VI Level Customization"]
    nodeG["VI Properties Dialog"]
    nodeH["Window Appearance Presets"]
    nodeI["Execution Properties Reentrancy"]
    nodeJ["Run Time Menu Editor"]

    nodeK["LAYER 3: Programmatic Customization"]
    nodeL["Property Nodes Read Write"]
    nodeM["Invoke Nodes Execute Method"]
    nodeN["Control References Local Variable"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeN
```

## 4.2 Window Appearance Customization State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DefaultPreset
    DefaultPreset --> DialogPreset: User selects dialog
    DefaultPreset --> ToplevelPreset: User selects top level
    DefaultPreset --> CustomMode: User clicks Customize

```


> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In Part B answers, draw a **layered block diagram** (UI layer → VI layer → Code layer) and label each customization you made. This fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5️⃣ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define "Window Appearance" in the context of VI customization. List its three preset styles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Window Appearance** is a tab in the **VI Properties dialog** that controls the Front Panel's runtime visual interface — specifically the visibility of the title bar, menu bar, scroll bar, toolbar, and the resizing behavior. The three preset styles are:
> 1. **Top-Level Application Window** — shows all bars and allows resizing (full IDE-like experience).
> 2. **Dialog** — minimal interface with only a title bar; used for pop-up dialogs.
> 3. **Default** — title bar + scrollbar + toolbar (no menu bar), partial customization.
> 4. **Custom** — user-defined combination of all five elements.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three presets with one-line purpose.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a "Property Node" and an "Invoke Node" in LabVIEW.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Property Node | Invoke Node |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Reads or writes an **attribute** of an object | **Executes an action/method** on an object |
| **Direction** | Get (read) or Set (write) | One-way execution |
| **Example** | `Color`, `Visible`, `Value`, `Disabled` | `Reinitialize to Default`, `Get Image` |
| **Returns** | A value (Boolean, Numeric, Cluster) | Often no return value, or status Boolean |

*(Award: 1.5 marks per aspect.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With a neat sketch, explain the **six Boolean mechanical actions** of a Boolean control in LabVIEW. Discuss which one is most appropriate for a "Stop Button" in a data-acquisition loop and justify your answer. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A thermistor outputs a non-linear resistance from 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ corresponding to a temperature range of -20 °C to 120 °C. Design a customized Front Panel **numeric indicator** with **logarithmic scale mapping** to display the temperature. Provide the derived formula and block diagram. **(7 marks)***

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Enumerate the six actions with timing diagrams.

| # | Action | Press Behavior | Release Behavior | Reset |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switch When Pressed | Toggles | — | Manual |
| 2 | Switch When Released | — | Toggles on release | Manual |
| 3 | Switch Until Released | TRUE while held | FALSE on release | Automatic |
| 4 | Latch When Pressed | TRUE + Latched | Latched until read | After 1 read |
| 5 | Latch When Released | — | TRUE + Latched on release | After 1 read |
| 6 | Latch Until Released | TRUE while held | TRUE + Latched after release | After 1 read |

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Sketch the timing diagrams (pulse train showing P/R/V signals for each).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For a "Stop Button":
- **Best choice: Latch When Pressed.**
- **Justification:** It guarantees that even a quick press (e.g., 50 ms) is detected by the While Loop's conditional terminal because the Boolean value remains TRUE until read. It also auto-resets to FALSE, so the next run of the VI starts with a clean state. A "Switch When Pressed" would require the user to press *twice* to stop and reset.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the parameters.
- $R_{\text{min}} = 1000$ Ω, $R_{\text{max}} = 100000$ Ω
- $T_{\text{min}} = -20$ °C, $T_{\text{max}} = 120$ °C

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the log-mapping formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{displayed}} &= T_{\text{min}} + (T_{\text{max}} - T_{\text{min}}) \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(R) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})}{\log_{10}(R_{\text{max}}) - \log_{10}(R_{\text{min}})} \\
&= -20 + 140 \cdot \frac{\log_{10}(R) - 3}{5 - 3} \\
&= -20 + 70 \cdot \left( \log_{10}(R) - 3 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Verify at $R = 1000$ Ω: $T = -20 + 70 \cdot 0 = -20$ °C ✅
At $R = 100000$ Ω: $T = -20 + 70 \cdot 2 = 120$ °C ✅
At $R = 10000$ Ω (geometric mean): $T = -20 + 70 \cdot 1 = 50$ °C ✅

**Step 4 (2 marks):** LabVIEW implementation:
1. Place a **Thermistor Indicator (Numeric)**.
2. Right-click → **Properties → Scale tab**.
3. Check **"Logarithmic Mapping"**.
4. Set **Scale Range (Mapped):** Min = -20, Max = 120.
5. Set **Scale Range (Unmapped):** Min = 1000, Max = 100000.
6. In the block diagram, the **Divide** function with log inputs and **Add/Subtract** blocks realize the formula.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Block diagram sketch.

```
[R_raw input] --> [Log10] --> [Subtract log10 Rmin] --> [Divide by deltalog]
              --> [Multiply by Trange] --> [Add Tmin] --> [Numeric Indicator with Log Scale]
```

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Explain the **VI Properties dialog** in LabVIEW. List any **five categories** of properties and their purpose. Which category is essential for deploying a "Kiosk-Mode" VI and why? **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Design a customized VI's **Run-Time Menu** for a Temperature Monitoring System with three top-level menus: File, View, and Help. Each menu must have at least two items. Show the Menu Editor structure and the Event Structure that handles the menu selection. **(7 marks)***

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define VI Properties:
> The VI Properties dialog (File → VI Properties or Ctrl+I) is a multi-tabbed dialog that allows the developer to configure VI-level settings: name, icon, window appearance, execution priority, reentrancy, documentation, and printing options.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Five categories:

| Category | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **General** | VI name, icon library, revision number, last modified date |
| **Window Appearance** | Configure title bar, menu bar, toolbar, scrollbar, resizing |
| **Window Size** | Default + minimum + run-time size policy |
| **Execution** | Reentrancy, priority (0–9), allow debugging, auto error handling |
| **Documentation** | VI description, help tag, context-sensitive help window |
| **Print Options** | Headers, footers, scaling, margins for print-to-PDF |
| **Security** | Password protection for block diagram (locked VIs) |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Kiosk-Mode answer:
- **Essential Category: Window Appearance → Custom.**
- **Why:** A kiosk-mode VI must hide the menu bar (no Ctrl+R), hide the toolbar (no Run button for users), disable resizing (fixed panel PC display), and keep only the title bar. This is achievable only via the **"Custom" preset**, where each of the 5 UI elements can be individually toggled.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Run-Time Menu Editor structure.

| Top-Level Menu | Submenu Item 1 | Submenu Item 2 | Submenu Item 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **File** | Open Log File | Save Data | Exit |
| **View** | Show Graph | Show Statistics | — |
| **Help** | About | User Manual | — |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Saving and linking.
1. Open **Menu Editor** (Edit → Run-Time Menu).
2. Build the menu tree as above.
3. Save as `temp_monitor.rtm`.
4. In the VI, on the Block Diagram, use **`Menu → CurrentVI's MenuBar`** property set to the `.rtm` file at startup.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Event Structure block diagram.

```
[While Loop]
  --> [Event Structure with Timeout = 100ms]
        Case: "Menu Selection (User)"
           --> Case Selector: "File;Open Log File"
                --> Open/Create File
                --> Read CSV
                --> Display in Table
           --> Case: "File;Save Data"
                --> Write to Spreadsheet File
           --> Case: "File;Exit"
                --> Stop Loop = TRUE
           --> Case: "View;Show Graph"
                --> Property Node: Waveform Chart.Visible = TRUE
           --> Case: "View;Show Statistics"
                --> Property Node: Numeric Indicator.Visible = TRUE
           --> Case: "Help;About"
                --> Display Dialog Box
        Case: Timeout
           --> Acquire Temperature from DAQ
```

**Block diagram description (1 mark):** A `While Loop` contains an `Event Structure` with cases for each menu item, an "Abort" button (latched) at the conditional terminal, and data-acquisition inside the Timeout case.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Customization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Property Node direction (READ vs WRITE):** In a Property Node, terminals on **TOP** are **WRITE inputs**, terminals on **BOTTOM** are **READ outputs**. Reversing this is a -2 mark error.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Auto-Reset" of Latch actions:** Students write in exams that "Latch When Pressed requires the user to press twice" — **WRONG**. It auto-resets after **one read by the Block Diagram**. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing Scale Mapping types:** When asked for logarithmic scale, students set the *Numeric Format* to "Scientific Notation" instead of checking the **"Logarithmic Mapping"** checkbox in the **Scale tab**. (-3 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to save the `.rtm` file:** Run-time menu answers are incomplete without specifying the `.rtm` filename and the property node used to load it. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram sketch:** In 14-mark questions, the block diagram is worth **at least 4 marks**. Writing only the formula without showing the wiring loses substantial marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Customization** = modifying UI + VI environment + runtime behavior of a VI.
- ✅ Three layers: **Front Panel Objects** | **VI Properties** | **Property/Invoke Nodes**.
- ✅ **Six Boolean mechanical actions** — know the difference between **Switch** (manual) and **Latch** (auto-reset after one read).
- ✅ **Window Appearance** has **3 presets** (Top-Level, Dialog, Default) + **1 Custom** mode — Custom is the KTU-favourite.
- ✅ **Property Node** = read/write an **attribute**; **Invoke Node** = execute a **method**.
- ✅ **Logarithmic scale** formula: $y = y_{\min} + (y_{\max}-y_{\min}) \cdot \frac{\log R - \log R_{\min}}{\log R_{\max} - \log R_{\min}}$.
- ✅ **Reentrancy**: Default = non-reentrant. For parallel calls, set to **Shared clone** or **Preallocated clone**.
- ✅ **Run-Time Menu Editor** saves as `.rtm` files; loaded via **CurrentVI's MenuBar** property.
- ✅ **Execution priority** ranges 0 (background) → 9 (time-critical).
- ✅ **Auto Error Handling** should be **disabled** in deployed VIs (use explicit error clusters).
- ✅ **Color customization** can be done statically (right-click → Properties) or dynamically (Property Node).
- ✅ **Disabled state** on a control: $0$ = enabled, $1$ = disabled, $2$ = disabled & greyed out.
- ✅ **Decorations** (raised/flat box, line, arrow) are non-functional UI elements for grouping.
- ✅ **Split Run / Cloning a VI** is part of subVI customization — use it to create **re-entrant** versions.
- ✅ Always press **Ctrl+I** to access VI Properties — the **single most-tested dialog** in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Hidden shortcut**: Right-click on a numeric control → **Visible Items → Digital Display** toggles a 7-segment-style display.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** When a 14-mark question says "Customize a VI for ...", structure your answer in **three sections**: (1) Front Panel changes (Properties dialog), (2) VI Properties (Window/Execution), (3) Block Diagram enhancements (Property/Invoke nodes). This systematic layout almost guarantees 12+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timed Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timed Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⏱️ MODULE 3 — TIMED STRUCTURES IN VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Timed Structures* are advanced execution structures in graphical data-flow programming environments (specifically **LabVIEW**) that allow the developer to execute a block of code (a sub-diagram) at **precise, deterministic, and configurable time intervals**, while simultaneously monitoring and controlling timing parameters such as **period, deadline, priority, and offset** at runtime.

In LabVIEW, there are two principal members of the Timed Structures family:

1. **Timed Loop (TL)** — An iterative loop structure with a configurable time base, similar in shape to a `For` or `While` loop, but with built-in high-resolution timing hardware/software clock control.
2. **Timed Sequence (TS)** — A flat-sequence-like structure where each frame executes deterministically at a defined time, used when **multiple sub-diagrams must run in a guaranteed sequential timing order**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Precision Metronome"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Imagine a **chefs kitchen timer that beeps every 5 seconds**, and each time it beeps, the chef must stir the pot. If the chef is slow, two choices exist: either (a) **skip the missed beat** and resume normal rhythm (Discard mode), or (b) **make up for it** by stirring two pots in rapid succession (Process Missed mode). A Timed Loop is *exactly this kitchen timer* — it ticks at a precise period, executes the sub-diagram, and gives you strict policy rules about what to do if the work over-runs the time slot (the *deadline*).

The **Timed Loop** is implemented using a high-resolution **OS timer** (in LabVIEW: the `millisecond tick count`, `µs tick count`, or dedicated **Real-Time OS timer** when used on RT targets). It is the cornerstone of **deterministic, time-critical VIs** such as:

- Data acquisition with **fixed sample rates**
- **Control loops** in real-time systems (PID, model-predictive control)
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** simulation
- **Signal generation** at exact frequencies
- **Multirate DAQ** with synchronized analog/digital I/O

### 🎯 Key Configuration Parameters (the Six Timed Loop "Knobs")

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Period** | $T$ | Time between two consecutive loop iterations (ms / µs) |
| 2 | **Priority** | $P$ | Execution priority (0 = lowest, 100 = highest) on RT targets |
| 3 | **Deadline** | $D$ | Maximum time allowed for one iteration to complete |
| 4 | **Offset** | $O$ | Time delay before the *first* iteration starts |
| 5 | **Mode** | $M$ | Behaviour when a deadline is missed (Discard / Process / Suspend) |
| 6 | **Timeout** | $\tau$ | Time to wait for the loop to start before giving up |

> [!TIP]
> The **Right Data Node** of the Timed Loop returns real-time diagnostics for every iteration: actual period, jitter, iteration count, missed deadlines, and processor utilisation. This makes it the most powerful *observability* tool for any real-time VI.

---

## 2. Why Timed Structures and not a simple "While Loop + Wait (ms)"?

A regular `While Loop` with a `Wait (ms)` block uses the **OS coarse-grained scheduler** with typical resolution of **~15 ms** on Windows, and is subject to **jitter** and **priority inversion**. Timed Structures in contrast:

- Use the **high-resolution OS timer** (down to **1 µs** on RT targets).
- Provide **feedback on every timing parameter** (jitter, deadline violations).
- Allow **dynamic reconfiguration** of period/priority *at runtime*.
- Enforce a **strict deadline** so the system never silently lags.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Timed Loop — Internal Architecture

A Timed Loop consists of four key UI elements on the block diagram:

1. **Left-side Input Node (Configuration Cluster)** — receives the timing parameters *at the start* of each iteration.
2. **Loop Body (sub-diagram)** — the user code executed every period.
3. **Right-side Data Output Node** — returns timing diagnostics *after* the iteration completes.
4. **Conditional Terminal (i)** — the loop counter, same as a `For`/`While` loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Execution Semantics (Step-by-step):**
> 1. At iteration start, the Input Node is read.
> 2. The structure **sleeps** for the specified *Period* $T$ (modified by *Offset* for the first iteration).
> 3. The sub-diagram is **invoked** on the configured priority thread.
> 4. The execution is monitored against the *Deadline* $D$.
> 5. If the sub-diagram finishes before $D$, the loop sleeps for the **remaining** fraction of the period.
> 6. On the next tick, diagnostics are written to the Right Data Node.
> 7. If the sub-diagram *exceeds* $D$, the **Mode** policy $M$ is invoked.

## 2.2 The Timed Sequence — Internal Architecture

A **Timed Sequence** is essentially a **Flat Sequence Structure** with one critical enhancement: each frame can be assigned a **start time** (delay from the previous frame). Execution is **strictly sequential** — frame 2 cannot start before frame 1 completes AND the configured frame delay elapses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Use Case:** When you need to run a startup procedure where:
> - **Frame 0:** Initialize hardware (must finish within 100 ms)
> - **Frame 1:** Calibrate sensor (must wait 50 ms after frame 0)
> - **Frame 2:** Begin acquisition loop

A flat sequence cannot *guarantee* the inter-frame delay under OS jitter — a Timed Sequence can.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Period** | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | s, ms, µs | $f_s$ = sampling rate |
| 2 | **Sampling frequency** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | Inverse of period |
| 3 | **Offset (1st iteration delay)** | $t_{start,0} = t_0 + O$ | s | $t_0$ = loop start time |
| 4 | **n-th iteration start time** | $t_{start,n} = t_{start,0} + n \cdot T$ | s | Deterministic scheduling |
| 5 | **Deadline constraint** | $t_{exec} \le D$ | s | $t_{exec}$ = iteration execution time |
| 6 | **Jitter** | $J = \vert T_{actual} - T_{ideal} \vert$ | s | Should be $\ll T$ |
| 7 | **CPU utilisation** | $U = \dfrac{t_{exec}}{T} \times 100\%$ | % | Should be < 70% for safety |
| 8 | **Missed deadline count** | $N_{miss} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mathbb{1}\{t_{exec,n} > D\}$ | count | Indicator of overload |
| 9 | **Frame delay (Timed Sequence)** | $\Delta t_{f,k} = t_{start,k} - t_{end,k-1}$ | s | Inter-frame pause |
| 10 | **Max sustainable rate** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{exec,WCET}}$ | Hz | $WCET$ = worst-case execution time |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING (KTU Examiner):**
> The *vertical bar* for absolute value / magnitude is **NOT** to be confused with *conditional probability*. In KTU scripts, always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$, never just $\vert x$ — students routinely lose marks for ambiguous notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU testing (HIL):** Tire-pressure sensors, engine knock detection, and airbag firing tests must be sampled at $f_s = 1 \text{ kHz} \pm 0.1\%$. A Timed Loop guarantees this determinism.
- **Medical instrumentation:** ECG sampling at 500 Hz with $J < 50 \,\mu s$ is mandated by IEC 60601-2-25.
- **Process control:** PID control loops on RT-FPGA targets run at $T = 1$–$10$ ms with hard real-time deadlines.
- **RF/Communications:** Baseband I/Q signal generation at $f_s = 100$ MS/s with sub-µs jitter.
- **Structural health monitoring:** Vibration analysis at 10 kHz on SHM sensors over 24/7.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Deterministic Start Time of the n-th Iteration

> **Goal:** Show that the n-th iteration of a Timed Loop starts at a mathematically exact, jitter-free time.

**Given:**
- Loop is initialised at absolute time $t_0$.
- Configured period is $T$ (constant per iteration).
- Configured offset is $O$.

**Step 1 — Define the start time of the *first* iteration.**
By definition of the offset parameter, the structure does not begin executing until $O$ time-units have passed since $t_0$.

$$
t_{start,0} = t_0 + O
$$

**Step 2 — The structure then waits *one full period* before triggering the second iteration.**

$$
t_{start,1} = t_{start,0} + T = t_0 + O + T
$$

**Step 3 — Generalising for the n-th iteration (mathematical induction).**

Assume the statement holds for iteration $k$:

$$
t_{start,k} = t_0 + O + k \cdot T
$$

For iteration $k+1$:

$$
t_{start,k+1} = t_{start,k} + T
$$

Substituting the induction hypothesis:

$$
t_{start,k+1} = (t_0 + O + k \cdot T) + T
$$

$$
t_{start,k+1} = t_0 + O + (k+1) \cdot T
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion: deterministic recurrence relation.**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{start,n} = t_0 + O + n \cdot T, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots\,}
$$

This is a *closed-form* solution and is the formal proof that a Timed Loop is **deterministic** — every iteration start time is computable *a priori*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Sampling Frequency from Period (and vice versa)

**Given:** Period $T$ in seconds.

**Step 1 — By definition, frequency is the reciprocal of the period:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply unit conversion.** If $T$ is given in **milliseconds**:

$$
T_{[s]} = \frac{T_{[ms]}}{1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_{[s]}} = \frac{1000}{T_{[ms]}} \;\;\;[\text{Hz}]
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A DAQ card must sample an audio signal. The user configures a Timed Loop with $T = 0.1\,\text{ms}$. Compute the sampling rate.

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}} = \frac{1}{10^{-4}\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_s = 10\,000\;\text{Hz} = 10\;\text{kHz}\,}
$$

This is the standard **CD-quality oversampling rate** used in professional audio acquisition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — CPU Utilisation and Real-Time Budget

> **Engineering Reality:** A real-time system is *overloaded* if the loop body consumes more time than is available in one period.

**Step 1 — Define the average execution time per iteration.**

$$
\bar{t}_{exec} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} t_{exec,n}
$$

**Step 2 — CPU utilisation is the ratio of busy time to total available time.**

$$
U = \frac{\bar{t}_{exec}}{T} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging, we obtain the maximum safe execution time for a given $U$ budget.**

$$
\bar{t}_{exec,max} = U_{budget} \cdot T
$$

**Step 4 — If $\bar{t}_{exec} > T$ the system is *unsustainable* and the loop will *always* miss deadlines.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (RT Systems):**
> Always reserve at least **30% headroom** ($U \le 70\%$) for the OS scheduler, interrupts, and worst-case OS jitter spikes. This is the classical *Liu & Layland* utilisation bound for hard real-time systems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Jitter Analysis and the Deadline-Mode Decision

**Step 1 — Define actual period measured in the $n$-th iteration.**

$$
T_{actual,n} = t_{start,n+1} - t_{start,n}
$$

**Step 2 — Define ideal period (configured value).**

$$
T_{ideal} = T
$$

**Step 3 — Jitter is the absolute deviation.**

$$
J_n = \lvert T_{actual,n} - T \rvert
$$

**Step 4 — Average jitter over a window of $N$ iterations.**

$$
\bar{J} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} J_n
$$

**Step 5 — Deadline violation test.**

If $t_{exec,n} > D$, then the structure applies the *Mode* policy:

$$
M = \begin{cases}
\text{Discard} & \text{(default — skip the missed tick and resync)} \\
\text{Swap} & \text{(swap to next queued iteration)} \\
\text{Process} & \text{(process all missed iterations in rapid succession)} \\
\text{Suspend} & \text{(halt the loop, raise an error)}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Timed Structures vs Conventional Structures

| Property | While Loop + Wait | Timed Loop | Flat Sequence | Timed Sequence |
|----------|-------------------|------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Timing resolution** | ~15 ms (Windows OS) | 1 ms / 1 µs (RT) | None (back-to-back) | 1 ms / 1 µs (RT) |
| **Determinism** | Low (OS dependent) | **High (RT-grade)** | None | **High (RT-grade)** |
| **Dynamic period change** | No (re-wire only) | **Yes (Input Node)** | N/A | N/A |
| **Priority control** | No | **Yes ($P \in [0,100]$)** | No | **Yes (per frame)** |
| **Deadline enforcement** | No | **Yes** | No | **Yes (per frame)** |
| **Jitter diagnostics** | None | **Built-in (Right Data Node)** | None | **Per-frame report** |
| **RT-target compatible** | No | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| **Use case** | Generic loops | Real-time control | Sequential code | Timed startup procedures |
| **CPU overhead** | Low | Higher (RT scheduler) | Negligible | Higher (RT scheduler) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timed Loop — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram maps the **data-flow and control-flow interactions** of a Timed Loop in LabVIEW, including the Input Node, sub-diagram, Right Data Node, and the OS timer / scheduler.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef cfgNode fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003D7A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef timerNode fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef execNode fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef policyNode fill:#E2D5F0,stroke:#5B2C6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    INIT[Initialization Cluster]:::cfgNode
    PERIOD[Period T]:::cfgNode
    OFFSET[Offset O]:::cfgNode
    DEAD[Deadline D]:::cfgNode
    PRIO[Priority P]:::cfgNode
    MODE[Mode M]:::cfgNode
    TIMEOUT[Timeout tau]:::cfgNode

    INPUTNODE[Left Input Node]:::cfgNode

    OSCLK[High Resolution OS Timer]:::timerNode
    RTCLK[RT Hardware Timer FPGA]:::timerNode

    STARTSLEEP[Sleep until next tick]:::timerNode
    OFFSETWAIT[Wait for Offset O first iteration]:::timerNode

    EXECBLOCK[Sub Diagram Execution]:::execNode
    MEASUREXEC[Measure Execution Time texec]:::execNode

    RTDATANODE[Right Data Node Diagnostics]:::outNode
    RTDOUT1[Actual Period Tactual]:::outNode
    RTDOUT2[Jitter J]:::outNode
    RTDOUT3[Iteration Count i]:::outNode
    RTDOUT4[Missed Deadlines Nmiss]:::outNode

    DECISION[texec greater than D]:::decNode
    DISCARD[Discard Mode M]:::policyNode
    SWAP[Swap Mode M]:::policyNode
    PROCESS[Process Missed Mode M]:::policyNode
    SUSPEND[Suspend Loop Raise Error]:::policyNode

    LOOPCHECK{Conditional Terminal i greater than N or Stop TRUE}:::decNode
    NEXT[Trigger Next Iteration]:::timerNode
    EXIT[Exit Loop Return Diagnostics]:::outNode

    INIT --> INPUTNODE
    PERIOD --> INPUTNODE
    OFFSET --> INPUTNODE
    DEAD --> INPUTNODE
    PRIO --> INPUTNODE
    MODE --> INPUTNODE
    TIMEOUT --> INPUTNODE

    INPUTNODE --> OFFSETWAIT
    INPUTNODE --> STARTSLEEP
    OSCLK --> STARTSLEEP
    RTCLK --> STARTSLEEP

    STARTSLEEP --> EXECBLOCK
    OFFSETWAIT --> EXECBLOCK
    EXECBLOCK --> MEASUREXEC

    MEASUREXEC --> DECISION
    DECISION -->|No| RTDATANODE
    DECISION -->|Yes| DISCARD
    DECISION -->|Yes| SWAP
    DECISION -->|Yes| PROCESS
    DECISION -->|Yes| SUSPEND
    DISCARD --> RTDATANODE
    SWAP --> RTDATANODE
    PROCESS --> RTDATANODE
    SUSPEND --> EXIT

    RTDATANODE --> RTDOUT1
    RTDATANODE --> RTDOUT2
    RTDATANODE --> RTDOUT3
    RTDATANODE --> RTDOUT4

    RTDATANODE --> LOOPCHECK
    LOOPCHECK -->|False| NEXT
    NEXT --> STARTSLEEP
    LOOPCHECK -->|True| EXIT
```

---

## 4.2 Timed Sequence — Multi-Frame Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef frameNode fill:#D1ECF1,stroke:#0C5460,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tNode fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decNode fill:#E2D5F0,stroke:#5B2C6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    T0[Absolute Time t0]:::tNode
    F0[Frame 0 Init Hardware 100 ms]:::frameNode
    F1[Frame 1 Calibrate 50 ms delay]:::frameNode
    F2[Frame 2 Start Acq 200 ms delay]:::frameNode
    F3[Frame 3 Acquire Data]:::frameNode
    OK[All Frames Complete Continue]:::decNode

    T0 --> F0
    F0 -->|exec done at 95 ms| F1
    F1 -->|wait 50 ms then exec| F2
    F2 -->|wait 200 ms then exec| F3
    F3 --> OK
```

---

## 4.3 Timed Loop vs While Loop — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef qNode fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003D7A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef yesNode fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noNode fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Q1{Need sub 1 ms timing precision}:::qNode
    Q2{Running on Real Time target}:::qNode
    Q3{Need dynamic period at runtime}:::qNode
    Q4{Need deadline enforcement}:::qNode

    A1[Use Timed Loop]:::yesNode
    A2[Use While Loop with Wait ms]:::noNode
    A3[Consider Timed Loop with high priority]:::yesNode
    A4[Use While Loop]:::noNode
    A5[Use Timed Loop]:::yesNode
    A6[Use While Loop with Wait ms]:::noNode

    Q1 -->|Yes| A1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q2 -->|No| A2
    Q3 -->|Yes| A4
    Q3 -->|No| Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5
    Q4 -->|No| A6
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme — ESE / Module Test):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B: Choice of 1 question × 14 marks (typically two sub-parts of 7 marks each)

---

## 📌 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Timed Loop in LabVIEW. List any FOUR configuration parameters that can be set in its Input Node.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Timed Loop** is a LabVIEW execution structure that runs its sub-diagram at a *user-specified period* with deterministic, high-resolution timing — typically down to **1 ms on Windows** and **1 µs on Real-Time targets**.
>
> The **Input Node** of the Timed Loop accepts the following four configuration parameters (among others):
>
> 1. **Period ($T$)** — time between two successive iterations.
> 2. **Offset ($O$)** — delay before the *first* iteration.
> 3. **Priority ($P$)** — execution priority on RT targets (0–100).
> 4. **Deadline ($D$)** — maximum time allowed for one iteration to complete.
>
> *(1 mark — definition, ½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks for parameters.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Timed Loop and a Timed Sequence with respect to (i) iteration behaviour, (ii) typical use case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Timed Loop | Timed Sequence |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **(i) Iteration behaviour** | **Iterative** — runs a sub-diagram repeatedly at a configured period $T$ until a stop condition is met. | **Non-iterative** — runs a fixed number of frames **once**, each with its own delay. |
| **(ii) Typical use case** | Continuous real-time **control loops** (e.g., PID at 1 kHz, DAQ streaming). | **Startup / shutdown procedures** where each step must occur in a guaranteed time order (e.g., power-on self-test). |

*(1.5 marks per row.)*

---

## 📌 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module choice typically offers two questions, each with two sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students answer **one full question**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the architecture of a **Timed Loop** in LabVIEW with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Input Node** and the **Right Data Node** in detail. (7 marks)

**Q.A (b)** A Timed Loop is configured with a **period $T = 2$ ms** and an **offset $O = 5$ ms**. The loop body has a measured execution time $t_{exec} = 1.2$ ms**, and the configured **deadline $D = 1.5$ ms**.
   1. Calculate the **sampling rate $f_s$**.
   2. Compute the **CPU utilisation $U$**.
   3. Will the loop **miss its deadline**? Justify.
   4. If the loop runs for **N = 10,000 iterations**, determine the **absolute start time of the last iteration** (assume $t_0 = 0$). (7 marks)

---

### 📘 Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture of Timed Loop (7 marks)**

The **Timed Loop** is a multi-input, multi-output iterative structure in LabVIEW. Its block-diagram representation is shown below (refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid schematic).

**Key components and their roles:**

1. **Input Node (left side) — 3 marks:**
   - Receives the **configuration cluster** *at the start* of each iteration.
   - Parameters accepted: **Period $T$, Offset $O$, Priority $P$, Deadline $D$, Mode $M$, Timeout $\tau$**.
   - These parameters can be **wired dynamically**, allowing runtime changes to timing behaviour without stopping the loop.
   - It functions as a *write-only* register from the developer's perspective.

2. **Sub-diagram (loop body):**
   - Contains the actual VI code: DAQ `Read`, signal processing, control output, etc.
   - Executes once per period.
   - Time taken to execute is monitored by the **Right Data Node**.

3. **Right Data Node (right side) — 3 marks:**
   - Returns **diagnostic information** *after* the iteration completes.
   - Outputs include: **iteration count $i$, actual period $T_{actual}$, jitter $J$, missed-deadline count $N_{miss}$, late count, processor affinity**.
   - Functions as a *read-only* register that the developer can monitor and log.

4. **Conditional Terminal:**
   - Behaves identically to a `For` or `While` loop — controls iteration termination.

**Marking split:** 1 mark for the labelled diagram (textual / schematic), 3 marks for Input Node role, 3 marks for Right Data Node role.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $T = 2$ ms $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $O = 5$ ms $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $t_{exec} = 1.2$ ms $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $D = 1.5$ ms $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $N = 10{,}000$, $t_0 = 0$

**(1) Sampling rate $f_s$ — 2 marks:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_s = 500\;\text{Hz}\,}
$$

**(2) CPU utilisation $U$ — 2 marks:**

$$
U = \frac{t_{exec}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{1.2}{2.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,U = 60\%\,}
$$

This is *within* the 70% safety bound — the system is safely loaded.

**(3) Deadline-miss check — 1.5 marks:**

$$
t_{exec} = 1.2\,\text{ms} \quad\text{vs.}\quad D = 1.5\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
t_{exec} = 1.2 < D = 1.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{The loop will NOT miss its deadline.}\,}
$$

**(4) Start time of the 10,000th iteration — 1.5 marks:**

Using the derived closed-form (Section 3.1):

$$
t_{start,n} = t_0 + O + n \cdot T
$$

For the 10,000th iteration, the iteration counter $n$ takes values $0, 1, 2, \dots, 9999$. The start time of the iteration with $n = 9999$ is:

$$
t_{start,9999} = 0 + 5 \times 10^{-3} + 9999 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
t_{start,9999} = 5 \times 10^{-3} + 19.998
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{start,9999} = 20.003\;\text{s}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls (Q.A — Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **Off-by-one error on the iteration counter:** The *n-th* iteration means $n$ ranges from $0$ to $N-1$, not $1$ to $N$. Many students use $n = 10{,}000$ and get $t_{start} = 20.005$ s — *wrong by 2 ms* — losing **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Always convert ms to seconds (or vice versa) *before* substituting into the formula. Mixing units (e.g., adding $5$ ms to $9999 \times 2$ seconds) is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing $T$ (period) with $D$ (deadline):** $T$ is *when the next tick fires*; $D$ is *how long the work can take*. Many students swap them.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3]`

**Q.B (a)** What is a **Timed Sequence** in LabVIEW? Explain its **frame-delay mechanism** and list **two scenarios** where it is preferred over a Timed Loop. (7 marks)

**Q.B (b)** A vibration-analysis VI uses a Timed Loop. The **configured period is $T = 100 \,\mu\text{s}$** and the **loop body executes in $t_{exec} = 75 \,\mu\text{s}$** on average. Compute:
   1. The **sampling frequency $f_s$**.
   2. The **loop start time of the 500th iteration** (assume $O = 0$, $t_0 = 0$).
   3. The **CPU utilisation $U$**.
   4. Whether the system is **sustainable** in the long run. (7 marks)

---

### 📘 Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Timed Sequence (7 marks)**

A **Timed Sequence** is a *flat-sequence-like* structure in LabVIEW where each frame is executed **once**, in a strict left-to-right order, and each frame can be configured with an explicit **start-time delay** relative to the completion of the previous frame. (2 marks)

**Frame-delay mechanism — 3 marks:**

- Each frame $k$ has a configurable delay $\Delta t_{f,k}$.
- The frame does not begin execution until the previous frame has *finished* AND $\Delta t_{f,k}$ has elapsed.
- Mathematically:

$$
t_{start,k} = t_{end,k-1} + \Delta t_{f,k}
$$

- This is a **deterministic sequential** pattern — no parallelism, no iteration.

**Two scenarios preferred over Timed Loop — 2 marks:**

1. **Power-on / boot-up sequence:** Power supply stabilisation $\rightarrow$ sensor warm-up $\rightarrow$ calibration $\rightarrow$ begin acquisition.
2. **Controlled shutdown sequence:** Stop data logging $\rightarrow$ flush buffers $\rightarrow$ close file $\rightarrow$ power down hardware.
3. *(Alternative)* **Multi-stage test procedure** where each stage is a *one-shot* event with a guaranteed time gap.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $T = 100\,\mu\text{s} = 100 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}$
- $t_{exec} = 75\,\mu\text{s} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s}$
- $O = 0$, $t_0 = 0$
- $N_{query} = 500$

**(1) Sampling frequency $f_s$ — 2 marks:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_s = 10{,}000\;\text{Hz} = 10\;\text{kHz}\,}
$$

**(2) Start time of the 500th iteration — 2 marks:**

The 500th iteration corresponds to $n = 499$ (zero-indexed). Using $t_{start,n} = t_0 + O + n \cdot T$:

$$
t_{start,499} = 0 + 0 + 499 \times 100\,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
t_{start,499} = 49{,}900\,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{start,499} = 49.9\;\text{ms}\,}
$$

**(3) CPU utilisation $U$ — 1.5 marks:**

$$
U = \frac{t_{exec}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{75}{100} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,U = 75\%\,}
$$

**(4) Sustainability verdict — 1.5 marks:**

Since $U = 75\% > 70\%$ (the conventional RT safety bound), the system is **borderline**. While not *strictly* overloaded, it has **insufficient headroom** for OS scheduler spikes, interrupts, and worst-case jitter. **Recommendation:** Reduce $t_{exec}$ (optimise code) or increase $T$ (reduce $f_s$) to bring $U$ below 70%. The system is **not advisable for long-term deployment** in its current configuration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls (Q.B — Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **µs vs ms confusion:** Students frequently compute $f_s = 10$ Hz instead of 10 kHz because they misread µs as ms. Always underline the unit. **Mark deduction: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting the 70% rule:** Many students mark this as "sustainable" simply because $U < 100\%$. The 70% RT-safety bound must be quoted. **Mark deduction: 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Off-by-one on iteration index:** Same issue as Question A — *n-th* iteration = index $n-1$. **Mark deduction: 0.5 mark.**

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Timed Structures**

- ✅ A **Timed Loop** is iterative; a **Timed Sequence** is one-shot / sequential.
- ✅ Timed Loop uses a **high-resolution OS / RT timer**, not the coarse OS scheduler.
- ✅ Six key parameters: **Period $T$, Offset $O$, Deadline $D$, Priority $P$, Mode $M$, Timeout $\tau$**.
- ✅ **Period** = time between iterations; **Deadline** = max time for one iteration; **Offset** = first-iteration delay.
- ✅ Sampling rate formula: $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ (inverse of period).
- ✅ Iteration start time: $t_{start,n} = t_0 + O + n \cdot T$ (deterministic, jitter-free on RT).
- ✅ CPU utilisation: $U = \dfrac{t_{exec}}{T} \times 100\%$; keep **$U \le 70\%$** for RT safety.
- ✅ **Mode $M$** decides policy on deadline miss: **Discard / Swap / Process / Suspend**.
- ✅ **Jitter $J = \lvert T_{actual} - T_{ideal} \rvert$**; aim for $J \ll T$.
- ✅ **Right Data Node** outputs diagnostics: $T_{actual}$, $J$, $N_{miss}$, iteration $i$.
- ✅ Timed Structures are **mandatory on Real-Time targets**; `While + Wait` is **insufficient** for sub-ms determinism.
- ✅ Use **Timed Loop** for continuous real-time control / DAQ streaming.
- ✅ Use **Timed Sequence** for deterministic startup / shutdown / calibration sequences.
- ✅ **N-th iteration = index $N-1$** (zero-based) — off-by-one is the #1 mark-loss reason in KTU scripts.
- ✅ Always **convert units consistently** (ms vs s vs µs) before substituting in any formula.
- ✅ Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ (not bare `|`) in markdown/LATEX to avoid layout corruption.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> In any 7-mark numerical sub-part, the *expected steps* are: (i) write the formula symbolically, (ii) substitute values with units, (iii) compute the result, (iv) state the answer in a box, (v) interpret the result in one sentence. Missing any of these steps costs **at least 0.5 mark**. Train this five-step habit before the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formula Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formula Nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Formula Node** in LabVIEW is a resizable rectangular structure placed on the Block Diagram that allows the user to embed and execute **C-like textual mathematical expressions, equations, and algorithmic logic** directly within the graphical dataflow environment. It acts as a textual "sub-window" that evaluates formulas using input variables supplied from the surrounding graphical code, and writes computed results back to output variables for the rest of the VI to consume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A Formula Node is a structure on the LabVIEW block diagram which is used to execute mathematical formulas and C-like expressions. It is preferable when the expression contains many variables or when the resulting block diagram becomes too large using built-in arithmetic functions."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Calculator Sticky-Note" Intuition

Imagine you are building a complex electrical circuit on a breadboard (this is your LabVIEW **Block Diagram**). Normally, every resistor, capacitor, and op-amp must be physically placed and wired by hand. But suddenly you need to compute a complex control equation — say a **PID algorithm** with 12 variables.

If you wire it with individual arithmetic blocks, the diagram becomes a tangled **"spaghetti of wires"** that is impossible to debug or read.

**Formula Nodes are like writing that entire 12-variable equation on a single yellow sticky-note and pasting it on the breadboard.** The inputs (voltage probes) poke into the sticky-note from the left, the equation is written in a simple C-style syntax inside, and the results pop out the right side — clean, compact, and instantly readable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> Formula Nodes do **NOT** replace the graphical philosophy of LabVIEW. They are an **escape hatch** reserved for situations where the graphical representation becomes unmanageable. The KTU examiner expects you to know **when** to use a Formula Node versus when to use built-in arithmetic primitives.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Formula Nodes

- **Structure type:** Resizable rectangular boundary placed on the Block Diagram.
- **Syntax style:** Identical to **ANSI C** expression syntax.
- **Input terminals:** Appear on the **left border** of the node.
- **Output terminals:** Appear on the **right border** of the node.
- **Data flow integration:** Inputs are read once, all statements execute **sequentially top-to-bottom**, and outputs are written to the right-side terminals after computation.
- **Supported elements:**
    - Variable declarations (implicit or explicit)
    - Arithmetic operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `**`, `%`)
    - Relational operators (`<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`)
    - Logical operators (`&&`, `||`, `!`)
    - Built-in math functions (`sin`, `cos`, `tan`, `sqrt`, `log`, `exp`, `pow`, `abs`, etc.)
    - Control flow statements (`if / else`, `switch / case`, `while`, `for`)
- **Case sensitivity:** **Yes** — all variable names are case-sensitive (just like C).
- **Statement terminator:** Every statement must end with a **semicolon** (`;`).
- **Comment style:** Both `//` (single-line) and `/* ... */` (block) comments are supported.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **LabVIEW Default Configuration Metrics:**
> - Default font: **Courier New**, size **13 pt**
> - Maximum node width: limited by **front panel viewing area**
> - Input/Output terminal default data type: **Double Precision (DBL)** when first created
> - Evaluation precision: Matches the precision of the input data type (DBL = 64-bit IEEE 754)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The Formula Node operates on a strict **input → process → output** dataflow model. Understanding this lifecycle is critical for KTU 14-mark questions.

1. **Terminal Creation Phase**
   - Right-click the Formula Node border → *Add Input* or *Add Output*.
   - A small terminal icon appears with a default name (e.g., `x`, `y`, `result`).
   - Renaming the terminal automatically creates a variable of the same name inside the node.

2. **Wiring Phase**
   - Wire a source (control, indicator, or function output) to the input terminal.
   - The terminal label becomes a local variable inside the Formula Node.
   - Output terminals must be wired to indicators, downstream functions, or other structures.

3. **Data Type Inference**
   - The terminal's color indicates the LabVIEW data type (Orange = Floating-Point DBL, Blue = Integer I32, Green = Boolean, Brown = String, Pink = Error cluster).
   - The variable inside the node inherits this data type.

4. **Expression Editing Phase**
   - Double-click the Formula Node interior to enter edit mode.
   - Type the C-style expression using the declared variables.
   - Each line of code must end with `;`.

5. **Execution Phase**
   - When the Formula Node receives all required input data, the engine executes all statements **sequentially from top to bottom**.
   - This is fundamentally different from the rest of LabVIEW, which is **parallel dataflow** — *inside* the Formula Node, it is **sequential imperative execution**.

6. **Output Writing Phase**
   - After the last statement completes, the values of all output variables are written to the corresponding terminals.
   - Dataflow then propagates these values to the rest of the block diagram.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why C-like syntax?** Because C is the de-facto language of engineering computation, making Formula Nodes instantly familiar to ECE/EEE students.
- **Why sequential execution inside the node?** Because algebraic formulas and iterative algorithms (like `for` loops) inherently require **temporal order**, unlike parallel dataflow.
- **Why pre-declared terminals instead of free variables?** Because the surrounding LabVIEW block diagram must know the data type and connection points before the node can be wired. This enforces **strong typing** consistent with LabVIEW's dataflow philosophy.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Operator Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Complete Formula Node Operator and Function Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)**

| Category | Operator / Function | Meaning / Syntax | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | `+` | Addition | `c = a + b;` |
| Arithmetic | `-` | Subtraction | `c = a - b;` |
| Arithmetic | `*` | Multiplication | `p = v * i;` |
| Arithmetic | `/` | Division | `r = v / i;` |
| Arithmetic | `**` | Exponentiation (Power) | `area = pi * r**2;` |
| Arithmetic | `%` | Modulo (Remainder) | `r = a % b;` |
| Relational | `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` | Magnitude comparison | `if (temp < 100) ...` |
| Relational | `==`, `!=` | Equality / Inequality | `if (x == 0) ...` |
| Logical | `&&` | Logical AND | `if (a>0 && b>0)` |
| Logical | `\|\|` | Logical OR | `if (a<0 \|\| a>10)` |
| Logical | `!` | Logical NOT | `if (!enable)` |
| Assignment | `=` | Variable assignment | `y = m*x + c;` |
| Trig | `sin(x)`, `cos(x)`, `tan(x)` | Trigonometric (radians) | `y = sin(theta);` |
| Inverse Trig | `asin(x)`, `acos(x)`, `atan(x)` | Inverse trig (radians) | `theta = atan(y/x);` |
| Hyperbolic | `sinh(x)`, `cosh(x)`, `tanh(x)` | Hyperbolic | `y = sinh(x);` |
| Power / Root | `pow(x, y)`, `sqrt(x)` | Power, square root | `d = sqrt(a*a + b*b);` |
| Log | `log(x)`, `log10(x)`, `ln(x)` | Logarithmic | `db = 20*log10(v);` |
| Exp | `exp(x)` | Natural exponential | `y = exp(-x);` |
| Rounding | `floor(x)`, `ceil(x)`, `round(x)` | Rounding | `n = floor(x);` |
| Absolute | `abs(x)`, `fabs(x)` | Absolute value | `mag = abs(x);` |
| Constant | `pi` | $\pi = 3.14159...$ | `area = pi * r**2;` |
| Selection | `if / else` | Conditional branch | See Section 3 |
| Selection | `switch / case` | Multi-way branch | See Section 3 |
| Looping | `while` | Pre-test loop | `while (i < 10) {...}` |
| Looping | `for` | Counted loop | `for (i=0; i<10; i++) {...}` |
| Comment | `//` | Single-line comment | `// compute power` |
| Comment | `/* ... */` | Block comment | `/* main calc */` |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX ALERT for KTU 2024:**
> - Use `**` for exponentiation — NOT `^` (which is a bitwise XOR in C syntax).
> - Every statement must end with `;`.
> - The logical NOT operator is `!` — NOT `~` (which is bitwise NOT in C, not allowed here).
> - Use `pi` (lowercase) for $\pi$ — it is pre-defined as a built-in constant.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Formula Nodes are extensively used in:

- **Control Systems:** Implementing PID, lead-lag, and state-space controllers.
- **Signal Processing:** Computing FFT magnitudes, RMS values, SNR, THD.
- **Instrumentation:** Sensor linearization (thermocouple J, K, T type conversions).
- **Communications:** Computing BER, Q-factor, Eb/N0 for digital links.
- **Power Systems:** Three-phase power calculations using $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$.
- **Mechanical Vibrations:** Damping ratio, natural frequency, transmissibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** When asked "Why Formula Nodes are preferred for complex equations?" in a 14-mark question, always mention **(1) compactness, (2) readability, (3) ease of debugging, and (4) ability to include loops and conditionals** that would otherwise clutter the block diagram.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Quadratic Equation Roots (Board-Favorite Problem)

**Problem Statement:** Write a Formula Node that computes the real roots of the quadratic equation $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, given inputs $a$, $b$, and $c$.

**Formula Required (Derivation from Algebra):**

The standard quadratic formula is:

$$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

**Step-by-Step Algorithmic Derivation:**

Step 1 — Identify the discriminant. The expression under the square root is called the **discriminant**, denoted $D$:

$$D = b^2 - 4ac$$

Step 2 — If $D < 0$, the roots are complex (no real solution). The Formula Node must check this and output a flag.

Step 3 — If $D \geq 0$, the two real roots are:

$$x_1 = \frac{-b + \sqrt{D}}{2a}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{-b - \sqrt{D}}{2a}$$

Step 4 — Assemble the C-style code for the Formula Node.

**Final Formula Node Code (LabVIEW-Compatible Text):**

```c
D = b*b - 4*a*c;
if (D < 0) {
    valid = 0;
} else {
    valid = 1;
    sqrtD = sqrt(D);
    x1 = (-b + sqrtD) / (2*a);
    x2 = (-b - sqrtD) / (2*a);
}
```

**Line-by-Line Logical Explanation:**

- **Line 1:** Computes the discriminant $D$ using the algebraic identity. Note: `b*b` is used instead of `b^2` because `^` is not a valid power operator in Formula Node syntax.
- **Line 2:** The `if` statement checks if $D$ is negative. If true, the equation has no real roots.
- **Line 3:** Sets the boolean output `valid` to **0** (false) when no real root exists.
- **Line 4:** The `else` branch handles the case when $D \geq 0$.
- **Line 5:** Sets `valid` to **1** (true) to signal that real roots were computed.
- **Line 6:** Computes $\sqrt{D}$ once and stores it in a temporary variable `sqrtD` (this avoids recomputing it).
- **Line 7:** Computes the plus root using the quadratic formula.
- **Line 8:** Computes the minus root using the quadratic formula.

**Block Diagram Concept (Textual Sketch):**

```
   [Numeric Control: a] ──┐
   [Numeric Control: b] ──┼──► [ Formula Node ] ──► [Numeric Indicator: x1]
   [Numeric Control: c] ──┘                │
                                           ├──► [Numeric Indicator: x2]
                                           │
                                           └──► [Boolean LED: valid]
```

## 3.2 Example 2 — RMS Value of an AC Waveform

**Problem Statement:** Compute the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of a sampled AC waveform using a Formula Node. The waveform samples are stored in an array, and the number of samples is known.

**Formula (Theoretical Derivation):**

By definition, the RMS value of a continuous signal $v(t)$ is:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\, dt}$$

For a discretely sampled waveform with $N$ samples $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_N$, the discrete approximation is:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Initialize a running sum to zero:

$$S = 0$$

Step 2 — Loop through every sample and accumulate the square:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2$$

Step 3 — Divide by $N$ to obtain the mean square value:

$$\bar{v^2} = \frac{S}{N}$$

Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain the RMS value:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\bar{v^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{S}{N}}$$

**Final Formula Node Code:**

```c
sumSq = 0;
for (i = 0; i < N; i = i + 1) {
    sumSq = sumSq + samples[i] * samples[i];
}
meanSq = sumSq / N;
Vrms = sqrt(meanSq);
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

- **Line 1:** Initializes the accumulator `sumSq` to **0**. In LabVIEW Formula Nodes, uninitialized variables default to 0, but explicit initialization is best practice.
- **Line 2:** Opens a `for` loop. The syntax `for (i = 0; i < N; i = i + 1)` is identical to ANSI C.
- **Line 3:** Adds the square of the $i^{th}$ sample to the running sum. The expression `samples[i]` accesses the $i^{th}$ element of the 1D array wired to the input terminal.
- **Line 4:** Closes the loop.
- **Line 5:** Divides the accumulated sum by $N$ to get the mean square value $\bar{v^2}$.
- **Line 6:** Takes the square root using the built-in `sqrt()` function and assigns the result to the output `Vrms`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle Point (Often Asked in KTU):** The array variable `samples` is passed to the Formula Node as a **1D array input terminal**. Formula Nodes natively support indexing into 1D arrays passed in this way.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Switch-Case Statement for Unit Conversion

**Problem Statement:** Write a Formula Node that accepts a temperature value and a unit selector (0 = Celsius, 1 = Fahrenheit, 2 = Kelvin) and outputs the equivalent temperature in **Celsius**.

**Theoretical Background:**

- Celsius to Celsius: $C = T$
- Fahrenheit to Celsius: $C = (T - 32) \times \frac{5}{9}$
- Kelvin to Celsius: $C = T - 273.15$

**Formula Node Code (Using switch-case):**

```c
switch (unit) {
    case 0:
        celsius = temp;
        break;
    case 1:
        celsius = (temp - 32.0) * 5.0 / 9.0;
        break;
    case 2:
        celsius = temp - 273.15;
        break;
    default:
        celsius = 0;
        break;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Walk-Through:**

- **Line 1:** The `switch` keyword evaluates the integer variable `unit` (which was passed as an input terminal from a ring or enum control).
- **Line 2:** `case 0:` handles the Celsius-to-Celsius identity conversion.
- **Line 3:** The variable `celsius` is assigned the input temperature directly.
- **Line 4:** `break;` exits the switch structure — **mandatory** in C-style syntax.
- **Line 5:** `case 1:` handles Fahrenheit-to-Celsius conversion using the standard linear formula.
- **Line 6:** Applies the algebraic transformation $(T - 32) \times \frac{5}{9}$.
- **Line 7:** `break;` prevents fall-through to the next case.
- **Line 8:** `case 2:` handles Kelvin-to-Celsius by subtracting 273.15 (the absolute zero offset).
- **Line 9:** Performs the offset subtraction.
- **Line 10:** `break;` exits the switch.
- **Line 11:** The `default:` branch handles invalid unit values.
- **Line 12:** Sets the output to 0 to indicate an error condition.
- **Line 13:** `break;` finalizes the switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Note for the Examiner:** Notice that the Fahrenheit conversion uses the literal constant `32.0` and `5.0/9.0` rather than `5/9`. In C-style integer arithmetic, `5/9` would evaluate to `0` (truncating division). Always use floating-point literals when fractional results are required.

## 3.4 Example 4 — Series Computation Using While Loop

**Problem Statement:** Compute the sum of the geometric series $S = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} ar^i = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ for $r \neq 1$, using both a Formula Node **with** and **without** a loop.

**Analytical Derivation (Closed-Form Identity):**

For a geometric series starting with first term $a$ and common ratio $r$:

$$S = a + ar + ar^2 + ar^3 + \dots + ar^{N-1}$$

Multiplying both sides by $r$:

$$rS = ar + ar^2 + ar^3 + \dots + ar^N$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first (telescoping series):

$$S - rS = a - ar^N$$

$$S(1 - r) = a(1 - r^N)$$

$$\boxed{S = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}}$$

**Closed-Form Formula Node Code (No Loop Needed):**

```c
S = a * (1 - pow(r, N)) / (1 - r);
```

**Iterative Formula Node Code (With While Loop):**

```c
i = 0;
S = 0;
term = a;
while (i < N) {
    S = S + term;
    term = term * r;
    i = i + 1;
}
```

**Comparative Insight for KTU Answers:** The closed-form approach is more computationally efficient ($O(1)$ complexity) but requires the user to know the analytical identity. The iterative approach is more general ($O(N)$ complexity) and works for any series where no closed form exists (e.g., transcendental series like $e^x$).

## 3.5 Comparison Table — Formula Node vs. Expression Node vs. Built-in Arithmetic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — Critical Comparison for KTU 14-Mark Questions**

| Feature | Formula Node | Expression Node | Built-in Arithmetic VIs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Syntax style | C-like multi-statement | Single algebraic line | Graphical function icons |
| Supports loops (`for`, `while`) | Yes | No | No |
| Supports conditionals (`if/else`, `switch`) | Yes | No | No |
| Supports arrays directly | Yes (1D indexing) | No | No (uses Index Array VI) |
| Supports local variables | Yes | No | No |
| Multi-line code | Yes | No (single line) | N/A |
| Best suited for | Complex algorithms with control flow | Single formula evaluation | Simple $a + b$ operations |
| Memory footprint | Larger | Smaller | Smallest |
| Reusability | Limited (instance-only) | Limited (instance-only) | High (subVI) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Formula Node Architecture in a LabVIEW VI

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of how a Formula Node integrates into a LabVIEW Virtual Instrument (VI), from front-panel controls to block-diagram execution to front-panel indicators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Front Panel Controls]:::input
    nodeB[Input Terminals on Formula Node Left Border]:::terminal
    nodeC[Formula Node Interior C like Code]:::core
    nodeD[Output Terminals on Formula Node Right Border]:::terminal
    nodeE[Downstream LabVIEW Functions or Indicators]:::output
    nodeF[LabVIEW Dataflow Engine]:::engine

    nodeA -- wire connection --> nodeB
    nodeB -- data dependency triggers --> nodeF
    nodeF -- executes sequentially --> nodeC
    nodeC -- writes results to --> nodeD
    nodeD -- wire connection --> nodeE

    classDef input fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef core fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef engine fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Yellow block (Front Panel Controls):** Numeric controls, knobs, or text inputs the user interacts with on the front panel.
- **Green blocks (Input/Output Terminals):** The orange (DBL), blue (I32), or green (Boolean) terminal icons on the left and right border of the Formula Node.
- **Red block (Formula Node Interior):** The C-style code text area where the user types formulas, loops, and conditionals.
- **Blue block (Downstream Functions):** Any other LabVIEW node or front-panel indicator that consumes the Formula Node's output.
- **Purple block (Dataflow Engine):** The implicit runtime that schedules Formula Node execution when all inputs are available.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Internal Sequential Execution of a Formula Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start Formula Node Execution]:::startNode
    stepB[Read All Input Terminal Values]:::ioStep
    stepC[Statement 1 Executes]:::execStep
    stepD[Statement 2 Executes]:::execStep
    stepE[Statement N Executes]:::execStep
    stepF[Write All Output Terminal Values]:::ioStep
    stepG[Release Dataflow Tokens Downstream]:::endNode

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef ioStep fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef execStep fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef endNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

**Key Observation:** The vertical chain `stepC → stepD → stepE` represents the **sequential** nature of execution *inside* the Formula Node. This is unique — everywhere else in LabVIEW, nodes execute in **parallel** governed by data availability. This is why Formula Nodes are sometimes called "**islands of sequential execution in a sea of dataflow**."

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison: Formula Node vs. Graphical Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    graphA[Graphical LabVIEW Block Diagram]:::graph
    graphB[Add Function]:::gfunc
    graphC[Multiply Function]:::gfunc
    graphD[Subtract Function]:::gfunc
    graphE[Divide Function]:::gfunc
    graphF[Tangled Wiring]:::warn
    graphG[Formula Node Compact Box]:::formula
    graphH[Clean C like Code]:::clean

    graphA -- implements --> graphB
    graphA -- implements --> graphC
    graphA -- implements --> graphD
    graphA -- implements --> graphE
    graphB --> graphF
    graphC --> graphF
    graphD --> graphF
    graphE --> graphF
    graphF -- replaced by --> graphG
    graphG -- contains --> graphH

    classDef graph fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef gfunc fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef warn fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef formula fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef clean fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Educational Takeaway:** The left side shows the **graphical approach** (multiple function icons + tangled wires) and the right side shows the **Formula Node approach** (one compact box with clean C-style code). This visual contrast is exactly what the KTU examiner expects you to explain in a 7-mark sub-question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Formula Node in LabVIEW. List any four of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Formula Node is a resizable rectangular structure placed on the LabVIEW block diagram that allows users to write and execute **C-like textual mathematical expressions** and algorithmic logic directly within the graphical dataflow environment.

**Four Key Characteristics (½ mark each):**

1. It uses **ANSI C-style syntax** with semicolon-terminated statements.
2. **Input terminals** appear on the left border; **output terminals** appear on the right border.
3. It supports **control flow statements** like `if / else`, `switch / case`, `for`, and `while` loops.
4. Execution inside the node is **sequential (top-to-bottom)**, unlike the parallel dataflow of the rest of LabVIEW.
5. (Bonus) Variable names are **case-sensitive** and the exponentiation operator is `**` (not `^`).

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Formula Node and an Expression Node. Mention two advantages of Formula Nodes over Expression Nodes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Formula Node | Expression Node |
|---|---|---|
| Statements | Supports **multiple statements** and multi-line code | Supports only a **single algebraic expression** |
| Control flow | Supports `if/else`, `switch`, `for`, `while` | **No** control flow constructs |
| Size | Larger rectangular structure | Smaller inline node |

**Two Advantages of Formula Node (1 mark each):**

1. **Multi-line support:** Complex equations spanning multiple lines can be written clearly, improving readability.
2. **Control flow support:** Loops and conditionals allow implementation of full algorithms (e.g., iterative solvers) that are impossible in an Expression Node.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture and working of a Formula Node in LabVIEW with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the data flow into and out of the Formula Node.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Architecture (3 marks):**

A Formula Node is a resizable **rectangular structure** placed on the block diagram from the *Programming → Structures → Formula Node* palette. It has:

- **Left border:** Hosts the **input terminals** where external data enters the node. Each input terminal represents a **local variable** with a user-defined name and data type.
- **Right border:** Hosts the **output terminals** that expose the computed results back to the block diagram.
- **Interior:** A text-editing area where C-like expressions, variable assignments, conditional statements, and loops are typed.

**Block Diagram Sketch (1.5 marks):**

```
  [a]──┐
  [b]──┤──[FORMULA NODE]──┬──► [Result1]
  [c]──┘   y = a*x + b;   │
           z = sqrt(c);   └──► [Result2]
```

**2. Working Mechanism (3 marks):**

The Formula Node operates in three phases:

- **Phase 1 — Data Acquisition:** When all input terminals receive valid data, the LabVIEW dataflow engine triggers the Formula Node.
- **Phase 2 — Sequential Execution:** All statements inside the node execute **strictly top-to-bottom** in imperative fashion (unlike the parallel dataflow of the surrounding block diagram).
- **Phase 3 — Output Propagation:** After the last statement, the final values of all declared output variables are written to the right-side terminals and propagated downstream.

**3. Key Properties (1 mark):**

- **Case-sensitive** variable names.
- Semicolon `;` terminates every statement.
- Exponentiation uses `**`, not `^`.
- Comments use `//` or `/* ... */`.
- Supports arrays, 1D indexing, and built-in math functions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a Formula Node to compute the equivalent resistance of a parallel combination of N resistors. Inputs: array `R` of N resistor values, integer `N`. Output: equivalent resistance `Req`. Show the Formula Node code and explain.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Theoretical Derivation (2 marks):**

For N resistors $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$ connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance is given by the reciprocal sum formula:

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \dots + \frac{1}{R_N} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{R_i}$$

Therefore, the equivalent resistance is:

$$\boxed{R_{eq} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{R_i}}}$$

**2. Formula Node Code (3 marks):**

```c
sumRecip = 0;
for (i = 0; i < N; i = i + 1) {
    sumRecip = sumRecip + 1.0 / R[i];
}
Req = 1.0 / sumRecip;
```

**3. Line-by-Line Explanation (2 marks):**

- **Line 1:** Initializes the running sum of reciprocals to zero.
- **Line 2:** Begins a counted `for` loop from `i = 0` to `i = N-1`.
- **Line 3:** For each iteration, adds $\frac{1}{R_i}$ to the accumulator. Using the floating-point literal `1.0` (not `1`) ensures floating-point division, avoiding integer truncation.
- **Line 4:** Closes the loop.
- **Line 5:** Computes $R_{eq}$ as the reciprocal of the accumulated sum, giving the final parallel equivalent resistance.

**Block Diagram Integration:**

```
[R array]──┐
           ├──[FORMULA NODE]──►[Req Indicator]
   [N]─────┘
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of operators and built-in functions supported by a Formula Node. Provide at least two examples of each category.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Arithmetic Operators (1.5 marks):**

These perform mathematical computation.

| Operator | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | Addition | `s = a + b;` |
| `-` | Subtraction | `d = a - b;` |
| `*` | Multiplication | `p = v * i;` |
| `/` | Division | `r = v / i;` |
| `**` | Exponentiation | `v2 = v1**2;` |
| `%` | Modulo (remainder) | `r = a % b;` |

**Example 1 — Area of a circle:**
```c
area = pi * r * r;
```

**Example 2 — Power dissipation in a resistor:**
```c
p = v * v / r;
```

**2. Relational Operators (1.5 marks):**

These compare two values and return a boolean (True/False) result.

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `<` | Less than |
| `>` | Greater than |
| `<=` | Less than or equal |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal |
| `==` | Equal to |
| `!=` | Not equal to |

**Example — Voltage limit check:**
```c
overLimit = (v > 250.0);
```

**3. Logical Operators (1.5 marks):**

These combine boolean expressions.

| Operator | Function |
|---|---|
| `&&` | Logical AND |
| `\|\|` | Logical OR |
| `!` | Logical NOT |

**Example — Safety interlock:**
```c
alarm = (temp > 100.0) && (pressure > 5.0);
```

**4. Built-in Math Functions (1.5 marks):**

Common functions include `sin`, `cos`, `tan`, `asin`, `acos`, `atan`, `sinh`, `cosh`, `tanh`, `sqrt`, `pow`, `log`, `log10`, `exp`, `abs`, `floor`, `ceil`, `round`.

**Example 1 — Distance between two points:**
```c
d = sqrt((x2-x1)*(x2-x1) + (y2-y1)*(y2-y1));
```

**Example 2 — dB conversion of a voltage gain:**
```c
dB = 20.0 * log10(v_out / v_in);
```

**5. Special Constants (1 mark):**

- `pi` — The constant $\pi \approx 3.14159265$.
- `epsilon`, `inf`, `nan` are also available in some LabVIEW versions.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write a Formula Node program to evaluate the temperature $T$ (in °C) of a thermocouple given its measured voltage $V$ (in mV) using the linear approximation formula:**

$$T = a_0 + a_1 \cdot V + a_2 \cdot V^2$$

**where the constants are $a_0 = 25.0$, $a_1 = 40.0$, $a_2 = -0.5$. Also implement a warning flag that activates when $T > 500$ °C.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Theoretical Background (1 mark):**

Thermocouples produce a small millivolt-level voltage proportional to the temperature difference between the hot junction and the cold junction. For a Type K thermocouple, the **NIST polynomial approximation** in a limited range is:

$$T(^\circ C) = a_0 + a_1 V + a_2 V^2 + a_3 V^3 + \dots$$

For the simplified second-order approximation specified in the problem:

$$T = 25.0 + 40.0 \cdot V - 0.5 \cdot V^2$$

**2. Formula Node Code (3 marks):**

```c
// Compute temperature from thermocouple voltage
T = a0 + a1*V + a2*V*V;

// Set high temperature warning flag
if (T > 500.0) {
    warning = 1;
} else {
    warning = 0;
}
```

**3. Step-by-Step Walk-Through (2 marks):**

- **Line 1:** A single-line comment describing the purpose of the code.
- **Line 2:** Implements the quadratic polynomial using two multiplications for $V^2$ (since `**` is also valid, but explicit multiplication is sometimes preferred for numerical stability in low-order polynomials).
- **Line 3:** Comments are not required but illustrate good practice.
- **Line 4:** Conditional `if` statement checks if the computed temperature exceeds 500 °C.
- **Line 5:** Sets the boolean output `warning` to **1** (True) to activate the high-temperature alarm.
- **Line 6:** The `else` branch handles the safe case.
- **Line 7:** Sets `warning` to **0** (False) to indicate normal operation.

**4. Block Diagram Sketch (1 mark):**

```
  [V control]──┐
  [a0 const]───┤
  [a1 const]───┼──[FORMULA NODE]──┬──► [T Indicator]
  [a2 const]───┘                   │
                                   └──► [Warning LED]
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the semicolon `;`** at the end of a Formula Node statement — costs **1–2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Using `^` instead of `**`** for exponentiation — the `^` is interpreted as a bitwise XOR in C-style syntax and will throw a syntax error or give wrong results.
> 3. **Using `5/9` instead of `5.0/9.0`** in temperature conversions — the integer division truncates to `0`, yielding a zero output and full loss of marks for that sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting `break;` in switch-case statements** — C-style fall-through is rarely the intended behavior in engineering code and causes logic errors.
> 5. **Confusing Formula Node with Expression Node** in 3-mark definition questions — the KTU examiner often awards partial credit, but mixing up the two is the **single most common reason** for losing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not wiring the output terminals** — Formula Nodes with unwired outputs run fine during execution but the KTU examiner explicitly tests whether you understand that outputs must be wired to indicators or downstream functions to be useful.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Formula Nodes (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Resizable rectangular structure on the LabVIEW block diagram for executing C-like textual formulas and algorithmic logic.
- ☐ **Placement Path:** *Functions Palette → Programming → Structures → Formula Node*.
- ☐ **Syntax:** ANSI C — every statement ends with `;` and code is case-sensitive.
- ☐ **Exponentiation operator:** `**` (double asterisk) — **NOT** `^`.
