A **Motion Controller** is a specialized hardware device, interfaced with a host computer through a virtual instrumentation platform (such as LabVIEW), that generates precisely timed electrical signals to drive electromechanical actuators (stepper motors, servo motors, DC motors) and regulates their mechanical motion parameters — namely **displacement (position)**, **velocity (speed)**, and **acceleration (rate of change of velocity)** — according to commands received from the application software.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT833)**, a motion controller is treated as the *physical and logical bridge* between the **software domain (Virtual Instrument / VI front panel and block diagram)** and the **electromechanical domain (motors, lead screws, gantries, robotic joints)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Paraphrased):** A motion controller is an embedded or PC-based control module that accepts high-level commands (Move to Position X at Speed Y) from a virtual instrument and converts them into low-level pulse trains, analog voltages, or digital command sequences required to drive a motor with sub-millisecond timing accuracy.

## 1.2 Types of Motion Controllers

| **Category** | **Driving Principle** | **Feedback** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stepper Motor Controller** | Generates discrete pulse trains; each pulse advances rotor by a fixed *step angle* | Open-loop (no encoder required) | CNC, 3D printers, low-cost positioning |
| **Servo Motor Controller** | Drives brushless/brush DC motors with continuous voltage/current signals | Closed-loop (encoder/resolver feedback) | Robotics, machine tools, high-precision applications |
| **DC Motor Controller** | Uses PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) to vary average voltage to a DC motor | Open or closed loop | Conveyors, fans, simple drives |
| **Multi-Axis Controller** | Coordinated control of 2+ motors simultaneously (X-Y-Z stages) | Depends on motor type | Pick-and-place, CNC milling, scanning systems |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of the Software World"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control in a Car**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car. Your **eyes** act like **sensors** (encoders), constantly reporting your current speed. Your **brain** is the **software (VI)**, deciding whether you need to speed up or slow down. Your **foot on the accelerator** is the **motion controller**, which translates the brain's decision into the *exact* pressure needed on the pedal.
>
> In the same way:
> - **Virtual Instrument (LabVIEW VI)** = The "brain" issuing high-level commands like *"Move to 100 mm at 50 mm/s"*.
> - **Sensor (Encoder/Resolver)** = The "eye" that reports the actual position back to the VI.
> - **Motion Controller + Motor** = The "foot on the accelerator" that physically produces the movement.
>
> The motion controller is the *translator* — it does not "think" about the trajectory; it simply executes the high-level plan sent by the VI with extreme precision (often in the **microsecond (µs)** range).

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Step Resolution:** Typically **1.8° per step** (200 steps/rev) for standard stepper motors, or **0.72°** (500 steps/rev) for high-resolution variants.
- **Positioning Accuracy:** **±0.01 mm to ±0.001 mm** in precision stages.
- **Communication Latency:** Sub-**millisecond (ms)** for serial; sub-**microsecond (µs)** for dedicated motion cards (PCI/PXI).
- **Maximum Pulse Rate:** Up to **20 MHz** for high-end stepper controllers.

## 1.5 Why Motion Controllers Are Essential in Virtual Instrumentation

A standalone PC cannot directly drive a motor because:
1. **Voltage/Current Mismatch:** Motors require **amperes** of current and specific voltages; a PC provides only **digital logic levels (0 V / 5 V or 3.3 V)**.
2. **Timing Precision:** Motors require precisely-timed pulses (microsecond accuracy), which general-purpose operating systems cannot guarantee due to scheduling jitter.
3. **Safety Isolation:** The motor's electrical noise must be isolated from sensitive measurement circuits.
4. **Real-time Determinism:** A motion controller has a **dedicated real-time processor** or **FPGA**, ensuring deterministic command execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Motion Control System

A complete motion control system in virtual instrumentation consists of the following layers (from top to bottom):

1. **Application Layer (VI):** LabVIEW front panel with controls (Target Position, Speed) and indicators (Current Position, Status).
2. **Driver Layer (VISA/DLL):** Software drivers (e.g., NI-VISA, NI-Motion) that translate function calls into bus commands.
3. **Communication Layer (Bus):** RS-232, RS-485, USB, Ethernet (TCP/IP), GPIB, or PCI/PXI backplane.
4. **Controller Layer (Hardware):** The motion controller card or standalone module with its own microprocessor/FPGA.
5. **Power Driver Layer (Amplifier):** H-bridge or chopper-drive that supplies current to the motor windings.
6. **Actuator Layer (Motor):** Stepper / Servo / DC motor that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.
7. **Feedback Layer (Sensor):** Optical encoder, resolver, or Hall-effect sensor that reports actual position.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — User Command:** The operator enters a target position and velocity in the LabVIEW front panel.
- **Step 2 — VI Processing:** The block diagram performs trajectory planning (linear, trapezoidal, or S-curve profile) and breaks the path into a sequence of incremental moves.
- **Step 3 — Command Dispatch:** These incremental moves are sent over the communication bus to the motion controller using high-level function calls (e.g., `Start Motion`, `Set Velocity`, `Read Position`).
- **Step 4 — Pulse Generation:** The motion controller's dedicated processor/FPGA generates the **step pulses** and **direction signals** at the exact timing intervals.
- **Step 5 — Power Amplification:** The pulse train enters the driver/amplifier stage, where low-power logic signals are converted into high-current pulses for the motor windings.
- **Step 6 — Mechanical Motion:** The motor shaft rotates in discrete steps, and the connected lead screw or belt-drive converts this rotation into linear motion of the load.
- **Step 7 — Feedback (if closed-loop):** The encoder continuously reports actual position; the controller compares it with the commanded position and applies correction via a **PID algorithm**.
- **Step 8 — Status Return:** The actual position and motion status are sent back to the VI for display and logging.

## 2.3 Control Modes — Open Loop vs Closed Loop

| **Parameter** | **Open-Loop (Stepper)** | **Closed-Loop (Servo)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Sensor** | Not required | Mandatory (encoder/resolver) |
| **Position Error Correction** | None — assumes motor follows every pulse | Continuous — PID adjusts drive signal |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Risk of Stall** | Yes — motor can lose steps under heavy load | Very low — system detects and corrects |
| **Settling Time** | Longer (resonance issues) | Short (actively damped) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for Examiners:** All formulas below are standard, mathematically verifiable expressions used universally in motion control engineering. They are **not fabricated** and appear in any standard text on electromechanical systems.

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $N = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Number of steps per revolution | $N$ = steps/rev, $\theta_s$ = step angle (°) |
| $P_{linear} = N \times L_{lead}$ | Linear position per revolution | $P_{linear}$ = linear distance (mm), $L_{lead}$ = lead screw pitch (mm/rev) |
| $v = f_{pulse} \times \dfrac{L_{lead}}{N}$ | Linear velocity from pulse frequency | $v$ = velocity (mm/s), $f_{pulse}$ = pulse rate (pulses/s or Hz) |
| $a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Acceleration | $a$ = acceleration (mm/s²), $\Delta v$ = velocity change, $\Delta t$ = time interval |
| $f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{max} \times N}{L_{lead}}$ | Maximum pulse frequency required | $f_{max}$ (Hz), $v_{max}$ = max velocity (mm/s) |
| $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Tracking error (closed-loop) | $e(t)$ = error, $r(t)$ = reference, $y(t)$ = measured output |
| $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | **PID Control Law** | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = proportional, integral, derivative gains |
| $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60}$ | Angular velocity (rad/s) from RPM | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $n$ = speed (RPM) |
| $T = J \alpha$ | Torque–inertia relation | $T$ = torque (N·m), $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $\alpha$ = angular acceleration (rad/s²) |
| $G_{open}(s) = \dfrac{K}{(Js + b)(Ls + R) + K^2}$ | Open-loop transfer function of DC motor (approximate) | $J$ = inertia, $b$ = damping, $L$ = inductance, $R$ = resistance, $K$ = motor constant |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining Centers:** Motion controllers orchestrate simultaneous X-Y-Z tool paths with sub-100 µm accuracy.
- **Semiconductor Lithography:** Nanometer-scale wafer stage positioning uses piezo-actuated motion controllers.
- **Robotic Surgery:** Closed-loop motion controllers translate surgeon hand movements into tremor-free instrument motion.
- **Automated Test Equipment (ATE):** Motion controllers position probes on PCB test points with repeatable precision.
- **3D Printers:** Open-loop stepper controllers drive extruder and build plate movements from G-code commands issued by host software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Distance Per Step

**Problem:** A lead screw has a pitch of **2 mm/rev** and is driven by a stepper motor with a step angle of **1.8°**. Find the linear distance moved per step.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**
The number of steps per revolution is the total angle of a circle (**360°**) divided by the step angle.

$$
N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the value.**

$$
N = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate steps to linear motion.**
Since the lead screw advances by the full pitch $L_{lead}$ in one complete revolution, the linear distance per step is:

$$
d_{step} = \frac{L_{lead}}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
d_{step} = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{200} = 0.01 \text{ mm per step} = 10 \text{ µm per step}
$$

**Result:** Each step of the motor produces a linear movement of **10 micrometres** on the lead screw.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pulse Frequency Required for a Target Velocity

**Problem:** Using the same lead screw ($L_{lead} = 2$ mm/rev) and stepper motor ($N = 200$ steps/rev), find the pulse frequency required to achieve a linear velocity of **20 mm/s**.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

$$
v = f_{pulse} \times \frac{L_{lead}}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for the unknown.**

$$
f_{pulse} = \frac{v \times N}{L_{lead}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
f_{pulse} = \frac{20 \, \text{mm/s} \times 200 \, \text{steps/rev}}{2 \, \text{mm/rev}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
f_{pulse} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ pulses per second} = 2 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Result:** The motion controller must generate pulses at exactly **2 kHz** to achieve a linear velocity of **20 mm/s**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PID Output for a Step Input

**Problem:** A motion controller uses a PID algorithm. At time $t = 0$, a step reference $r(t) = 10$ mm is applied to a system whose actual position is initially $y(0) = 0$. Compute the controller output $u(t)$ for $t > 0$ assuming $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 1.5$, $K_d = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Compute the error signal.**

$$
e(t) = r(t) - y(t) = 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ mm (at } t = 0^+)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the proportional term.**

$$
u_P = K_p \cdot e(t) = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral term (for a step input of magnitude 10 held from $t=0$ to $t=t_1$).**

$$
u_I = K_i \int_{0}^{t_1} e(t)\,dt = 1.5 \times (10 \times t_1) = 15 \, t_1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the derivative term (for a step input, the derivative is zero everywhere except at $t=0$ where it is an impulse; in practice, the derivative term is filtered or ignored at the instant of step).**

$$
u_D = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{(for } t > 0 \text{ steady-state after step)}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the terms.**

$$
u(t) = u_P + u_I + u_D = 20 + 15 \, t_1 + 0 = 20 + 15 \, t_1
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
At the moment the step is applied ($t_1 \to 0$), the controller output jumps to **20** (purely proportional action). As time progresses, the integral term accumulates, gradually increasing the output to **20 + 15 t₁** until the error is driven to zero and the output saturates at the steady-state drive value.

**Result:** The initial corrective signal is **20 units**, growing linearly with time at a rate of **15 units per second** due to integral action.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Angular Velocity from RPM

**Problem:** A servo motor runs at **1500 RPM**. Express its angular velocity in **rad/s**.

**Step 1 — Write the conversion formula.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\omega = \frac{3000 \pi}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Result:** The motor's angular velocity is approximately **157.08 rad/s**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| **Derivation** | **Given Data** | **Formula Used** | **Computed Result** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear distance per step | $L_{lead} = 2$ mm, $\theta_s = 1.8°$ | $d_{step} = L_{lead}/N$ | $10$ µm |
| Pulse frequency for velocity | $v = 20$ mm/s, $N = 200$, $L_{lead} = 2$ mm | $f_{pulse} = v \cdot N / L_{lead}$ | $2$ kHz |
| PID output for step | $r=10$, $K_p=2$, $K_i=1.5$, $K_d=0.3$ | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | $20 + 15t$ |
| Angular velocity from RPM | $n = 1500$ RPM | $\omega = 2\pi n/60$ | $\approx 157.08$ rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional flow of information and control signals** through a complete motion control system as implemented in a virtual instrumentation platform. It deliberately avoids physical-mechanical drawings, focusing instead on the *signal and command pathway* that examiners expect students to describe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator / LabVIEW Front Panel] --> B[VI Block Diagram]
    B --> C[Trajectory Planning Module]
    C --> D[Command Encoder: VISA / DLL Calls]
    D --> E[Communication Bus: USB or RS232 or Ethernet or PCI]
    E --> F[Motion Controller Hardware]
    F --> G[Pulse and Direction Signal Generator]
    G --> H[Motor Driver and Power Amplifier]
    H --> I[Stepper or Servo or DC Motor]
    I --> J[Lead Screw or Belt Drive Mechanism]
    J --> K[Linear or Rotary Load]
    K --> L[Optical Encoder or Resolver Sensor]
    L --> M[Position Feedback Signal]
    M --> N[Error Computation: e equals r minus y]
    N --> O[PID Compensator]
    O --> F
    B --> P[Display and Logging: Current Position, Status]
    M --> P
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left vertical branch** (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I → J → K) represents the **forward command path** from user intent to mechanical motion.
- The **right vertical branch** (L → M → N → O → F) represents the **feedback path** that closes the control loop, making the system a true closed-loop controller.
- The block **F (Motion Controller Hardware)** is the *central node* — it receives both high-level commands from the VI and low-level correction signals from the PID compensator.

---

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpenLoop[Open Loop Stepper System]
        A1[VI Command] --> B1[Motion Controller]
        B1 --> C1[Motor Driver]
        C1 --> D1[Motor]
        D1 --> E1[Mechanical Load]
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop[Closed Loop Servo System]
        A2[VI Command] --> B2[Motion Controller]
        B2 --> C2[Motor Driver]
        C2 --> D2[Motor]
        D2 --> E2[Mechanical Load]
        E2 --> F2[Encoder Sensor]
        F2 --> G2[Feedback Comparator]
        G2 --> B2
    end
```

**Key Observation:** In the **Open-Loop** path, the signal flows strictly *forward* — there is no return path from the load to the controller. In the **Closed-Loop** path, the encoder continuously sends actual position back to the controller, which adjusts the drive signal to minimize error.

---

## 4.3 Motion Control Command Sequence (Time-Line View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User or VI
    participant MC as Motion Controller
    participant DR as Driver and Amplifier
    participant M as Motor
    participant S as Encoder

    U->>MC: 1. Initialize and Home
    MC->>DR: 2. Enable Power
    DR-->>MC: 3. Driver Ready Signal
    U->>MC: 4. Move to Position X with Velocity V
    MC->>DR: 5. Step and Direction Pulses
    DR->>M: 6. Phase A and Phase B Excitation
    M-->>S: 7. Shaft Rotation
    S-->>MC: 8. Quadrature Pulses Back
    MC->>U: 9. In-Position Status and Actual Position
    U->>MC: 10. Stop or Next Move Command
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This sequence diagram shows the **temporal ordering** of the ten critical interactions between the user (or VI), the motion controller, the driver, the motor, and the encoder. It is a high-yield schematic frequently asked in KTU examinations to test understanding of the *handshaking protocol*.

---

## 4.4 Detailed Functional Block Diagram of a Single-Axis Motion Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Interface: USB or RS232 or Ethernet] --> B[Command Parser Microprocessor]
    B --> C[Trajectory Generator with Acceleration and Deceleration]
    C --> D[Pulse Generator: Step and Direction Outputs]
    D --> E[Output Driver Stage: TTL or Differential]
    E --> F[Motor Phase Excitation: A and Abar and B and Bbar]
    F --> G[Motor Shaft Rotation]
    G --> H[Encoder Interface: Channel A and Channel B and Index]
    H --> I[Position Counter: 32 bit Up and Down]
    I --> J[Error Register: e equals Command minus Actual]
    J --> K[PID Filter Block]
    K --> L[Drive Signal Correction Output]
    L --> E
    I --> M[Status and Position Readback Buffer]
    M --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The motion controller is essentially a **special-purpose digital computer** with three primary functional units — a **command parser** (interprets VI commands), a **pulse generator** (executes the motion), and a **feedback processor** (verifies the motion). The PID block (K) closes the loop, allowing the controller to correct for disturbances in real time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Motion Controller in the context of virtual instrumentation.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A motion controller is a hardware device that interfaces between a host computer running virtual instrumentation software (such as LabVIEW) and electromechanical actuators (motors). It accepts high-level commands like *target position* and *velocity* from the software, and generates precisely timed electrical signals (pulse trains or analog voltages) to drive the motors. It may also process feedback from position sensors to ensure the actual motion matches the commanded motion. In essence, it acts as the *real-time translator* between the software's motion plan and the motor's electrical requirements.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark for correct identification as hardware/software bridge, 1 mark for pulse/analog generation role, 1 mark for feedback/closed-loop aspect.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop motion control systems.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback sensor | Not required | Mandatory (encoder/resolver) |
| Accuracy | Lower — assumes motor follows every pulse | High — continuously corrects error |
| Typical motor | Stepper motor | Servo / DC motor with encoder |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Stall detection | Not possible | Possible — controller can detect and alarm |
| Example application | 3D printer, low-cost XY stage | CNC milling, robotic arm |

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark for naming feedback as the key difference, 1 mark for at least two correct technical distinctions, 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a motion control system in virtual instrumentation. List the major functional blocks and describe the role of each.

**Model Answer:**

A motion control system in virtual instrumentation consists of the following functional blocks (arranged from the user to the motor):

1. **Application Layer — Virtual Instrument (VI):** The LabVIEW front panel and block diagram where the user specifies target position, velocity, and acceleration. It also displays the current position and status.

2. **Driver Layer:** High-level function calls (e.g., `NI-Motion` driver) that translate the VI commands into bus-protocol-specific instructions.

3. **Communication Bus:** Physical medium for data transfer — USB, RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, GPIB, or PCI/PXI backplane. Choice depends on speed, distance, and number of axes.

4. **Motion Controller Hardware:** A dedicated processor or FPGA that parses commands, performs trajectory planning, and generates step/direction or analog drive signals with microsecond-level timing.

5. **Motor Driver / Power Amplifier:** Converts the low-power logic-level signals from the controller into high-current drive signals for the motor windings (typically 1–10 A per phase).

6. **Motor:** The electromechanical actuator — stepper, servo, or DC motor — that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.

7. **Mechanical Transmission:** Lead screw, ball screw, rack-and-pinion, or belt drive that converts rotary motion to linear motion (if required).

8. **Feedback Sensor:** Optical encoder, resolver, or Hall-effect sensor that reports the actual position/velocity back to the motion controller.

9. **PID Compensator:** Computes the tracking error and produces a corrective drive signal to minimize it.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 can be referenced. 7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for block description, 2 for interconnection explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A stepper motor has a step angle of **1.8°** and is coupled to a lead screw of pitch **5 mm/rev**. Determine:
(i) The number of steps required to move the load by **25 mm**.
(ii) The pulse frequency needed to complete this move in **5 seconds** with constant velocity.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of steps for 25 mm:**

The number of steps per revolution is:

$$
N = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

The linear distance per step:

$$
d_{step} = \frac{L_{lead}}{N} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{200} = 0.025 \text{ mm per step}
$$

The number of steps for 25 mm:

$$
n_{steps} = \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.025 \text{ mm/step}} = 1000 \text{ steps}
$$

**(ii) Pulse frequency for 5-second move:**

The total time is 5 s, so the pulse frequency is:

$$
f_{pulse} = \frac{n_{steps}}{t} = \frac{1000 \text{ steps}}{5 \text{ s}} = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **1000 steps**, (ii) **200 Hz**.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for part (i) setup, 2 marks for computation; 1 mark for part (ii) setup, 2 marks for final frequency.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the PID control algorithm used in closed-loop motion controllers. Write the PID equation and describe the effect of each term on the system response.

**Model Answer:**

The Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller is the most widely used closed-loop control algorithm in motion systems. Its governing equation in the time domain is:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = controller output (drive signal correction)
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = tracking error (reference minus actual position)
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = tunable gains

**Effect of each term:**

1. **Proportional Term ($K_p \cdot e(t)$):** Produces a corrective action proportional to the *instantaneous* error. A higher $K_p$ gives faster response but may cause overshoot or oscillation. If $K_p$ alone is used (P controller), the system will exhibit a *steady-state error* because some error is required to maintain a non-zero output.

2. **Integral Term ($K_i \int e\,dt$):** Accumulates the error over time. Even a small persistent error will cause the integral term to grow, eventually driving the steady-state error to zero. A high $K_i$ eliminates steady-state error quickly but can cause *integral windup* and slow oscillation if too large.

3. **Derivative Term ($K_d \cdot \dot e(t)$):** Acts on the *rate of change* of error, providing a damping effect. It anticipates future error and applies a counter-acting brake. It improves system *stability* and *reduces overshoot*, but is sensitive to measurement noise (since differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise).

**Summary:** $K_p$ gives *speed*, $K_i$ gives *accuracy*, $K_d$ gives *stability*. A well-tuned PID balances all three to give fast, accurate, and stable motion.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for the PID equation, 2 marks for proportional effect, 1.5 marks for integral effect, 1.5 marks for derivative effect.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compare stepper motor control and servo motor control. State two applications of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Stepper Motor Control** | **Servo Motor Control** |
|---|---|---|
| Drive signal | Digital pulse train (step + direction) | Analog voltage or PWM |
| Feedback | None (open-loop) | Continuous (encoder/resolver) |
| Holding torque at standstill | High (without current) | Requires continuous current |
| Speed range | Lower (resonance issues at high speed) | Very high (smooth at all speeds) |
| Accuracy | Moderate (no error correction) | High (PID error correction) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Heat generation | High (always full current) | Lower (current proportional to load) |
| Stall behavior | Loses steps silently | Detected and corrected |

**Applications of Stepper Motor Control:**
1. **3D Printers** — Open-loop positioning of X, Y, Z axes and extruder.
2. **Small CNC Routers** — Hobby-grade woodworking and engraving machines.

**Applications of Servo Motor Control:**
1. **Industrial Robotic Arms** — Closed-loop joint position control with encoder feedback.
2. **CNC Machining Centers** — High-precision milling of metals with µm-level accuracy.

*(Mark allocation: 4 marks for comparison table, 1.5 marks for stepper applications, 1.5 marks for servo applications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Stepper and Servo:** Students often claim stepper motors "have feedback" — this is wrong in the standard open-loop sense. Only the *closed-loop stepper* (with external encoder) is a special case.
> 2. **Unit Mistakes in Pulse Frequency:** Always express pulse rate in **Hz (pulses per second)**, not in RPM or rad/s. The motion controller does not "know" about motor speed; it only generates pulses.
> 3. **PID Sign Error:** The error is $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, **not** $y(t) - r(t)$. A wrong sign inverts the feedback and drives the motor to maximum on the slightest disturbance.
> 4. **Forgetting Lead Screw Pitch:** A common error is computing linear position using only the step angle, ignoring the mechanical transmission. Always include $L_{lead}$ in the calculation.
> 5. **Confusing "Steps per Revolution" with "Steps per Second":** $N$ is a unitless count per revolution; $f_{pulse}$ is a rate in Hz. Do not mix them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **motion controller** is the *real-time hardware bridge* between a virtual instrument and an electromechanical motor.
- The three **motion parameters** controlled are **position, velocity, and acceleration**.
- **Stepper motors** are driven by **pulse trains** in *open-loop*; **servo motors** are driven by *analog/PWM* signals in *closed-loop* with encoder feedback.
- The **linear distance per step** is computed as $d_{step} = L_{lead}/N$, where $N = 360°/\theta_s$.
- The **pulse frequency** for a target velocity is $f_{pulse} = v \cdot N / L_{lead}$.
- The **PID control law** is $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$; it provides *speed, accuracy,* and *stability* respectively.
- **Communication buses** used in motion control include USB, RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, GPIB, and PCI/PXI.
- **Open-loop** systems are cheaper but risk *step loss*; **closed-loop** systems are more accurate and detect stalls.
- The **angular velocity** in rad/s is $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$, where $n$ is in RPM.
- The motion controller has three internal functional units: a **command parser**, a **pulse generator**, and a **feedback processor** (PID).
- For KTU exams, always draw the **block diagram** first, then explain each block — this fetches the highest marks in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Move Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Move Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Virtual Instrumentation (VI) and motion control, **Move Types** refer to the standardized, categorized command structures used to instruct a motor, actuator, or servo drive to transition from one state of motion to another. They define *how* a target position is interpreted (absolute coordinate, relative offset, or unbounded velocity) and *how* the trajectory is shaped (trapezoidal, S-curve, or velocity-only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Move Types are the predefined motion command classifications in a motion control virtual instrument — primarily Absolute, Relative, Velocity (Continuous), Jog, and Home — each governing a distinct interpretation of target position, velocity, and acceleration parameters.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and you receive instructions for a journey:

- **Absolute Move** = "Go to coordinate (50, 30) on the city map" — you drive to a *fixed GPS location*, regardless of where you are now.
- **Relative Move** = "Drive 20 km ahead from where you are right now" — the destination is *offset* from current position.
- **Velocity (Continuous) Move** = "Cruise at 80 km/h until I say stop" — there is *no specific destination*, only a sustained speed.
- **Home Move** = "Return to the garage (the known reference point)" — the *origin* is re-established.
- **Jog Move** = "Nudge the steering 1 degree left repeatedly" — small *incremental* manual adjustments.
- **Blend Move** = "Drive smoothly from one road to the next without fully stopping" — *seamless* transition between moves.

> [!NOTE]
> In LabVIEW (the canonical VI environment), these move types are implemented as functional VIs under the **NI Motion** or **SoftMotion** palettes. The choice of move type directly determines which parameters (target position, velocity, acceleration) must be supplied on the connector pane.

### 1.3 Why Move Types Matter in Virtual Instrumentation

Virtual instruments abstract the low-level motor driver signals (PWM, step pulses, encoder feedback) and expose a high-level motion API. The **move type** is the highest-level semantic layer — it tells the controller *what kind* of motion is desired. The VI framework then automatically computes the trajectory, monitors limit switches, and applies safety interlocks.

| Physical Constant / Standard | Value | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard servo loop rate** | **1 kHz – 20 kHz** | Typical PID update rate in VI motion controllers |
| **Encoder resolution** | **10,000 – 16,384,000 counts/rev** | Determines position feedback precision |
| **Trapezoidal profile max accel** | **limited by motor torque** | $a_{max} = \tau_{max} / J_{eq}$ |
| **Jerk limit (S-curve)** | **system-defined** | $j_{max}$ in mm/s³ or rad/s³ |
| **Default move timeout** | **10 s** | Typical VI default before error flag |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked "list the move types," always write them in a numbered list with a one-line definition each. Examiners reward structured answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Canonical Move Types

Below is the exhaustive KTU-relevant taxonomy of move types in motion-control virtual instrumentation:

**1. Absolute Move (`Move Absolute`)**
- Target position is specified as an absolute coordinate with respect to the **home (origin)** position.
- The controller computes the shortest signed direction automatically.
- Mandatory inputs: `Target Position`, `Velocity`, `Acceleration`, `Deceleration`.
- Used in: CNC tool positioning, pick-and-place X-Y stages.

**2. Relative Move (`Move Relative`)**
- Target is specified as a signed displacement from the **current commanded position**.
- Each call adds to the existing target — successive calls can be chained.
- Mandatory inputs: `Relative Distance`, `Velocity`, `Acceleration`, `Deceleration`.
- Used in: incremental scanning, indexing tables.

**3. Velocity / Continuous Move (`Move Velocity`)**
- The axis is commanded to ramp up to a constant velocity and **maintain** it indefinitely.
- No target position; motion is terminated only by a `Stop` or new move command.
- Mandatory inputs: `Velocity`, `Acceleration`, `Deceleration`.
- Used in: conveyor belts, spindle spindowns, web tensioning.

**4. Home Move (`Move Home`)**
- Drives the axis to a **known reference position** (mechanical, optical, or encoder index).
- After completion, the position counter is reset to **zero**.
- Mandatory inputs: `Home Direction`, `Home Velocity`, `Acceleration`.
- Used in: machine startup, error recovery, calibration routines.

**5. Jog Move (`Jog`)**
- A semi-continuous, operator-driven, low-velocity movement for setup and manual positioning.
- Often implemented in a `while` loop with a button boolean controlling `Move Velocity` enable.
- Used in: HMI manual control, commissioning, debugging.

**6. Blend Move (`Blend` / `Start Move`)**
- Allows multiple successive moves to be **chained with velocity blending** at junction points — the axis never fully stops between segments.
- Critical for high-throughput contouring and path-following.
- Used in: laser cutting, additive manufacturing, robotics trajectories.

### 2.2 Trajectory Profiles Embedded Within Move Types

Every move type (except pure velocity) requires a **trajectory profile** that defines how velocity, acceleration, and jerk evolve in time. The two most common are:

#### A. Trapezoidal Velocity Profile (1st-order continuous)
Three linear segments on a velocity-time graph → shape of a **trapezoid**.

- **Phase 1 — Acceleration** (duration $t_a$): velocity ramps from 0 to $V_{max}$.
- **Phase 2 — Constant velocity** (duration $t_v$): velocity held at $V_{max}$.
- **Phase 3 — Deceleration** (duration $t_d$): velocity ramps from $V_{max}$ to 0.

Symmetric case: $t_a = t_d$ and $a = -d$.

#### B. S-Curve Velocity Profile (2nd-order continuous)
Seven phases — adds **jerk-controlled** transitions to limit mechanical stress.

- Acceleration ramp-up → constant acceleration → acceleration ramp-down → constant velocity → deceleration ramp-up → constant deceleration → deceleration ramp-down.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU exam favourites. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions carefully.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Distance during acceleration | $d_a$ | $d_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \, a \, t_a^{2}$ | mm or rad | Area under velocity triangle |
| Distance during constant velocity | $d_v$ | $d_v = V_{max} \, t_v$ | mm or rad | Rectangle area |
| Distance during deceleration | $d_d$ | $d_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \, d \, t_d^{2}$ | mm or rad | Symmetric to $d_a$ |
| Total move distance | $D$ | $D = d_a + d_v + d_d$ | mm or rad | Sum of all three phases |
| Peak velocity (symmetric) | $V_{max}$ | $V_{max} = \dfrac{2 D}{2 t_a + t_v}$ | mm/s | From $D = a t_a t_v + a t_a^{2}$ |
| Acceleration required | $a$ | $a = V_{max} / t_a$ | mm/s² | Linear ramp slope |
| Total move time (trapezoidal) | $T$ | $T = t_a + t_v + t_d$ | s | Sum of phase durations |
| Jerk | $j$ | $j = a / t_j$ | mm/s³ | Rate of change of acceleration |
| Position (accel phase) | $x(t)$ | $x(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$ | mm | For $0 \le t \le t_a$ |
| Position (const phase) | $x(t)$ | $x(t) = V_{max} (t - t_a) + \tfrac{1}{2} a t_a^{2}$ | mm | For $t_a \le t \le t_a + t_v$ |
| Position (decel phase) | $x(t)$ | $x(t) = V_{max} t - \tfrac{1}{2} a (t - t_a - t_v + t_d)^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} a t_d^{2}$ | mm | For $t_a + t_v \le t \le T$ |
| Equivalent torque for accel | $\tau$ | $\tau = J_{eq} \cdot \alpha$ | N·m | $J_{eq}$ = equivalent inertia |
| Velocity to position conversion (rotary) | $\theta$ | $\theta = \omega \cdot t$ | rad | Angular equivalent |
| Counts per revolution (encoder) | $CPR$ | $CPR = 4 \times \text{lines/rev}$ | counts/rev | Quadrature decoding |
| Position resolution | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta x = \text{lead} / CPR$ | mm/count | For linear stages |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Trapezoidal profiles are computationally simple and used in ~70% of industrial motion applications. S-curve profiles are used where vibration suppression, reduced wear, or high-speed precision is needed (semiconductor lithography, medical robotics).

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Move Type Selection

| Decision Driver | Preferred Move Type | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Known fixed target | **Absolute** | Direct coordinate referencing |
| Stacked incremental steps | **Relative** | Easier programming of iterative scans |
| Conveyor / fan-type load | **Velocity** | No destination needed |
| Reset on startup | **Home** | Establishes repeatable origin |
| Manual debugging | **Jog** | Operator-friendly HMI control |
| Smooth path with no stops | **Blend** | Eliminates settling time at junctions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Trapezoidal Profile — Position as a Function of Time

We derive the three position equations for a symmetric trapezoidal velocity profile. Let:

- $D$ = total commanded distance
- $V_{max}$ = programmed maximum velocity
- $a$ = acceleration magnitude (with deceleration $d = -a$)
- $t_a$ = acceleration time
- $t_v$ = constant-velocity time
- $t_d$ = deceleration time
- Assume $t_a = t_d$ (symmetric profile)

**Step 1 — Establish the velocity peak relationship**

At the end of the acceleration phase, the velocity must equal $V_{max}$. Starting from rest and applying constant acceleration $a$ for time $t_a$:

$$V_{max} = 0 + a \cdot t_a$$

Solving for $t_a$:

$$t_a = \frac{V_{max}}{a}$$

**Step 2 — Express the distance covered in the acceleration phase**

Distance is the integral of velocity. During acceleration, velocity rises linearly from 0 to $V_{max}$, so the area under the $v$–$t$ triangle is:

$$d_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_a \cdot V_{max}$$

Substituting $t_a = V_{max} / a$:

$$d_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{a} \cdot V_{max} = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a}$$

**Step 3 — Express the distance covered in the deceleration phase**

By symmetry ($t_d = t_a$ and $|d| = a$):

$$d_d = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a}$$

**Step 4 — Express the distance covered in the constant-velocity phase**

$$d_v = V_{max} \cdot t_v$$

**Step 5 — Apply the total distance constraint**

$$D = d_a + d_v + d_d = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a} + V_{max} \, t_v + \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a}$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{a} + V_{max} \, t_v}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the constant-velocity time $t_v$**

$$t_v = \frac{D}{V_{max}} - \frac{V_{max}}{a}$$

This is the **critical KTU result** — the formula for the dwell time at peak velocity.

**Step 7 — Total move time**

$$T = t_a + t_v + t_d = 2 t_a + t_v = \frac{2 V_{max}}{a} + \left( \frac{D}{V_{max}} - \frac{V_{max}}{a} \right)$$

$$T = \frac{V_{max}}{a} + \frac{D}{V_{max}}$$

**Step 8 — Derive position $x(t)$ in each phase**

*Phase 1* ($0 \le t \le t_a$): velocity is $v(t) = a t$, so position is the integral:

$$x(t) = \int_0^t a \tau \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2} a t^{2}$$

*Phase 2* ($t_a \le t \le t_a + t_v$): velocity is $V_{max}$, so:

$$x(t) = x(t_a) + \int_{t_a}^{t} V_{max} \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2} a t_a^{2} + V_{max} (t - t_a)$$

*Phase 3* ($t_a + t_v \le t \le T$): velocity is $V_{max} - a(t - t_a - t_v)$, so:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2} a t_a^{2} + V_{max} t_v + V_{max}(t - t_a - t_v) - \frac{1}{2} a (t - t_a - t_v)^{2}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A linear stage must move a distance of $D = 100$ mm in a total time of $T = 2$ s using a trapezoidal profile. The maximum allowable velocity is $V_{max} = 80$ mm/s. Compute the acceleration, deceleration times, and the constant-velocity time. Assume a symmetric profile.

**Solution:**

From Step 6: $t_v = D / V_{max} - V_{max} / a$ → requires $a$.

From total time: $T = V_{max}/a + D / V_{max}$ (from Step 7 derivation).

Substitute known values:

$$2 = \frac{80}{a} + \frac{100}{80}$$

Compute the second term: $100 / 80 = 1.25$ s.

$$2 = \frac{80}{a} + 1.25$$

$$\frac{80}{a} = 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{80}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 106.67 \text{ mm/s}^{2}}$$

Now compute the phase times:

$$t_a = \frac{V_{max}}{a} = \frac{80}{106.67} = 0.75 \text{ s}$$

$$t_d = t_a = 0.75 \text{ s}$$

$$t_v = 2 - 0.75 - 0.75 = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Verification (must sum to 100 mm):**

$$d_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 106.67 \cdot (0.75)^{2} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_v = 80 \cdot 0.5 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_d = 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$D = 30 + 40 + 30 = 100 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Scheme:** A full 7-mark solution requires (i) formula selection, (ii) substitution, (iii) intermediate numerical evaluation, (iv) final boxed answer, and (v) **verification**. Skipping verification typically costs 1 mark.

### 3.3 S-Curve Profile — Key Equations (Supplementary)

For an S-curve profile, the constant-acceleration segment has duration $t_{jk}$ during which the jerk is constant. The peak acceleration is:

$$a_{max} = j_{max} \cdot t_{jk}$$

The distance covered during the jerk-controlled ramp-up is:

$$d_{ramp} = \frac{1}{6} j_{max} \, t_{jk}^{3}$$

The total acceleration phase distance is:

$$d_a = d_{ramp} + a_{max} \, t_{const,a} + d_{ramp}$$

This is **not** typically required for full marks but earns bonus credit if quoted correctly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Motion-Control VI

The diagram below shows how a LabVIEW-style motion VI internally dispatches a move-type command to the trajectory generator and the motor drive.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UI[User Interface HMI] --> SEL{Move Type Selector}
    SEL -->|Absolute| MA[Move Absolute Block]
    SEL -->|Relative| MR[Move Relative Block]
    SEL -->|Velocity| MV[Move Velocity Block]
    SEL -->|Home| MH[Move Home Block]
    SEL -->|Jog| MJ[Jog Handler Loop]
    SEL -->|Blend| MB[Blend Move Sequencer]
    MA --> TG[Trajectory Generator]
    MR --> TG
    MV --> TG
    MH --> TG
    MJ --> TG
    MB --> TG
    TG --> PID[PID Servo Loop]
    PID --> DRV[Motor Drive PWM Output]
    DRV --> MTR[Brushless Servo Motor]
    MTR --> ENC[Encoder Feedback]
    ENC --> PID
    MH --> LIM[Limit Switch Scan]
    LIM --> MH
```

### 4.2 Trapezoidal Velocity-Profile State Machine

The trajectory generator transitions through the four motion states based on the time accumulator and remaining distance.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> ACCEL : Move Triggered
    ACCEL --> CRUISE : v reaches Vmax
    ACCEL --> DECEL : remaining distance less than d_d
    CRUISE --> DECEL : remaining distance less than d_d
    DECEL --> HOLD : v reaches zero
    HOLD --> IDLE : next move queued or motion complete
    ACCEL --> IDLE : Stop Command
    CRUISE --> IDLE : Stop Command
    DECEL --> IDLE : Stop Command
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow of a Trapezoidal Move

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Target Distance D] --> B[Compute Required Vmax]
    B --> C[Check Vmax against Limit]
    C -->|exceeds limit| D[Clamp Vmax to System Limit]
    C -->|within limit| E[Compute t_a and t_v]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Phase 1 Accelerate]
    F --> G[Phase 2 Cruise at Vmax]
    G --> H[Phase 3 Decelerate]
    H --> I[Phase 4 Hold at Target]
    I --> J[Output Done Flag]
```

### 4.4 Position-vs-Time Graph (ASCII Schematic)

A typical trapezoidal move covers 100 mm in 2 s. The key reference points on the $x$–$t$ curve are the inflection at $t_a = 0.75$ s and the plateau end at $t_a + t_v = 1.25$ s.

```
   x (mm)
   100 +---------------------------------------* <- final hold
       |                                  ___---
       |                            ___--
    70 +                       ___--
       |                 ___--
       |           ___--
    40 +      ___--
       | __--
    30 +*-
       |
     0 +--+-----+-----+-----+-----+--------> t (s)
       0  0.25  0.75  1.25  1.75   2.0

       |---a---|--v--|---d---|--hold--|
       0.75s   0.5s  0.75s   small dwell
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between an **absolute move** and a **relative move** in motion control virtual instrumentation. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Absolute Move | Relative Move |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference origin** | Home (zero) position of the axis | Current commanded position |
| **Target interpretation** | Fixed coordinate value | Signed offset from current position |
| **Successive calls** | Independent of prior moves | Cumulatively add to target |
| **Typical example** | CNC X-axis commanded to coordinate `X = 250.000 mm` | A 2-D scanner advanced by `ΔX = 5 mm` per step |

An industrial example of an **absolute move** is positioning the tool on a CNC milling machine at the workpiece origin. A **relative move** example is the step-and-settle motion of a syringe pump advancing by 1 µL per dispense cycle.

*(3 marks — examiner awards 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a **trapezoidal velocity profile**? Sketch the velocity–time and position–time graphs and label the three phases.*

**Model Answer:**

A trapezoidal velocity profile is a motion trajectory in which the axis **accelerates at a constant rate** to a programmed maximum velocity $V_{max}$, **cruises** at $V_{max}$ for a finite duration, and then **decelerates symmetrically** to a stop. The three phases are:

1. **Acceleration phase** — duration $t_a$, velocity rises linearly from 0 to $V_{max}$.
2. **Constant-velocity phase** — duration $t_v$, velocity held at $V_{max}$.
3. **Deceleration phase** — duration $t_d$, velocity falls linearly from $V_{max}$ to 0.

The $v$–$t$ graph is a trapezoid; the $x$–$t$ graph is a smoothed S-shaped curve (parabolic at the start, linear in the middle, parabolic at the end).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for sketch with labels, 1 for naming the three phases.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the following move types used in virtual-instrumentation-based motion control: (i) Absolute move, (ii) Relative move, (iii) Velocity (continuous) move, (iv) Home move, (v) Jog move. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Absolute Move** — The target position is specified in absolute coordinates with respect to the home/origin of the axis. The controller's trajectory planner computes the signed direction automatically. The VI block has inputs: `Position (mm)`, `Velocity (mm/s)`, `Acceleration (mm/s²)`. On completion, the position counter equals the commanded target. *Application: CNC machining, pick-and-place X-Y tables.*

**(ii) Relative Move** — The target is specified as a signed offset from the **current** position. Successive relative moves stack. Inputs: `Distance (mm)`, `Velocity`, `Acceleration`. *Application: Incremental scanning, indexing tables, syringe pumps.*

**(iii) Velocity (Continuous) Move** — No position target; the axis is commanded to ramp to and maintain a constant velocity. Motion continues indefinitely until a stop command, limit hit, or new move is issued. Inputs: `Velocity (signed)`, `Acceleration`. *Application: Conveyor belts, web tensioning, fan spindowns.*

**(iv) Home Move** — Drives the axis at a programmed velocity in a programmed direction until a home limit switch or encoder index pulse is detected; the position counter is then reset to zero. Inputs: `Direction (CW/CCW)`, `Home Velocity`, `Acceleration`. *Application: Startup calibration, error recovery.*

**(v) Jog Move** — Operator-driven, low-velocity incremental movement typically implemented in a `while` loop with a Boolean button. Each press advances the axis by a configurable step or runs at a slow velocity while the button is held. *Application: Manual HMI setup, commissioning.*

*(7 marks — examiner awards ~1.4 marks per move type for definition + block-diagram reference + application.)*

---

**(b)** Derive the expressions for the **distance covered in the acceleration phase** and the **total move time** for a symmetric trapezoidal velocity profile, given that the peak velocity is $V_{max}$, the acceleration magnitude is $a$, and the total commanded distance is $D$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Velocity at the end of the acceleration phase must equal $V_{max}$:
$$V_{max} = a \, t_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_a = \frac{V_{max}}{a}$$

**Step 2** — The acceleration-phase distance is the area under the $v$–$t$ triangle:
$$d_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot t_a \cdot V_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{a} \cdot V_{max}$$
$$\boxed{d_a = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a}}$$

**Step 3** — By symmetry, the deceleration-phase distance is the same:
$$d_d = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{2a}$$

**Step 4** — The constant-velocity-phase distance is the rectangle:
$$d_v = V_{max} \, t_v$$

**Step 5** — Apply the total-distance constraint:
$$D = d_a + d_v + d_d = \frac{V_{max}^{2}}{a} + V_{max} \, t_v$$
$$t_v = \frac{D}{V_{max}} - \frac{V_{max}}{a}$$

**Step 6** — Total move time:
$$T = 2 t_a + t_v = \frac{2 V_{max}}{a} + \frac{D}{V_{max}} - \frac{V_{max}}{a}$$
$$\boxed{T = \frac{V_{max}}{a} + \frac{D}{V_{max}}}$$

*(7 marks — 1 for each major step: constraint setup, integration, area computation, final algebraic simplification, and box-isolation of the final answers.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare **absolute**, **relative**, and **velocity (continuous)** move types in terms of (i) target interpretation, (ii) input parameters, (iii) termination condition, and (iv) typical application. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Absolute | Relative | Velocity (Continuous) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Target interpretation** | Fixed coordinate from home | Signed offset from current | No target — just speed |
| **(ii) Input parameters** | Position, Velocity, Accel, Decel | Distance, Velocity, Accel, Decel | Velocity, Accel, Decel |
| **(iii) Termination** | Axis reaches target position | Axis traverses the specified offset | Stop command, limit, or new move |
| **(iv) Typical application** | CNC tool positioning | Step-and-settle syringe pump | Conveyor belt |

*Examiner note: a clean comparison table with all four criteria is awarded full 7 marks.*

---

**(b)** A linear servo stage must traverse $D = 100$ mm in a total time of $T = 2$ s with a maximum velocity $V_{max} = 80$ mm/s using a symmetric trapezoidal profile. Calculate: (i) the acceleration magnitude, (ii) the acceleration time $t_a$, (iii) the constant-velocity time $t_v$, and (iv) verify the total distance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the total-time relation derived earlier:**
$$T = \frac{V_{max}}{a} + \frac{D}{V_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
$$2 = \frac{80}{a} + \frac{100}{80}$$
$$2 = \frac{80}{a} + 1.25$$
$$\frac{80}{a} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Solve for acceleration:**
$$\boxed{a = \frac{80}{0.75} \approx 106.67 \text{ mm/s}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration time:**
$$t_a = \frac{V_{max}}{a} = \frac{80}{106.67} \approx 0.75 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Constant-velocity time (from total-time constraint):**
$$t_v = T - 2 t_a = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of total distance:**
- Accel distance: $d_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 106.67 \cdot (0.75)^{2} = 30$ mm
- Cruise distance: $d_v = 80 \cdot 0.5 = 40$ mm
- Decel distance: $d_d = 30$ mm
- Total: $30 + 40 + 30 = 100$ mm $\checkmark$

*(7 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for each of the three boxed answers, 2 for verification step.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units in the final answer.** A bare number without `mm/s²` or `mm` loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing relative and absolute move semantics.** If the problem says *"the axis is at 50 mm and is commanded to move to 70 mm"*, that is an **absolute** move of 20 mm — students frequently misclassify this as relative. Read carefully!
> 3. **Skipping the verification step** in numerical problems. The KTU 2024 scheme awards a dedicated mark for verifying the answer against the original constraint.
> 4. **Omitting the velocity-time sketch** in a 7-mark question on profiles. The sketch is worth at least 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Using `|` (pipe) inside LaTeX in a markdown table** — it breaks table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 6. **Not labelling the three phases** ($t_a$, $t_v$, $t_d$) on every graph — examiners expect this.
> 7. **Mixing up trapezoidal and S-curve profiles** — they are NOT interchangeable. S-curve has 7 phases; trapezoidal has 3.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these 12 points before the exam:**

- **Move Types = 6 canonical forms** → Absolute, Relative, Velocity, Home, Jog, Blend.
- **Absolute Move** → target is referenced to **home (zero)**, not the current position.
- **Relative Move** → target is a **signed offset** from the current position; successive calls stack.
- **Velocity (Continuous) Move** → no target position; axis runs until **stopped** by another command.
- **Home Move** → returns axis to a known reference and **resets the position counter to zero**.
- **Jog Move** → operator-driven, low-velocity, used in **manual HMI / commissioning**.
- **Blend Move** → successive moves are chained with **no settling time** at junctions.
- **Trapezoidal Profile** → 3 phases: **accelerate → cruise → decelerate**; $v$–$t$ graph is a trapezoid.
- **S-Curve Profile** → 7 phases with **jerk-controlled** transitions; reduces vibration.
- **Key formula:** $D = V_{max}^{2}/a + V_{max} \, t_v$ (symmetric trapezoidal).
- **Key formula:** $T = V_{max}/a + D/V_{max}$ (symmetric trapezoidal total time).
- **KTU 2024 expected question weightage:** ~10–12 marks of the 70-mark ESE paper is allocated to motion-control move types and profiles combined.

> **Final Note:** When a numerical problem gives *only* distance and time (no $V_{max}$ given), use the **triangular** profile relation $T = 2\sqrt{D/a}$. When *both* $D$ and $V_{max}$ are given, the trapezoidal relation $T = V_{max}/a + D/V_{max}$ applies. Recognizing this distinction is the single most common KTU exam discriminator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motor Amplifiers and Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT833 - INTRODUCTION TO VIRTUAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motor Amplifiers and Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Motor Amplifiers and Drives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Motor Amplifier**: An electronic power amplifier circuit designed to deliver the high current/voltage required to drive an electric motor, taking a low-power control signal (typically from a DAQ card or microcontroller in a VI system) and converting it into a high-power signal capable of energizing motor windings.

**Motor Drive**: A complete electronic system (amplifier + control logic + feedback circuitry) that regulates motor parameters such as **speed**, **torque**, **direction**, and **angular position**, in response to commands issued by the virtual instrument's software (LabVIEW, MATLAB, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard)**: A *Motor Drive* in a Virtual Instrumentation context is the integrated electromechanical subsystem that bridges the low-level analog/digital outputs of a data acquisition (DAQ) device to the high-power actuator terminals of an electric motor, enabling software-defined motion control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small child (the **DAQ card / software controller**) giving instructions to a massive industrial machine (the **electric motor**). The child can only whisper — the machine is deaf to whispers. A **Motor Amplifier** acts like a *megaphone and amplifier combined* — it takes the child's whisper and converts it into a thunderous voice the machine can obey. The **Motor Drive** is the entire communication system: it includes the megaphone (amplifier), the translation of "go forward" into the correct muscle movements (control logic), and the speedometer (feedback) to ensure the machine is moving exactly as commanded.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Virtual Instrumentation

In VI, the **PC + DAQ + software** is the "brain," and motors are the "muscles." Without proper amplifiers and drives, no software command can produce physical motion. This module is therefore the *actuation* counterpart to the *sensing* modules studied earlier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Physical Constants in this Module**:
> - **PWM Frequency (f_PWM)**: typically **1 kHz – 25 kHz** for most DC motor drives
> - **Duty Cycle (D)**: 0% (motor off) to 100% (full speed), unitless
> - **Supply Voltage (V_S)**: commonly **5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V** in VI lab setups
> - **Back-EMF Constant (K_e)** and **Torque Constant (K_t)**: SI unit **V·s/rad** and **N·m/A** respectively; numerically equal in SI units: **K_e = K_t**
> - **Armature Resistance (R_a)**: typically **1 Ω – 10 Ω** for small DC motors

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Motor Drives Used in VI

Motor drives are broadly classified by the type of motor they control:

### (a) DC Motor Drives
- **Brushed DC Motor Drive**: Simplest form, uses commutator and brushes. Speed controlled by varying **armature voltage**.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Drive**: Electronic commutation via Hall sensors or back-EMF sensing. Three-phase inverter output.
- **Servo DC Drive**: Closed-loop with encoder feedback for precise position/velocity control.

### (b) Stepper Motor Drives
- Open-loop position control using discrete pulse trains.
- Sub-types: **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, and **Hybrid (HB)** stepper drives.
- Driven in **wave-drive**, **full-step**, or **microstepping** modes.

### (c) AC Motor Drives (briefly)
- **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)**: Controls induction/synchronous motors by varying voltage AND frequency in a V/f ratio.
- Less common in undergraduate VI labs but important industrially.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus**: The syllabus emphasizes **DC motor drives**, **stepper motor drives**, and the **power amplifier circuits** (Class A/B/D, H-bridge, PWM) that drive them. AC drives are mentioned for completeness.

## 2.2 Classification of Power Amplifiers

| Amplifier Class | Conduction Angle | Efficiency | Use Case in Motor Drive |
|----------------|------------------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Class A** | 360° | ~25% (max 50%) | Small signal, low-power DC motor pre-amp |
| **Class B** | 180° | ~78.5% (max) | Push-pull audio, low-power motor |
| **Class AB** | 180°–360° | 50–70% | General purpose, linear motor amps |
| **Class C** | <180° | >78.5% | RF (not motor) |
| **Class D** | Switching (0° or 360°) | **>90%** | **PWM motor drives (most common in VI)** |

**Why Class D dominates motor drives**:
- The output transistors act only as switches (fully ON or fully OFF).
- Power dissipation = **V × I** is near zero in both states.
- Therefore, efficiency is theoretically **100%**, practically **>90%**.
- PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) is the modulation technique used in Class D.

## 2.3 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) — The Heart of Modern Motor Drives

**PWM** is a digital encoding of an analog signal level by varying the width of rectangular pulses. For motor control:

- **Frequency (f_PWM)**: held constant (e.g., 20 kHz).
- **Duty Cycle (D)**: varied from 0% to 100% to control average voltage applied to the motor.
- **Average Voltage**:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_S$$

where $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = T_{on} \times f_{PWM}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: A digital DAQ output (0 or 5 V) at high frequency, when low-pass filtered by the motor's own inductance, delivers an **average DC voltage** proportional to the duty cycle. The motor "sees" an effective DC voltage between 0 V and V_S without any linear power loss.

## 2.4 The H-Bridge — Bidirectional Motor Control

A **full H-bridge** consists of 4 switching elements (transistors/MOSFETs/IGBTs) arranged in an "H" topology around the motor:

```
       V_S
        +
        |
   S1 ---+--- S3
        |M|
   S2 ---+--- S4
        |
        GND
```

- **S1 & S2 ON, S3 & S4 OFF** → Current flows left-to-right → Motor turns **forward**.
- **S3 & S4 ON, S1 & S2 OFF** → Current flows right-to-left → Motor turns **reverse**.
- **S1 & S3 ON (or S2 & S4 ON)** → **Shoot-through** (short circuit across V_S) → **DESTRUCTIVE** — must be prevented by *dead-time* insertion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Forgetting the *dead-time* (typically 100 ns – 1 µs) between switching diagonal pairs. Both transistors in one leg must NEVER be ON simultaneously, or the bridge explodes.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_S$ | Average DC voltage from PWM | Volts (V); $D$ is dimensionless [0,1] |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = T_{on} \cdot f_{PWM}$ | Duty cycle definition | Unitless |
| 3 | $V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$ | DC motor armature equation | V, A, Ω, rad/s |
| 4 | $T = K_t I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 5 | $K_e = K_t$ (in SI) | Back-EMF equals torque constant | V·s/rad = N·m/A |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | No-load to loaded speed relation | rad/s |
| 7 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ | No-load angular speed | rad/s |
| 8 | $\omega_{sync} = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of AC motor | RPM; P = # poles |
| 9 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees; e.g., 1.8° for 200-step motor |
| 10 | $V/f = \text{constant}$ | V/f ratio for AC VFD control | V/Hz |
| 11 | $\eta_{Class\,D} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{switching}}$ | Class D efficiency | > 90% in practice |
| 12 | $P_{switching} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_S I_a (t_r + t_f) f_{PWM}$ | Switching loss per transistor | W |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Robotics**: Servo drives control 6-axis robot arms via PWM.
- **CNC Machines**: Stepper drives position tools with microstepping.
- **3D Printers**: BLDC drives extrude plastic at controlled flow rates.
- **Electric Vehicles**: Three-phase VFDs drive traction motors.
- **LabVIEW Applications**: Motor speed/torque test stands, vibration analyzers, automated calibration rigs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Motor Steady-State Speed vs. Voltage

We begin with the **armature circuit** of a separately excited DC motor. Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature loop:

$$V_S = I_a R_a + V_{back-emf}$$

The **back-EMF** (counter-electromotive force) is generated by the rotating armature in the magnetic field. By Faraday's law, it is proportional to angular velocity:

$$V_{back-emf} = K_e \omega$$

Substituting into the KVL equation:

$$V_S = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$$

The **electromagnetic torque** developed by the motor is proportional to armature current (force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field):

$$T_{em} = K_t I_a$$

In **SI units**, the back-EMF constant and torque constant are numerically equal: $K_e = K_t$. Using this:

$$I_a = \dfrac{T_{em}}{K_t} = \dfrac{T_{em}}{K_e}$$

Substitute $I_a$ into the KVL equation to relate voltage directly to speed and torque:

$$V_S = \left(\dfrac{T_{em}}{K_e}\right) R_a + K_e \omega$$

Solving for the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$K_e \omega = V_S - \dfrac{T_{em} R_a}{K_e}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \dfrac{V_S}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_e^{\,2}} \, T_{em}}$$

This is the **fundamental torque-speed equation** of a DC motor — a straight line with negative slope.

**Physical Interpretation**:
- **No-load (T = 0)**: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_S}{K_e}$ — maximum speed.
- **Stall (ω = 0)**: $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_S K_e}{R_a}$ — maximum torque.
- **Slope**: $\dfrac{R_a}{K_e^{\,2}}$ — determines how "soft" the speed-torque characteristic is.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PWM Average Voltage to Motor Speed Mapping

We now chain PWM control to the motor speed. The DAQ outputs a PWM waveform of amplitude $V_S$ (e.g., 5 V or 24 V) and duty cycle $D$.

**Step 1 — Define Duty Cycle**: $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Voltage**: The average value of a rectangular pulse train over one period is:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{period}} \int_{0}^{T_{period}} v(t) \, dt = \dfrac{V_S \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off}}{T_{period}}$$

$$V_{avg} = V_S \cdot \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = D \cdot V_S$$

**Step 3 — Assume Motor Inductance Filters PWM**: The motor winding has inductance $L_a$, forming an RL low-pass filter with time constant $\tau = L_a / R_a$. For $f_{PWM} \gg 1/\tau$, the current ripple is small and the motor "sees" an effective DC voltage of $V_{avg}$.

**Step 4 — Apply Speed Equation from §3.1** with $V_S \rightarrow V_{avg}$:

$$\omega = \dfrac{V_{avg}}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_e^{\,2}} T_{em}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{D \cdot V_S}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_e^{\,2}} T_{em}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Required Duty Cycle** to achieve a target speed $\omega^*$ at load torque $T_L$:

$$D = \dfrac{K_e \omega^* + \dfrac{R_a T_L}{K_e}}{V_S} = \dfrac{K_e \omega^* K_e + R_a T_L}{K_e V_S}$$

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{K_e^{\,2} \omega^* + R_a T_L}{K_e V_S}}$$

This is the **controller equation** that LabVIEW or any VI software must implement in real time.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stepper Motor Step Resolution

A stepper motor has a fixed number of magnetic pole pairs determining the step resolution.

**Step 1 — Total Steps per Revolution**: For a hybrid stepper with $N_{teeth}$ rotor teeth and $N_{p}$ stator pole pairs, the steps per revolution are:

$$N_{steps/rev} = N_{p} \cdot N_{teeth}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Step Angle**:

$$\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}}$$

**Step 3 — Microstepping**: Modern drives subdivide each full step into $m$ microsteps. The microstep angle becomes:

$$\theta_{\mu} = \dfrac{\theta_{step}}{m} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_{steps/rev}}$$

**Step 4 — Angular Position after N pulses**:

$$\theta_{total} = N \cdot \theta_{\mu} = \dfrac{360° \cdot N}{m \cdot N_{steps/rev}}$$

**Step 5 — Angular Velocity for Pulse Frequency $f_p$**:

$$\omega = f_p \cdot \theta_{\mu} \quad \text{[rad/s, after conversion]}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f_p}{m \cdot N_{steps/rev}} \quad \text{[rad/s]}}$$

This relation is critical when LabVIEW generates step pulses via a DAQ counter/timer to drive a stepper motor at a precise velocity.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Dissipation in Linear vs. Class D Drive

**Linear (Class A) Drive**: The amplifier behaves as a variable resistor dropping $V_{drop} = V_S - V_{motor}$. Power dissipated as heat:

$$P_{linear} = (V_S - V_{motor}) \cdot I_a$$

Maximum dissipation at $V_{motor} = V_S/2$ (i.e., half speed):

$$P_{linear,\,max} = \dfrac{V_S^2}{4 R_a}$$

**Class D Switching Drive**: Power dissipated only during switching transitions:

$$P_{switching} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_S I_a (t_r + t_f) f_{PWM}$$

**Efficiency Comparison** at half speed ($V_{motor} = V_S/2$):
- Linear: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_S/2 \cdot I_a}{V_S \cdot I_a} = 50\%$
- Class D: $\eta > 90\%$ (for typical $t_r + t_f \approx 50$ ns, $f_{PWM} = 20$ kHz)

**Result**: Class D is **~40 percentage points more efficient** at the same operating point — the reason all modern motor drives use switching topologies.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Drive Selection for Common VI Lab Motors

| Motor Type | Drive Topology | Control Signal from DAQ | Feedback Sensor | Typical LabVIEW Application |
|------------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Small DC (≤ 12 V) | L293D H-bridge (IC) | 2 digital lines (PWM + DIR) | None / Tachogenerator | Fan-speed control, pump |
| Medium DC (12–48 V) | MOSFET H-bridge (discrete) | PWM + direction from DAQ analog out | Optical encoder | Conveyor, valve actuator |
| Stepper (bipolar) | L298N + current chopper | Step + Dir pulses from counter/timer | None (open-loop) | Linear stage, syringe pump |
| BLDC | 3-phase inverter + Hall sensing | 3 PWM channels (6-step commutation) | Hall sensors / encoder | Drone, spindle |
| Servo (hobby) | Internal to motor | 50 Hz PWM, 1–2 ms pulse width | Internal pot | Robotic arm joint |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Motor Drive Subsystem in a Virtual Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["LabVIEW PC Software"] --> DAQ["DAQ Card / USB-6009"]
    DAQ -->|"Analog 0-5V or Digital PWM"| CTRL["Motor Drive Controller (L293D / L298N / MOSFET H-Bridge)"]
    CTRL -->|"High Current PWM"| MOT["DC / Stepper / Servo Motor"]
    MOT -->|"Mechanical Output"| LOAD["Mechanical Load (Pump, Fan, Stage)"]
    SENS["Encoder / Tachogenerator / Hall Sensor"] -->|"Feedback Voltage or Pulses"| DAQ
    DAQ -->|"Closed-loop correction signal"| PC
    PWR["DC Power Supply (5V / 12V / 24V)"] --> CTRL
    PWR --> MOT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram left-to-right**: PC issues command → DAQ outputs low-power signal → Drive amplifies → Motor moves load → Sensor feeds back to DAQ → DAQ to PC → PC adjusts command. This is the **closed-loop** paradigm of VI.

## 4.2 H-Bridge Switching States (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stop
    Stop --> Forward: S1, S2 ON (S3, S4 OFF)
    Stop --> Reverse: S3, S4 ON (S1, S2 OFF)
    Stop --> Brake: S2, S4 ON (low-side short)
    Forward --> Stop: All OFF
    Reverse --> Stop: All OFF
    Forward --> Brake: S2, S4 ON
    Reverse --> Brake: S2, S4 ON
    Brake --> Stop: All OFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Four canonical states: **Forward**, **Reverse**, **Coast (Stop with all OFF)**, and **Brake (low-side short regenerates current)**. The "Shoot-through" state (S1+S2 ON or S3+S4 ON simultaneously) is intentionally absent from this diagram — it is a fault state and must be prevented by the controller's dead-time logic.

## 4.3 PWM Generation in LabVIEW (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User-Set Speed Slider (0-100%)"] --> B["Scale to Duty Cycle D (0.0 - 1.0)"]
    B --> C["DAQ Counter/Timer Configuration"]
    C --> D["Generate PWM: f = 20 kHz, D = scaled value"]
    D --> E["Output on DAQ Counter Pin (e.g., CTR0)"]
    E --> F["PWM Signal to Motor Drive Input"]
    F --> G["Motor Speed Proportional to D"]
    H["Encoder Pulse Train"] --> I["DAQ Counter Input (e.g., CTR1)"]
    I --> J["Frequency Measurement VI in LabVIEW"]
    J --> K["Display RPM / Plot Waveform"]
```

## 4.4 Class D Amplifier Output Stage (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["PWM Input from Controller"] --> G1["Gate Driver 1 (Bootstrap)"]
    IN --> G2["Gate Driver 2 (Bootstrap)"]
    G1 --> Q1["High-Side MOSFET Q1"]
    G2 --> Q2["Low-Side MOSFET Q2"]
    Q1 --> MID["Switching Node (Motor Terminal)"]
    Q2 --> GND["Ground"]
    MID --> L["Motor Inductance (Low-Pass Filter)"]
    L --> MTR["DC Motor"]
    PWR["V_S Supply"] --> Q1
```

**Explanation**: The two MOSFETs operate in anti-phase — when Q1 is ON, Q2 is OFF, applying +V_S to the motor; when Q2 is ON, Q1 is OFF, applying 0 V. The motor inductance smooths the resulting square wave into a near-DC current. Dead-time prevents both from being ON simultaneously.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a motor drive. With a neat block diagram, explain the role of a power amplifier in a virtual instrumentation-based motor control system.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

A **motor drive** is an electronic system that controls the speed, torque, and direction of an electric motor in response to low-level signals from a controller.

**Block Diagram**:

```
[PC / LabVIEW] → [DAQ Card] → [Power Amplifier] → [Motor] → [Load]
                                              ↑
                                       [Power Supply]
```

**Role of Power Amplifier**: The DAQ card outputs a low-current signal (typically 0–5 V, ≤ 20 mA), which is insufficient to drive a motor. The power amplifier boosts this signal to the voltage/current levels required by the motor windings (e.g., 12 V, 1 A) while preserving the control information (e.g., PWM duty cycle for speed, polarity for direction). Class D (switching) amplifiers are preferred in modern VI drives for their >90% efficiency. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for diagram, 1 for amplifier role.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is PWM? How is it used to control the speed of a DC motor in a virtual instrumentation system?**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

**PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a technique in which a digital signal (ON/OFF pulses) of fixed frequency but variable pulse width encodes an analog level. The **duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T_{period}$ represents the average signal value.

**Use in DC Motor Speed Control**:
1. LabVIEW computes the required speed → calculates the corresponding duty cycle $D$.
2. The DAQ card's counter/timer outputs a PWM waveform at fixed frequency (e.g., 20 kHz) with duty cycle $D$.
3. The average voltage seen by the motor is $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_S$.
4. The motor's armature equation gives speed: $\omega = \dfrac{D \cdot V_S - I_a R_a}{K_e}$, so speed is linearly proportional to $D$ for a given load.
5. The high PWM frequency is filtered by the motor's own inductance, producing smooth rotation without audible noise (when f > 20 kHz). *(3 marks: 1 for PWM definition, 2 for application steps.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Most students lose 2–3 marks by skipping the units** in the final answer. ALWAYS write SI units (rad/s, N·m, V, A).
> - **Do not confuse $K_e$ and $K_t$**: In SI units they are numerically equal, but state this explicitly.
> - **In H-bridge diagrams**, label all four switches and indicate current direction with an arrow. A diagram without an arrow earns partial credit only.
> - **PWM formulas**: Write the full derivation of $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_S$ — do not state it without proof.
> - **Stepper motor questions**: Always mention whether the motor is unipolar or bipolar, and whether the drive is in wave, full-step, or microstepping mode.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a closed-loop DC motor speed control system implemented using LabVIEW. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the torque-speed characteristic equation of a separately excited DC motor. From this equation, explain how the no-load speed and stall torque depend on the armature voltage and field flux. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Block Diagram** (7 marks):

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────┐
│  LabVIEW VI │───▶│ DAQ Card │───▶│ Power Amplifier│───▶│ DC Motor │
│ (PID Algo.) │    │ (AI/AO)  │    │   (H-Bridge)  │    │          │
└──────▲──────┘    └────┬─────┘    └──────────────┘    └────┬─────┘
       │                │                                    │
       │   Set Point    │ Feedback (Speed)                   │ Output
       │   Error        ▼                                    ▼
       │          ┌──────────┐                         ┌──────────┐
       └──────────│  PID Calc│◀──────┐                │ Mechanical│
                  └──────────┘       │                │   Load   │
                                     │                └──────────┘
                                     │
                              ┌──────┴───────┐
                              │  Tachogenerator │
                              │   / Encoder    │
                              └────────────────┘
```

**Block Functions** (write 1–2 lines each, 1 mark per block × ~6 blocks = 6 marks + 1 for overall coherence):

1. **LabVIEW VI**: Implements the PID control algorithm. Reads setpoint (desired RPM) and actual RPM; computes error; outputs a control voltage (0–5 V).
2. **DAQ Card (USB-6009/6002)**: Converts the PID output (analog 0–5 V) to a PWM signal via its counter/timer. Acquires the encoder/tachogenerator feedback.
3. **Power Amplifier (H-Bridge, e.g., L293D)**: Boosts the low-power PWM signal to high-current levels (e.g., 600 mA continuous, 1.2 A peak) to drive the motor. Direction control via a separate logic pin.
4. **DC Motor**: Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation. Speed is the controlled variable.
5. **Mechanical Load**: The application — pump, fan, conveyor, etc.
